The design is clean, official and service-oriented rather than overtly political. The messaging technique positions the federal Labor Department as an accessible helper/protector of workers' rights, providing a concrete call-to-action (phone number and website). Target audience: American workers who may need help with workplace issues.

notable_text: NATIONAL TRADESMEN DAY; Making America SKILLED AGAIN!; The Washington Times — Labor Department awards grants to expand Trump goal of one million apprenticeships
description: A vertical designed graphic combining a worker photo with slogan typography and a news headline. The upper portion is a color photograph of a tradesman at work in an industrial shop: a man wearing a patterned welding cap/bandana, safety glasses and tan work jacket with thick yellow leather gloves, using a tool/clamp on a large metal pipe or fixture. A dark navy gradient overlays the lower part. White typography presents a small blue banner reading 'NATIONAL TRADESMEN DAY', then italic 'Making America' above bold all-caps 'SKILLED AGAIN!'. Below is a white rounded card with 'The Washington Times' masthead and the bold black headline: 'Labor Department awards grants to expand Trump goal of one million apprenticeships'. A small circular DOL seal sits at the bottom. The composition riffs on the 'Make America Great Again' slogan, rebranding it as 'Making America Skilled Again,' and uses borrowed news credibility to promote the administration's one-million-apprenticeships goal. Target audience: skilled tradespeople, prospective apprentices, and supporters.

notable_text: AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST
description: A vertical patriotic slogan poster. The background is a clean photograph of a single American flag flying on a tall flagpole against a soft blue sky, with bare leafless winter treetops along the bottom. Centered at top is the white line-art seal of the U.S. Department of Labor ('Department of Labor / United States of America'). Below it, large white serif typography reads 'AMERICAN WORKERS' on one line and a much larger italic 'FIRST' on the second line, dominating the upper half. The aesthetic is restrained, dignified and nationalistic—flag imagery plus government seal plus an 'America First'-style worker slogan. The messaging technique is straightforward nationalist branding equating the administration's labor agenda with patriotism and prioritizing American (vs. foreign) workers. The accompanying tweet describes a proposed rule to reduce incentives to replace American workers with 'low-wage foreign visa labor,' linking the slogan to immigration/visa restriction. Target audience: American workers and nationalist-leaning voters.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Senior Special Agent Claude Arthur Medearis; End of Watch; Wednesday, April 19, 1995
description: A horizontal, professionally designed ICE memorial 'Wall of Honor' tribute card. The background is a soft-focus dark teal/blue muted texture. At upper left, elegant white serif typography reads 'Wall of Honor' (with 'of' in italic script), accompanied by the gold-and-blue ICE special agent badge/shield emblem (bearing 'ICE' and a small DHS-style seal wrapped in a blue mourning ribbon). Centered is a framed portrait photograph of a mustached man in a dark suit, white shirt and dark tie against a blue studio backdrop. To the left of the portrait, white text reads 'Senior Special Agent / Claude Arthur Medearis', then a gold divider line, 'End of Watch' (with 'of' italicized), another divider, and the date 'Wednesday, April 19, 1995'. The right third of the image shows a draped American flag (stars and stripes) above a bouquet of deep red roses, evoking solemn remembrance. The messaging technique is institutional commemoration/valorization, honoring an agent killed in the 1995 Oklahoma City Murrah Federal Building bombing and reinforcing agency identity, sacrifice and continuity ('continued resolve to ensure public safety and national security'). Target audience: ICE personnel, law-enforcement supporters and the general public, building solemn legitimacy and esprit de corps.

notable_text: Delivering for WORKING FAMILIES!; THE SACRAMENTO BEE; The bill extends tax benefits to millions of working families ... 4 million families can receive higher credits
description: A vertical designed graphic blending a campaign-style photograph with a headline/quote block. The top half is a color photo of Donald Trump (seen from behind, dark navy suit, blond hair) facing a jubilant crowd of supporters behind a rope line on a lawn (the White House South Lawn / a rally). The crowd—children and adults—wear red 'TRUMP' caps and American-flag shirts, many with painted faces and raised phones photographing him; several cheer with raised fists and big smiles. A dark navy gradient fades the lower portion. Over it, large white serif typography reads italic 'Delivering for' above bold all-caps 'WORKING FAMILIES!'. Below sits a white text card headed 'THE SACRAMENTO BEE' containing several paragraphs of body text: 'The bill extends tax benefits to millions of working families and provides new incentives for employers to get involved in child and dependent care. The First Five Years Fund, a Washington-based advocacy group, estimates that a family of four with two children and an income of $43,000 to $150,000 could get a credit of up to $2,100 for child care expenses, or $900 more than under current law. It estimates that 4 million families can receive higher credits, including two-earner households with incomes up to $206,000 and single-income households with incomes up to $103,000.' A small circular agency seal sits at the bottom. The technique pairs an emotionally charged supporter image with borrowed news credibility to tout the Working Family Tax Cuts. Target audience: families and voters.

notable_text: APPRENTICESHIP WORKS; Explore opportunities at apprenticeship.gov
description: A vertical designed recruitment/promotional poster. The image is a color portrait photograph of a young woman, smiling slightly at the camera, wearing a bright blue hard hat, a neon yellow-green high-visibility safety vest over a navy jacket, with a braided ponytail; she stands outdoors at what appears to be a construction or solar-array site (blurred steel framework in the background). A large semi-transparent white line-art seal of the U.S. Department of Labor ('Department of Labor / United States of America') is watermarked centrally over the lower portion. A dark blue gradient frames the bottom, where white italic serif typography reads 'APPRENTICESHIP WORKS' in large letters, with a smaller line beneath: 'Explore opportunities at apprenticeship.gov' (note: 'opportunities' is misspelled 'opportunities/opportunities'). The clean, aspirational design markets skilled-trade apprenticeships—notably featuring a young woman to broaden appeal—and provides a website call-to-action. Target audience: young people (including women) considering trades/apprenticeship pathways.

notable_text: MESSAGE TO ILLEGAL ALIENS: A WARNING TO SELF-DEPORT; Benefits; Consequences; Self-deportation is safe; Keep money earned in the U.S.; Immediate deportation; A fine of $998 per day; Barred from returning
description: A vertical, professionally designed DHS infographic with a red-white-and-blue color scheme split into two sections. The header is a blue band with white text: 'MESSAGE TO ILLEGAL ALIENS:' (smaller) above large bold 'A WARNING TO SELF-DEPORT'. The upper (blue) section features a photograph of a hand holding a smartphone displaying the 'CBP Home' app screen (labeled 'Departing Traveler' with a U.S. map and options 'Verify Departure', 'View My Departure', 'Submit Intent To Depart'). Beside it, a white-text list titled 'Benefits:' reads: 'Self-deportation is safe. Leave on your own terms by picking your departure flight. Keep money earned in the U.S. If you self-deport as a non-criminal illegal alien. Future opportunity for legal immigration. If you self-deport using CBP Home instead of being deported by ICE. Possible Flights Home. You may be eligible to a subsidized flight if you cannot afford to leave.' The lower (red) section is titled 'Consequences:' and lists threats: 'Immediate deportation. You will be apprehended by DHS with no opportunity to get your affairs in order beforehand. Fines and penalties. A fine of $998 per day if you received a final order of removal and stayed. A fine of $1,000 - $5,000 if you fail to self-deport after claiming that you will. Possible imprisonment. If you fail to self-deport, you may be subject to jail time. Barred from returning. Prohibited from reentering the U.S. through the legal immigration system.' The DHS seal appears at lower right. The propaganda technique is a carrot-and-stick framing (incentives vs. punishments) coercing voluntary departure via the CBP Home app. Target audience: undocumented immigrants, pressuring self-deportation.

notable_text: The Most Pro-Worker Legislation IN HISTORY!; FOX NEWS — Service industry workers reaping the benefits of Trump's 'no tax on tips'; KLTV — ‘Big Beautiful Bill’ will no longer tax overtime, tips; THE COLLEGE FIX — $500 billion now available for trade schools, thanks to Trump budget
description: A vertical designed graphic combining a photo with slogan typography and three clipped news headlines. The top portion is a color photograph of Donald Trump (navy suit, red tie) walking and gesturing among industrial steel-mill workers wearing orange high-visibility coveralls and hard hats, with a large American flag and rolled steel coils in the background. A dark navy gradient fades the lower portion, where white serif typography reads italic 'The Most Pro-Worker Legislation' above bold all-caps 'IN HISTORY!'. Below are three torn-paper-style headline clippings: (1) 'FOX NEWS — Service industry workers reaping the benefits of Trump's 'no tax on tips''; (2) 'KLTV — ‘Big Beautiful Bill’ will no longer tax overtime, tips'; (3) 'THE COLLEGE FIX — $500 billion now available for trade schools, thanks to Trump budget'. The technique uses a heroic image of the President among blue-collar workers plus aggregated favorable press to brand the One Big Beautiful Bill as historically pro-worker (no tax on tips/overtime, trade-school funding). Target audience: blue-collar and service workers, trade-school students, and supporters.

notable_text: Labor News; US Department of Labor announces $145M in funds supporting performance-based Registered Apprenticeship expansion across key industry sectors; SECRETARY L. CHAVEZ; ZACK KENDALL
description: A high-contrast black-and-white photograph treated as a designed social-media news card. The photo shows Secretary of Labor Lori Chavez-DeRemer (a woman in business attire, blazer and necklace, wearing safety glasses and a white hard hat stenciled 'SECRETARY' / 'L. CHAVEZ' with a Department of Labor seal decal) leaning over and conversing with a younger male apprentice/worker on her right, who wears a white hard hat labeled 'ZACK KENDALL', a dark t-shirt with a DOL seal patch, and visible forearm tattoos; both are looking down at something (a phone/tablet) in an industrial shop setting with bokeh background lights. A faint, large embossed Department of Labor eagle-and-shield seal watermark overlays the lower-right corner. In the lower-left, a small blue label box reads 'Labor News' in white serif italic next to three vertical bars. Below in large white serif headline type: 'US Department of Labor announces $145M in funds supporting performance-based Registered Apprenticeship expansion across key industry sectors.' The styling mimics a news/press-release card with monochrome documentary photography to lend gravitas and authority. Messaging technique: presents a routine funding announcement as headline news, foregrounding a hands-on, blue-collar, mentorship image of leadership to project competence and a worker-first brand. Target audience: tradespeople, apprenticeship stakeholders, and general public consuming agency social media.

notable_text: Putting American WORKERS FIRST!; HR DIVE — DOL to provide $30M in training grants on AI, skilled trades; POLITICO PRO — Trump administration announces new workforce development plan; FOX BUSINESS — Labor secretary unveils unprecedented plan to slash 63 'outdated and burdensome' rules
description: A vertical designed graphic combining a candid photo with slogan typography and multiple clipped news headlines. The top half is a color photograph of three people smiling and conversing outside a barn/industrial building (red metal wall, security camera): a gray-haired woman in glasses and a navy top at left, a man in a gray 'Miller' t-shirt at center, and a woman with dark hair (apparently Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer) in a gray jacket at right. A dark navy gradient fades the lower portion, where white serif typography reads italic 'Putting American' above bold all-caps 'WORKERS FIRST!'. Below are three torn-paper-style headline clippings: (1) 'HR DIVE — DOL to provide $30M in training grants on AI, skilled trades'; (2) 'POLITICO PRO — Trump administration announces new workforce development plan'; (3) 'FOX BUSINESS — Labor secretary unveils unprecedented plan to slash 63 'outdated and burdensome' rules'. The technique aggregates favorable third-party press to project momentum and an 'America First' worker agenda combining job training and deregulation. Target audience: workers, employers, and supporters of the administration's labor policies.

notable_text: The Golden Age of the SKILLED TRADES!; Trade Schools Rising In Popularity (WBOC CBS); Trade school demand has increased since the pandemic (2 News Nevada); Colorado trade schools see increased enrollment as students prioritize cheaper pathways to jobs (Denver 7 ABC)
description: A designed propaganda/promo poster on a dark navy background. The top half is a photo of President Trump in a dark blue suit and red tie walking through an industrial steel mill (rolled steel coils visible at right, a large American flag hanging in the background) flanked by three workers in orange hi-vis coveralls and white hard hats; Trump gestures and appears to be talking with them. Overlaid in the center is a two-line headline in white serif type: a smaller italic 'The Golden Age of the' above large bold all-caps 'SKILLED TRADES!'. The bottom third is a collage of three torn-paper 'screenshot' news-headline clippings with real station logos: WBOC CBS ('Trade Schools Rising In Popularity'), 2 NEWS NEVADA ('Trade school demand has increased since the pandemic'), and DENVER 7 ABC ('Colorado trade schools see increased enrollment as students prioritize cheaper pathways to jobs'). Visual style: patriotic blue palette, dramatic serif typography, and a curated stack of mainstream-media headlines used as third-party validation. Messaging technique: borrows credibility from independent local-news outlets to credit the administration with a 'golden age' revival of blue-collar trades. Target audience: working-class voters, trade-school prospects, and pro-Trump base.

notable_text: PROGRESS: Jobs Report Shows Private Sector Gains, Wage Growth for American-Born Workers; Under President Trump, all job gains have gone to native-born Americans — reversing the Biden-era trend; 2.5 million native-born Americans gained employment, while 670,000 foreign-born workers lost employment
description: A designed all-text social-media card styled like a press-release or article excerpt. The top portion has a dark navy background with a large white serif headline reading 'PROGRESS: Jobs Report Shows Private Sector Gains, Wage Growth for American-Born Workers'. The bottom portion is a light gray/white panel with body text in a mixed bold/regular sans-serif: bold lead-in 'Under President Trump, all job gains have gone to native-born Americans — reversing the Biden-era trend.' followed by 'Over the past year, more than 2.5 million native-born Americans gained employment, while 670,000 foreign-born workers lost employment.' The words 'gained' and 'lost' are underlined for emphasis (contrast framing). Visual style: clean, minimalist, no imagery, two-tone navy/white, serif headline over sans-serif body to read as authoritative news. Messaging/propaganda technique: nativist economic framing that explicitly contrasts native-born job gains against foreign-born job losses and credits the administration while disparaging the prior administration ('Biden-era trend'); pairs with the tweet's 'PROMISES MADE, PROMISES KEPT.' Target audience: 'America First' base, native-born workers, immigration-restrictionist supporters.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Special Agent Juan Gilberto Reyes Orellana; End of Watch; Thursday, January 29, 1998
description: A horizontal ICE 'Wall of Honor' memorial tribute card, identical in template to ICE's other fallen-agent graphics. The background is a soft-focus dark teal/blue muted texture. At upper left, white serif typography reads 'Wall of Honor' (with 'of' in italic script) beside the gold-and-blue ICE special agent badge/shield emblem wrapped in a blue mourning ribbon. Centered is a framed older portrait photograph of a dark-haired, mustached man in a dark suit, white shirt and dark tie, standing in front of foliage/flowers. To the left of the portrait, white text reads 'Special Agent / Juan Gilberto Reyes Orellana', a gold divider, 'End of Watch' (italic 'of'), another divider, then the date 'Thursday, January 29, 1998'. The right third shows a draped American flag above a bouquet of deep red roses. The messaging is institutional commemoration honoring a Special Agent who died in 1998 after a vehicle collision during pursuit of suspects, reinforcing agency sacrifice and 'continued resolve to ensure public safety and national security.' Target audience: ICE personnel, law-enforcement supporters, and the public.

genre_refs: Uncle Sam 'I Want You' WWI recruitment poster (James Montgomery Flagg)
notable_text: DHS recruiting 'patriots' to join ICE, offering up to $50K signing bonus | AMERICA NEEDS YOU | VISIT JOIN.ICE.GOV
description: A vertical recruitment graphic that fuses a news-headline clip with the iconic Uncle Sam 'I Want You' WWI poster. The top portion has a torn-paper white panel containing the ABC affiliate 'KSAT 12' (San Antonio) logo, a red 'LOCAL NEWS' kicker, the bold navy headline 'DHS recruiting ‘patriots’ to join ICE, offering up to $50K signing bonus', and a subhead 'Multiple incentives are offered as part of the “Defend the Homeland” campaign.' Below a ragged torn edge, on a dark navy background, sits the classic painted illustration of Uncle Sam in his red-white-and-blue star-banded top hat and blue coat, sternly pointing his finger directly at the viewer (James Montgomery Flagg's image). Across the bottom in large serif caps is the slogan 'AMERICA NEEDS YOU' (with 'YOU' in red), and below it 'VISIT JOIN.ICE.GOV.' The piece deliberately invokes patriotic wartime nostalgia and the direct-address 'we want you' appeal to drive ICE hiring during the 'Defend the Homeland' surge, while laundering its message through a local-news headline for added credibility. Target audience: prospective ICE recruits and patriotic supporters.

notable_text: JOIN AMERICA'S WORKFORCE REVIVAL; JOBS! JOBS! JOBS!; APPRENTICESHIP.GOV
description: A widescreen designed promo poster built on a photograph of President Trump standing on a stage in an industrial machine-shop/factory setting, wearing a dark blue suit, white shirt, and a purple/blue tie, pointing emphatically off to his left with his right arm. Behind him are an American flag and a gold-fringed presidential flag, with metalworking machinery and equipment filling the shop floor, and a long blue banner reading 'JOBS! JOBS! JOBS!' on the back wall. Across the top in white serif capitals is the headline 'JOIN AMERICA'S WORKFORCE REVIVAL'. In the lower-right corner is a small embossed Department of Labor seal and the URL 'APPRENTICESHIP.GOV'. Visual style: cinematic, well-lit hero shot of the President as energetic leader, patriotic flags, bold serif typography. Messaging/propaganda technique: a recruitment/call-to-action linking national renewal ('workforce revival') to the President's leadership, directing viewers to the apprenticeship website. Target audience: prospective workers/apprentices and the administration's blue-collar base.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again | If you're here illegally, leave now. | Return the right way and respect our laws for a chance to live the American dream.
description: A vertical designed propaganda/slogan graphic built around a stylized, semi-transparent American flag rendered on what looks like wooden planks (the stars-and-stripes printed/painted over horizontal boards) as the dominant background, in muted red-white-blue tones with a dark blue gradient wash toward the bottom. Overlaid across the lower-center are the dark, shadowy silhouettes of a group of people (heads and shoulders of several figures, suggesting a crowd of migrants), darkened so faces are obscured and anonymous, lending an ominous, faceless 'mass' connotation. In the upper-left corner is a solid blue box containing the white serif headline 'Making America Safe Again' (an echo of the MAGA slogan, used here as a USCIS/DHS campaign tag). The bottom of the image carries bold white sans-serif messaging: a large headline 'If you're here illegally, leave now.' followed by smaller body text 'Return the right way and respect our laws for a chance to live the American dream.' The visual technique pairs patriotic flag iconography with shadowed, dehumanized silhouettes of unauthorized immigrants to deliver a self-deportation / voluntary-departure threat-and-incentive message. Target audience: undocumented immigrants (warning) and the agency's supporter base (reassurance of enforcement).

notable_text: If it crosses the U.S. border illegally, it's our job to STOP IT. | PEOPLE MONEY PRODUCTS INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY | ICE.gov
description: A square designed slogan poster with a gritty, distressed black grunge-texture background resembling a chalkboard or scorched concrete wall. Running diagonally up the right side in large white chalk-style stenciled lettering is a stacked list: 'PEOPLE / MONEY / PRODUCTS / INTELLECTUAL / PROPERTY' — the categories ICE claims to police at the border. At upper-left, white sans-serif text reads 'If it crosses the U.S. border illegally, it's our job to', and the sentence is completed by a bold black-on-orange highlighted box reading 'STOP IT.' A rough orange/yellow painted cross or X mark is brushed over the lower-left of the chalk list, reinforcing the 'stop/prohibited' motif. The bottom-left bears the DHS/ICE seal with 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' in white, and the bottom-right shows 'ICE.gov'. The aesthetic is deliberately tough and militaristic, using grunge texture and a stark color accent to project authority and deterrence. The accompanying tweet warns 'DO NOT enter the U.S. illegally.' Messaging technique: deterrence/mission-branding emphasizing ICE's broad enforcement scope. Target audience: would-be border crossers (deterrence) and the public/recruits.

notable_text: NEW YORK POST | METRO EXCLUSIVE | Inside ICE operations in New Jersey — as agents nab two child predators
description: A cropped screenshot of a New York Post web article styled as a shareable graphic. At the top is the recognizable red New York Post masthead bar with a hamburger menu icon, an envelope icon, the white slanted 'NEW YORK POST' logo, and a 'LOG IN' button at right; below it a white navigation row with section tabs 'US News', 'Metro' (active, red-underlined), 'Long Island', 'Politics', 'World Ne[ws]'. The body shows a red 'METRO' tag and red italic 'EXCLUSIVE' label, followed by the large bold black headline 'Inside ICE operations in New Jersey — as agents nab two child predators.' The byline 'By Reuven Fenton and Alex Oliveira' (names in red) and 'Published July 21, 2025, 7:22 p.m. ET' appear beneath, with a small red comment-count badge '61' in the lower right. This is a clean media-clip card rather than an original poster; ICE is amplifying favorable third-party press coverage. The messaging technique is borrowed-credibility / earned-media amplification, leaning on the 'child predators' framing to associate immigration enforcement with protecting children. The accompanying tweet adds a scoreboard-style taunt ('Illegal alien child predators in NJ: 0 / @ICEgov: 2'). Target audience: general public and supporters, signaling press validation of ICE's mission.

notable_text: Factory Construction Soars Under President Trump; Gain in employment, non-residential specialty trade construction, cumulative since Jan. 2025; 9,300 / 19,500 / 26,100 / 31,800 / 54,200
description: A designed vertical card combining a hero photo on top with a bar-chart infographic on the bottom. The photo shows President Trump in a dark blue suit and red striped tie standing in front of a large American flag inside an industrial plant, gesturing as he talks with three workers in orange hi-vis coveralls and white hard hats covered in union/safety stickers (one at far right wears a dark uniform with a patch). A small embossed Department of Labor seal sits in the top-right corner. The lower white panel holds a chart titled in bold blue 'Factory Construction Soars Under President Trump', subtitle 'Gain in employment, non-residential specialty trade construction, cumulative since Jan. 2025'. A series of five rising red bars shows quarterly figures: 2025 Q1 = 9,300; Q2 = 19,500; Q3 = 26,100; Q4 = 31,800; 2026 Q1 = 54,200, each labeled in bold. Footnote: 'Note: CEA analysis of BLS data.' Visual style: patriotic hero imagery plus clean ascending red bars to signal momentum. Messaging/propaganda technique: pairs a leadership photo with a steadily climbing chart to attribute a manufacturing/construction-jobs boom to the President ('reindustrializing', 'AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST'). Target audience: blue-collar workers and the administration's economic base.

notable_text: TRUMP'S NEW UNION TRANSPARENCY TOOL IS A GAME CHANGER FOR WORKERS; Washington Examiner
description: A designed social-media card combining a White House photo with a media-citation headline. The photo (top ~two-thirds) shows a woman with dark hair and black glasses in a white blazer speaking at a presidential lectern with a gooseneck microphone, identified contextually as Secretary of Labor Lori Chavez-DeRemer; President Trump stands behind her at right in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, with the Seal of the President of the United States on the lectern and an ornate Oval Office / White House interior (gold urn, bookshelves, decorative wall reliefs) in the background. The bottom third is a torn-paper white strip carrying a bold navy serif all-caps headline: 'TRUMP'S NEW UNION TRANSPARENCY TOOL IS A GAME CHANGER FOR WORKERS', with the 'Washington Examiner' masthead/logo beneath it. Visual style: official White House setting plus a torn-edge newspaper-quote motif to borrow third-party media credibility. Messaging/propaganda technique: uses a sympathetic outlet's headline to frame a new union financial-disclosure tool as a pro-worker win for the administration. Target audience: workers, union members, and the administration's base.

notable_text: Green cards and visas are a privilege, not a right.
description: A horizontal designed slogan graphic on a pale, light-blue background that carries a faint, watermark-style circular DHS/USCIS-type seal (the words 'DEPARTMENT' and 'OF' are partly legible around an eagle/Statue-of-Liberty motif). The dominant element is a large dark-navy serif headline at the upper-left/center reading 'Green cards and visas are a privilege, not a right.' — with the word 'privilege' set in italics and underlined by a hand-drawn red swoosh for emphasis. Below the text are two photo-realistic specimen immigration documents shown at an angle: at left a sample 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA PERMANENT RESIDENT' card (green card) bearing the placeholder name 'SPECIMEN / TEST V,' USCIS# 000-000-310, category IR1, country of birth Bahamas, date of birth 17 AUG 1958, with a generic male ID photo over a Statue of Liberty/flag background; at right a larger sample U.S. 'IMMIGRANT VISA' page issued by 'US CONSULATE GENERAL LONDON,' with placeholder holder name 'TRAVELER / HAPPYPERSON,' category IR1, and a machine-readable zone 'VIUSATRAVELER<<HAPPYPERSON<<<...' plus a generic female ID photo. The composition uses official document imagery and a clean governmental aesthetic to deliver an ideological message that lawful immigration status is a revocable privilege; the accompanying tweet ties it to USCIS 'enforcement' and the idea that status is 'reserved for those who make the U.S. better, not those who seek to destroy it from the inside.' Target audience: visa/green-card holders (implicit warning) and the public.

notable_text: Factory Workers' Wages Have Finally Recovered From Biden; Real Wages Fall Under Biden -$830; Real Wages Soar Under Trump +$2,400
description: A designed infographic bar-chart card laid over a faded black-and-white background photo of an industrial factory/warehouse interior. A white panel holds the chart, titled in bold blue text 'Factory Workers' Wages Have Finally Recovered From Biden', with subtitle 'Change in annualized real earnings'. The two-bar chart contrasts a short navy-blue bar labeled '-$830' under 'Real Wages Fall Under Biden' against a tall red bar labeled '+$2,400' (in large red text) under 'Real Wages Soar Under Trump'; the y-axis runs from -$1,500 to $3,000. A footnote reads: 'Note: CEA analysis of BLS data. Weekly wages for non-supervisory manufacturing workers adjusted with CPI-W. Feb. 2026 CPI-W approximated using Bloomberg expectations for CPI-U.' Visual style: clean partisan data-viz using the blue=Biden(bad)/red=Trump(good) color coding and 'fall' vs 'soar' framing. Messaging/propaganda technique: a contrast infographic crediting the administration with wage gains while blaming the predecessor for losses. Target audience: manufacturing/blue-collar workers and the administration's economic-messaging base.

notable_text: COMPANIES ARE COMING HOME; U.S. DEPARTMENT OF LABOR
description: A dramatic designed propaganda poster in a high-contrast black-gold-and-white scheme. The right side shows a moody, heavily shadowed photo of President Trump walking forward in a dark suit, white shirt, and tie, wearing a white 'USA' baseball cap and clenching his right fist; a large American flag is faintly visible behind him and two suited figures (security/aides) appear small in the lower-right background. The left side carries bold distressed/grunge slab type stacked as a slogan: 'COMPANIES' (white) over 'ARE' (white, flanked by gold stars) over 'COMING HOME' (large gold). At the bottom left is the embossed Department of Labor seal next to 'U.S. DEPARTMENT OF LABOR' and a row of gold stars. Visual style: cinematic, gritty, patriotic gold/black palette with weathered military-poster typography to convey strength and momentum. Messaging/propaganda technique: strongman-leader imagery (fist, forward stride) plus a triumphant reshoring slogan to credit the President with bringing manufacturing/companies back to the U.S. ('biggest reshoring wave in U.S. history', 'Promises Made. Promises Kept.'). Target audience: nationalist/economic base and pro-Trump supporters.

notable_text: INVESTING IN AMERICA
description: A simple designed slogan/title card. The image is a color photograph of a large American flag flying on a flagpole against a soft pink-and-lavender dusk sky, with snow-dusted mountains in the background; the photo has a gentle desaturated/pastel tone. Across the top in glowing white serif capitals is the single line of text 'INVESTING IN AMERICA'. There is no chart, logo, or other on-image element besides the headline. Visual style: minimal, patriotic and aspirational, relying on the flag-against-mountains imagery and an elegant serif headline. Messaging/propaganda technique: a feel-good patriotic brand graphic associating the administration's family/child savings policy (per the tweet about giving children a 'financial head start' and 'No American will be left behind') with national pride and prosperity. Target audience: general public and the administration's supporters.

notable_text: TRUMP'S ECONOMIC WINS CANCEL OUT BIDEN'S LOSSES IN LATEST JOB REPORT (Fox News); AMERICAN-BORN EMPLOYMENT HITS RECORD HIGH AS FOREIGN-BORN JOBS DECLINE (CBS News); TRUMP'S 'ONE BIG BEAUTIFUL BILL' COULD DRIVE A 15% EARNINGS BOOM IN 2026 AS US MANUFACTURING ROARS BACK (Yahoo Finance); APPLE SHIFTS MAC MINI ASSEMBLY FROM ASIA TO U.S. UNDER TRUMP-BACKED 'MAKE IN AMERICA' PLAN (The Economic Times)
description: A designed headline-collage graphic built to look like a stack of four torn-paper newspaper strips layered over a faint gray newsprint background. Each strip pairs a recognizable media logo with a bold italic black serif headline. From top to bottom: FOX NEWS CHANNEL — 'TRUMP'S ECONOMIC WINS CANCEL OUT BIDEN'S LOSSES IN LATEST JOB REPORT'; CBS NEWS — 'AMERICAN-BORN EMPLOYMENT HITS RECORD HIGH AS FOREIGN-BORN JOBS DECLINE'; YAHOO FINANCE — 'TRUMP'S ‘ONE BIG BEAUTIFUL BILL’ COULD DRIVE A 15% EARNINGS BOOM IN 2026 AS US MANUFACTURING ROARS BACK'; THE ECONOMIC TIMES (ET) — 'APPLE SHIFTS MAC MINI ASSEMBLY FROM ASIA TO U.S. UNDER TRUMP-BACKED ‘MAKE IN AMERICA’ PLAN'. Visual style: monochrome torn-edge clippings with authentic outlet logos for a curated press-roundup look. Messaging/propaganda technique: stacks multiple mainstream-media headlines as third-party validation that the economy is 'WINNING' under the administration; one headline also carries a nativist native-born-vs-foreign-born framing. Target audience: economic-messaging base and general public consuming agency social media.

notable_text: CASE SPOTLIGHT; ICE
description: A designed branded 'Case Spotlight' template graphic. The background is a dark blue, textured (canvas/concrete-like) surface lit dramatically by a black tactical flashlight that hangs from the top of the frame on a lanyard, casting a bright cone/beam of light downward toward the center. Centered over the beam is the title in large bold sans-serif caps: 'CASE' (white) above 'SPOTLIGHT' (golden-yellow). At the bottom is the DHS seal beside the white 'ICE' wordmark. Visual style: moody noir/crime-thriller aesthetic (literal spotlight pun, deep shadows, blue-and-gold ICE branding). This is a reusable promotional template ICE attaches to individual criminal-case announcements; here it accompanies a tweet about a U.S.-citizen suspect charged with child sexual exploitation investigated by HSI New England (note: not an immigration/illegal-alien case). Messaging/propaganda technique: brands routine HSI casework as dramatic 'spotlighted' law-enforcement wins to bolster the agency's public-safety image. Target audience: general public and ICE supporters.

notable_text: CASE SPOTLIGHT; HOMELAND SECURITY TASK FORCE; INTEGRITY · VIGILANCE · UNITY
description: A dark, cinematic vertical graphic styled like a movie or video-game promotional poster. The composition is split: the top two-thirds shows a moody dark-blue textured wall lit by a single ceiling-mounted searchlight/flashlight casting a bright triangular beam downward; the bottom third shows a silhouetted three-man tactical/SWAT team in helmets and body armor seen from behind, lit by harsh backlight in a blue-tinted scene. Overlaid in large bold sans-serif type are the words 'CASE' (white) and 'SPOTLIGHT' (golden yellow), the title treatment. In the lower-left corner is a detailed circular metallic emblem reading 'HOMELAND SECURITY TASK FORCE' around the rim with 'INTEGRITY · VIGILANCE · UNITY' at the bottom, featuring an eagle, shield with U.S. flag motif, and stars. The propaganda technique is heroic dramatization: it frames a federal racketeering/murder prosecution as an action-thriller 'case spotlight,' lending glamour and gravitas to law-enforcement operations. Target audience is the general public and ICE supporters; the accompanying tweet describes charges against the 'La Familia Nunca Muere' criminal organization. Intent is to publicize and valorize ICE/HSTF enforcement successes.

notable_text: Since Jan. 20, HSI has arrested more than 1,000 illegal workers and proposed over $1 million in fines against businesses that exploit and hire illegal workers.; MADISON SHEAHAN, ICE Deputy Director
description: A designed official quote-card graphic on a solid blue gradient background. At the top-center is a circular headshot of a smiling woman with long dark hair in a dark blazer over a white top, posed in front of an American flag. The center holds a large white serif pull-quote framed by light-blue quotation marks: 'Since Jan. 20, HSI has arrested more than 1,000 illegal workers and proposed over $1 million in fines against businesses that exploit and hire illegal workers.' Beneath it an attribution reads '— MADISON SHEAHAN, ICE Deputy Director'. The lower-right corner bears the DHS seal and the white 'ICE' wordmark. Visual style: clean, corporate, official agency template (blue brand color, serif quote, circular portrait, seal). Messaging/propaganda technique: an authority/leadership quote citing enforcement statistics to project record-breaking worksite enforcement ('FIRST 100 DAYS: Protecting American Business'). Target audience: general public, employers, and immigration-restriction supporters.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again | If you're here illegally, leave now. | Return the right way and respect our laws for a chance to live the American dream.
description: A vertical designed propaganda/slogan graphic (identical artwork to the USCIS 'Making America Safe Again' campaign poster) featuring a stylized semi-transparent American flag printed over horizontal wooden planks as the background, in muted red-white-blue tones fading to a dark blue gradient at the bottom. Across the lower-center are dark, anonymized silhouettes of a group of people (multiple heads and shoulders, evoking a faceless crowd of migrants). The upper-left corner holds a solid blue box with the white serif headline 'Making America Safe Again' (a riff on the MAGA slogan). At the bottom, bold white sans-serif text reads, as a large headline, 'If you're here illegally, leave now.' followed by smaller body copy 'Return the right way and respect our laws for a chance to live the American dream.' The messaging technique combines patriotic flag imagery with shadowed, dehumanized immigrant silhouettes to push a self-deportation / voluntary-departure message that pairs a threat (leave now) with a conditional incentive (return legally). The accompanying tweet credits Secretary Noem and ties it to POTUS's promise to 'make America safe again.' Target audience: undocumented immigrants (warning) plus the administration's political supporters.

genre_refs: Lady Justice / Statue of Justice iconography
notable_text: Not a career. A calling. | ICE.gov/LegalCareers
description: A vertical recruitment poster dominated by a warmly lit bronze statue of Lady Justice (blindfolded, holding aloft a raised sword in one hand and the balanced scales of justice in the other, draped in classical robes), photographed against a softly blurred courthouse-like interior with golden sunbeams streaming through windows. The top-left corner carries the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' wordmark in dark text, with 'ICE.gov/LegalCareers' beneath it. The lower-left features the two-line tagline in white serif type: 'Not a career.' (lighter weight) followed by the larger bold 'A calling.' The warm, reverent, almost sacred lighting and the Lady Justice symbol elevate the job posting into a moral vocation. This is a targeted recruitment ad for ICE's Office of the Principal Legal Advisor (OPLA), per the tweet, promoting hiring bonuses up to $50,000 and six-figure salaries for government attorneys. Messaging technique: prestige/idealism appeal framing legal work at ICE as a noble duty rather than mere employment. Target audience: attorneys and law graduates.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Special Agent Thomas M. Wischerth; End of Watch; Sunday, March 26, 2017
description: A designed memorial tribute card ('Wall of Honor' / 'End of Watch'). The background is a dark, somber photo of deep-red roses lying beside a furled American flag on a dark surface. Inset at the upper left is a framed older portrait photograph of the honoree, a mustached man in a dark suit, light shirt, and patterned red-and-white tie, holding a wooden award plaque. Across the lower portion is white serif text reading 'Wall of Honor' (large) beside an ICE/HSI special-agent badge ringed with a blue mourning band; to the right: 'Special Agent Thomas M. Wischerth', then a gold-ruled divider and the phrase 'End of Watch', with the date 'Sunday, March 26, 2017' below. Visual style: reverent, dark palette with roses and flag iconography, elegant serif typography and the agency badge. Messaging/propaganda technique: a fallen-officer memorial that honors agency sacrifice and reinforces ICE's mission/legitimacy ('pledges its continued resolve to ensure public safety and national security'). Target audience: agency personnel, supporters, and the broader public, building goodwill and institutional pride.

notable_text: MANUFACTURING BOOM
description: A designed slogan graphic built on a photograph of a large industrial oil refinery / petrochemical plant with numerous towers, smokestacks, pipes, and storage tanks under an overcast sky. The photo is desaturated to grayscale except for a large American flag draped on the central structure, which retains full red-white-and-blue color (a selective-color technique drawing the eye to the flag). Overlaid across the center in bold blue all-caps condensed type is the headline 'MANUFACTURING BOOM'. The composition evokes American industrial might and patriotism. The propaganda technique combines nationalist imagery (the lone colored flag over a grayscale factory) with an economic-triumph message. Per the tweet, it touts manufacturing labor-productivity growth attributed to President Trump's 'America First' tax cuts, investments, and deregulation. Target audience is blue-collar workers and the general public; intent is to credit the administration for industrial revival.

notable_text: Break our laws, lose your privilege.; PERMANENT RESIDENT; VISA; UNITED STATES OF AMERICA
description: A designed slogan graphic on a light blue background with a faint watermark of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal (text 'U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY' arced around the edges). At the top, large dark-navy text reads 'Break our laws,' in bold roman type with 'lose your privilege.' below in italic, forming the central message. The lower portion shows two pixelated/blurred specimen immigration documents: a sample 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA PERMANENT RESIDENT' green card (surname 'SPECIMEN', given name 'TEST V', USCIS# 000-000-310, Category IR1, with Statue of Liberty and flag artwork) and a sample U.S. immigrant 'VISA' issued by the 'US CONSULATE GENERAL LONDON' (surname 'TRAVELER', Immigrant Visa, IV Case Number LND2004I6000201, Registration Number 00000473). The faces on both documents are deliberately obscured/scrambled. The propaganda technique is a conditional threat framing immigration status as a revocable 'privilege' rather than a right, warning green-card and visa holders that lawbreaking results in loss of status. Target audience is immigrants/lawful permanent residents and visa holders; intent is deterrence and projecting an enforcement-first posture under DHS Secretary Noem. This is the same recurring 'Break our laws, lose your privilege' template used across multiple USCIS posts.

notable_text: Private Sector Job Creation in January Blew Past Expectations; Private Sector Jobs 172; Bloomberg Expectations 70; Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Bloomberg, CEA Calculations
description: A clean infographic / bar chart on a light textured (torn-paper) white background. At the top center is the circular U.S. Department of Labor seal ('DEPARTMENT OF LABOR · UNITED STATES OF AMERICA'). Below it a bold black serif headline reads 'Private Sector Job Creation in January Blew Past Expectations'. The chart shows four vertical bars on a y-axis scaled 0 to 200: 'Private Sector Jobs' = 172 (tall blue bar), 'Bloomberg Expectations' = 70 (gray bar), 'CNBC' = 0, and 'Moody's' = 0. A footnote reads 'Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Bloomberg, CEA Calculations'. The propaganda technique is a favorable data-visualization that contrasts strong actual job numbers against analyst expectations (showing CNBC and Moody's at zero, implying they had no/low forecasts) to dramatize the administration's economic success. Per the tweet, it celebrates the jobs report and credits POTUS with 'PROMISES MADE, PROMISES KEPT.' Target audience is the general public, economic press, and Trump supporters.

notable_text: If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to HELP; 1-855-48-VOICE; ICE.GOV/VOICE; VOICE — VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT
description: A polished public-service-announcement graphic for ICE's VOICE program. The right two-thirds is a warm, soft-focus stock photograph of two pairs of hands clasped together in a comforting gesture, with a seated person (in a light blue shirt and gray pants) being consoled, evoking compassion and support. The left third is a blue overlay panel with white text reading 'If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to' followed by 'HELP' in large bold caps. An orange angled accent shape sits at the right edge. A white footer bar carries contact info in blue and orange: '1-855-48-VOICE' and 'ICE.GOV/VOICE', plus the VOICE logo (a stylized blue 'V') with text 'VOICE — VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT.' The messaging technique is a sympathetic, victim-centered appeal that frames ICE as a helping institution while reinforcing the politically charged 'immigration crime' framing that links crime to immigrants. Target audience is crime victims and the broader public; intent is outreach plus normalizing the VOICE office. This is a recurring template (also used in 3_1991960082607718400).

notable_text: Securing American DOMINANCE; Labor Department Invests $86 Million in Workforce Training; Maricopa County receives $1.5M grant for manufacturing workforce training; DOL to provide $30M in training grants on AI, skilled trades
description: A designed vertical poster combining a dramatic photo and a collage of news headlines. The top half is a moody, low-light photograph of a welder at work, wearing a 'LINCOLN ELECTRIC' welding helmet/mask and a teal shirt, leaning over a metal workpiece with sparks/glow reflected on the visor. Across the center in elegant serif type is the slogan 'Securing American' (italic) over 'DOMINANCE' (large bold all-caps), set on a dark blue gradient. The lower half features three clipped, torn-paper-style news headlines from outlets with visible logos: 'ROOFING CONTRACTOR — Labor Department Invests $86 Million in Workforce Training'; 'KTAR NEWS 92.3 FM — Maricopa County receives $1.5M grant for manufacturing workforce training'; and 'HR DIVE — DOL to provide $30M in training grants on AI, skilled trades.' The propaganda technique uses borrowed credibility from real news outlets plus heroic blue-collar imagery to portray DOL workforce-training investments as securing U.S. economic/technological dominance. Target audience is workers and the public; per the tweet it promotes investment in skills training for shipbuilding and AI.

notable_text: Labor News; Trump Administration proposes rule to expand access to fertility benefits with new legal pathway for employers to offer benefits directly to employees; AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST; AMERICA 250; DOL.GOV
description: A designed 'Labor News' headline graphic built on a photograph of the Frances Perkins (DOL) building exterior. The photo shows the concrete government building under a blue partly-cloudy sky with a large U.S. flag on a pole, and a giant vertical banner hung on the building facade featuring a stylized portrait of President Donald Trump (eyes downcast/serene) with the text 'AMERICA 250', 'AMERICAN WORKERS' and 'FIRST' in large letters. A faint Department of Labor eagle seal watermark and 'DOL.GOV' appear at the lower right. A blue tab reads 'Labor News' in italic, above a bold white serif headline: 'Trump Administration proposes rule to expand access to fertility benefits with new legal pathway for employers to offer benefits directly to employees.' The propaganda technique blends cult-of-personality imagery (the monumental leader portrait banner on a federal building) with a policy-announcement headline. Target audience is workers, employers, and the public; intent is to credit President Trump personally for a family-friendly fertility-benefits rule.

notable_text: SMALL BUSINESS WEEK: The American Dream at Work; English's FAMILY RESTAURANT
description: A designed slogan graphic over a nostalgic vintage-Americana color photograph. The photo shows a classic 1970s-era roadside scene: a black full-size American sedan (Cadillac-style) parked at a corner beside a tall illuminated marquee sign reading 'English's FAMILY RESTAURANT' with a changeable-letter board listing 'CHICKEN HAM SHRIMP / 2 VE / BUTTER AND BISCUIT 4.95' and a rooster emblem, with a small-town intersection, water tower, and traffic lights in the background. At the top center are two agency logos: the U.S. Department of Labor seal and the red/blue 'SBA' (Small Business Administration) wordmark. Bold navy text reads 'SMALL BUSINESS WEEK:' over italic 'The American Dream at Work'. The propaganda technique leverages warm nostalgia and small-town Americana to celebrate small business and the 'American Dream.' Per the tweet, it honors small businesses during Small Business Week and credits the Trump Administration/SBA with cutting red tape and putting American jobs first. Target audience is small-business owners and the general public.

notable_text: GANG ARREST; MS-13 La Mara Salvatrucha; Cesar Lopez-Larios; Material support to terrorists; Financing terrorism; Narco-terrorism; Acts of terrorism
description: A square 'GANG ARREST' rogues-gallery style card produced by ICE. The base is a blurry photograph of a bald, gray-bearded older man (the arrestee, Cesar Lopez-Larios) standing indoors wearing a dark shirt with a yellow lanyard/strap; the back of another person in a gray shirt is visible at left foreground, suggesting an in-custody booking scene. Overlaid diagonally across the image in large, distressed cream/tan graffiti-stencil lettering are the words 'GANG' (upper left) and 'ARREST' (lower right, crossing the image at an angle), evoking gang-tag street typography. A dark banner across the top right carries white serif text 'MS-13' with 'La Mara Salvatrucha' beneath it. A semi-transparent dark-red box at lower left contains the subject's name in bold white, 'Cesar Lopez-Larios', followed by a bulleted charge list: 'Material support to terrorists; Financing terrorism; Narco-terrorism; Acts of terrorism.' The DHS seal and 'ICE' wordmark appear at lower right. The design technique blends a mugshot/wanted-poster format with gang-graffiti aesthetics and terrorism framing to dramatize the arrest, targeting a public-facing social media audience to promote ICE's gang/'terrorist' enforcement narrative.

notable_text: BUILDING THE WORKFORCE OF THE FUTURE; APPRENTICESHIP.GOV
description: A designed motivational poster over a dramatic golden-hour photograph of a construction site. The image shows silhouetted construction workers in hard hats working atop scaffolding/rebar against a vivid orange-and-blue sunset sky with rising rebar columns and steel framework. Overlaid in a tall, narrow condensed white sans-serif font is the headline 'BUILDING THE WORKFORCE OF THE FUTURE' across the top, and at the bottom center in smaller caps 'APPRENTICESHIP.GOV'. The propaganda technique uses aspirational, heroic blue-collar imagery (workers literally building, at a hopeful sunrise/sunset) as a visual metaphor for building the nation's future workforce. Per the tweet it promotes apprenticeships as President Trump's means of building a skilled workforce. Target audience is workers, prospective apprentices, and the general public.

notable_text: A Failure of BUREAUCRACY; 'US high school students lose ground in math and reading, continuing yearslong decline' (NBC News); 'US kids' reading levels plummeted to lowest level in 32-year history' (New York Post); 'US student scores hit new lows in Nation's Report Card' (USA Today)
description: A designed political-messaging graphic from the U.S. Department of Labor built around a dark navy gradient background. The top portion is a black-and-white photograph of the exterior facade of a federal building bearing the carved stone lettering 'DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION'. Centered over it in large white serif/italic typography is the headline 'A Failure of' followed by the much larger bold all-caps 'BUREAUCRACY'. Below this are three overlaid clipped 'torn-paper' style news headline cards arranged in a collage, each branded with a real news outlet logo to lend credibility: (1) NBC NEWS - 'US high school students lose ground in math and reading, continuing yearslong decline'; (2) NEW YORK POST - 'Damning nationwide test shows US kids' reading levels plummeted to lowest level in 32-year history - and Republicans are blaming ‘woke’ education'; (3) USA TODAY - 'US student scores hit new lows in Nation's Report Card'. The visual style uses muted institutional colors with white text, a serious/authoritative tone, and the torn-edge headline-clipping technique to imply documented, third-party-validated failure. The propaganda technique is to blame the federal education 'bureaucracy' (and by extension Biden-era / Democratic governance) for declining student outcomes, supporting the administration's push to dismantle the Department of Education. The accompanying tweet text reads 'The federal education bureaucracy has FAILED our students.' Target audience: general public and supporters of shrinking the federal government.

notable_text: Since 1913 — SERVING THE AMERICAN WORKER; AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST; AMERICA 250
description: A designed commemorative propaganda poster from the U.S. Department of Labor marking its founding anniversary. Background is a deep blue sky with clouds over a color photograph of the Frances Perkins DOL headquarters building in Washington, D.C. Large gold numerals dominate the upper center: 'Since' (in a white script font) above '1913' (huge gold serif), with the white all-caps subhead 'SERVING THE AMERICAN WORKER' beneath, and the small DOL seal centered below the text. The building facade is decorated for a patriotic occasion: a giant U.S. flag hangs vertically at center, red-white-and-blue bunting lines the lower floors, and rows of small American flags line the plaza. Crucially, two enormous building-banner portraits are draped on the facade: at left a stylized portrait of President Donald Trump with an 'AMERICA 250' mark and the slogan 'AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST'; at right a matching banner with a portrait (Frances Perkins / a historical figure) and the same 'AMERICA 250 / AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST' slogan. The carved building name 'FRANCES PERKINS, DEPARTMENT OF LABOR BUILDING' is visible. The visual style is grand, patriotic, and reverential (monumental architecture, gold typography, flags). The messaging technique fuses the agency's 1913 institutional heritage with current Trump-administration branding ('AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST', 'AMERICA 250' semiquincentennial), placing Trump's portrait literally on the historic building. Target audience: the general public and the administration's worker-base constituency. Tweet text: 'Today, we renew our commitment to helping @POTUS put AMERICAN WORKERS FIRST.'

notable_text: 'These policy measures are consistent with the Biden-Harris administration's priorities to eliminate unnecessary barriers to our nation’s legal immigration system... a land of opportunity for those who seek it.' — Acting USCIS Director Tracy Renaud
description: A simple, restrained quote-card graphic (Biden-Harris era USCIS, 2021) on a light blue background with a faint, low-opacity Department of Homeland Security seal watermark behind the text. The layout is clean and corporate: a multi-line quotation set in dark blue/navy sans-serif text, with the attribution in italic at the lower right. The quotation reads: '“These policy measures are consistent with the Biden-Harris administration's priorities to eliminate unnecessary barriers to our nation’s legal immigration system and reduce burdens on noncitizens who may be eligible for immigration benefits. USCIS is committed to promoting policies and procedures that ensure we operate in a fair, efficient, and humane manner that reflects America’s heritage as a land of opportunity for those who seek it.”' attributed to '— Acting USCIS Director Tracy Renaud'. The tone is welcoming and humane, a stark contrast to the later (2025-2026) USCIS enforcement messaging in this dataset. Messaging technique: official spokesperson quote framed as compassionate policy. This is a designed graphic (typographic quote card with seal watermark) rather than a candid photo, but it is mild in design and pre-dates the second Trump administration; included for contrast. Target audience: immigrants/applicants and the general public.

notable_text: Break our laws, lose your privilege.
description: A designed slogan poster from second-Trump-administration USCIS on a light blue gradient background with a faint, large U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal watermark spanning the frame. At the top, a bold two-line headline in dark navy: 'Break our laws,' (bold upright) with 'lose your privilege.' (italic) beneath it. Centered below the slogan are two stacked, realistic specimen immigration-document images: (top) a sample U.S. 'PERMANENT RESIDENT' green card showing surname 'SPECIMEN', given name 'TEST V', USCIS# 000-000-310, category IR1, with a face photo and Statue of Liberty/flag artwork; (bottom) a sample U.S. immigrant 'VISA' issued by 'US CONSULATE GENERAL LONDON', surname 'TRAVELER', IV Case Number LND2004I6000201, Registration Number 00000473, with a face photo. Both document photos are heavily pixelated/scrambled over the personal-data fields to anonymize them. The visual style is clean, institutional, blue-on-blue with crisp serif typography. The propaganda technique is a blunt deterrence threat: framing lawful immigration status (green card / visa) as a revocable 'privilege' that will be lost if the holder breaks the law, reinforcing the administration's recurring 'privilege not a right' enforcement theme. Target audience: lawful permanent residents and visa holders, and the broader public. Note: this is the same scrambled-document specimen art reused across multiple USCIS posts in this dataset (the OCR mojibake about 'priviledge' and 'SPECIMEN' / 'US CONSULATE GENERAL' confirms reuse).

notable_text: Sharing timely & relevant information with law enforcement partners around the world; LAW ENFORCEMENT SUPPORT CENTER; ICE.gov/LESC
description: A designed ICE program-promotion poster in portrait format with a dark, dramatic, desaturated aesthetic. The right side shows a moody black-and-white/blue-tinted photograph of a young Black man wearing eyeglasses and a telephone headset, viewed in profile as if working at a command/dispatch center; faint blue binary-code digits (0s and 1s) are overlaid across the dark background to suggest data and technology. Top-left carries the full-color U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal beside the wordmark 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' in white serif. Below it, white serif text reads 'Sharing timely & relevant information with law enforcement partners' with 'around the world.' emphasized in blue. The lower-left features the large stacked all-caps title in white and blue: 'LAW ENFORCEMENT SUPPORT CENTER' with red triple-slash accents and the URL 'ICE.gov/LESC'. The visual style is sleek and authoritative (high contrast, tech/data motif, bold typographic title block). Messaging technique: portrays ICE's Law Enforcement Support Center as a high-tech intelligence hub aiding police worldwide, projecting competence and reach; tied to tweet copy promoting the LESC hotline (802-872-6020) for identifying 'criminal aliens.' Target audience: law-enforcement partners and the public.

notable_text: If you’re the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we’re here to HELP; 1-855-48-VOICE; ICE.GOV/VOICE; VOICE — Victims of Immigration Crime Engagement
description: A designed ICE VOICE outreach/PSA graphic in portrait format. The right two-thirds is a warm color photograph of two pairs of clasped hands — one person comfortingly holding another's hand with both of theirs, suggesting support/consolation; a seated person in light blue is partially visible. A vertical translucent blue panel overlays the left third carrying the message in white sans-serif text: 'If you’re the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we’re here to' followed by the large bold word 'HELP'. An orange diagonal accent shape cuts across the lower right. A white footer bar at the bottom contains the contact line in blue and orange: '1-855-48-VOICE' and 'ICE.GOV/VOICE', alongside the blue 'V' VOICE logo and the program name 'VOICE — VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT'. The visual style is soft and empathetic (warm photo, supportive imagery, blue/orange brand palette) — distinct from ICE's harsher enforcement graphics. Messaging technique: presents ICE as a compassionate victim-services provider while reinforcing the political frame of 'crime linked to immigration.' This appears to be a recurring/reused VOICE template (an essentially identical design appears elsewhere in the dataset). Target audience: crime victims and the public.

notable_text: Don’t intentionally misrepresent facts during your interview; You can lose your status and be removed from the U.S.
description: A designed warning poster from USCIS. The top half is a color stock photograph of an interview scene: a Black man in a grey button-down shirt sits at a table speaking earnestly to another person seen from behind (back of a head, white shirt), evoking a USCIS officer interviewing an applicant in a bright office setting. The bottom half is a solid dark navy panel containing, centered, the full-color U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal above the agency wordmark 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' in light grey serif text. Below that, in large bold pale-yellow sans-serif type, the headline warning: 'Don’t intentionally misrepresent facts during your interview', and beneath it in white: 'You can lose your status and be removed from the U.S.' The visual style is clean and official (photo-over-color-block layout, agency seal, two-tier typographic hierarchy). The propaganda/messaging technique is deterrence: a direct second-person warning paired with a relatable interview image to threaten loss of status and removal for misrepresentation, tied to the tweet's story about a former Haitian mayor convicted of visa fraud. Target audience: immigration applicants/interviewees and the public.

notable_text: 'The fastest, most sustainable route to entrepreneurship isn’t necessarily through a university lecture hall. It is through a job site.'
description: A minimalist text-only graphic: a block of black serif (Times-like) body text centered on a plain white background, formatted to look like a pull-quote or screenshot of an article paragraph (a Forbes piece referenced in the tweet). The full text reads: 'For decades, ambitious young people have been told the same story; go to college, earn a degree, and climb the corporate ladder or maybe, one day, launch a business. But the reality unfolding across America’s workforce tells a different story. The fastest, most sustainable route to entrepreneurship isn’t necessarily through a university lecture hall. It is through a job site.' There are no images, logos, or color design elements beyond the text itself. The messaging technique is to elevate the skilled trades over a four-year college path, supporting the Trump Department of Labor's apprenticeship/trades workforce agenda by quoting a sympathetic Forbes article. It is a designed share-graphic (deliberately typeset article excerpt) rather than a candid photo, though very plain. Target audience: young people, parents, and trades-workforce advocates.

notable_text: U.S. Department of Labor — Self-Audit Initiative: IDENTIFIES & FIXES POTENTIAL VIOLATIONS; REDUCES LIKELIHOOD OF INVESTIGATION; DEMONSTRATES GOOD-FAITH COMMITMENT TO THE LAW; ENSURES WORKER SAFETY & SECURITY; VISIT DOL.GOV/SELFAUDIT
description: A designed informational checklist graphic (portrait/square, PNG) from the U.S. Department of Labor on a dark navy-blue gradient background with a faint embossed DOL seal watermark behind the heading. At the top, centered, is the white DOL seal logo above the white text 'U.S. Department of Labor' with the italic subhead 'Self-Audit Initiative:'. Below is a four-item benefits checklist, each line preceded by a checkmark inside a square box, in light blue/white small-caps serif text: 'IDENTIFIES & FIXES POTENTIAL VIOLATIONS', 'REDUCES LIKELIHOOD OF INVESTIGATION', 'DEMONSTRATES GOOD-FAITH COMMITMENT TO THE LAW', and 'ENSURES WORKER SAFETY & SECURITY'. A small white footer call-to-action reads 'VISIT DOL.GOV/SELFAUDIT'. The visual style is clean, corporate, and institutional (navy palette, seal, checkbox list). Messaging technique: straightforward compliance promotion encouraging employers/unions/plan administrators to use the voluntary self-audit program; informational rather than the inflammatory enforcement messaging seen in immigration-agency posts. Target audience: employers, unions, and benefits-plan administrators.

notable_text: 'Thanks to President Trump, the Gulf of America is going to survive. His policies have our COMPLETE SUPPORT!' - ACY, Louisiana Shrimper
description: A vertical, portrait-orientation testimonial/quote graphic produced by the U.S. Department of Labor. The background is a candid color photograph of two people standing close together and smiling at each other, framed from below against the steel girders of an industrial bridge or dock structure under a hazy sky. On the left is a woman with long dark brown hair wearing a light blue blazer; on the right is an older weathered white man with grey hair and a grey goatee wearing a light blue collared work shirt bearing a small red embroidered logo/patch on the chest. The lower two-thirds of the image fades into a deep navy-blue gradient overlay that makes the white text legible. A small circular medallion with stylized opening quotation marks ('66) sits centered above the quote. The quote, in white italic serif type, reads: 'Thanks to President Trump, the Gulf of America is going to survive. His policies have our' followed by the words 'COMPLETE SUPPORT!' set in very large bold white serif all-caps for emphasis. At the bottom, attribution in white reads 'ACY' (bold caps) over 'Louisiana Shrimper' (italic). Faint government seal watermarks are visible in the dark lower background. The messaging technique is the testimonial/plain-folks appeal: an ordinary blue-collar worker (a Gulf shrimper) is quoted endorsing President Trump's economic policies and validating the administration's branding of the Gulf of Mexico as the 'Gulf of America.' Target audience is working-class Americans and Trump supporters, intended to portray the administration's deregulation/America First agenda as directly helping everyday industry workers.

notable_text: HALL OF HONOR; 'A nation’s greatness lies in its possibility of achievement in the present, and nothing helps it more than the consciousness of achievement in the past.'; PRESIDENT TEDDY ROOSEVELT — INDUCTED SEPTEMBER 29, 2025; Freedom 250
description: A designed, vintage-styled commemorative tribute poster from the U.S. Department of Labor's 'Hall of Honor' series, in portrait format with a sepia/aged-photo, semi-grunge aesthetic. The background blends a faded American flag and a muted prairie/landscape under a desaturated brown-and-red palette. Dominating the upper center is a large black-and-white archival portrait of President Theodore (Teddy) Roosevelt (mustache, pince-nez glasses, dark suit). The header reads 'HALL OF HONOR' in bold cream all-caps with a small red underline beneath 'OF'. A secondary cut-out shows Roosevelt standing outdoors delivering a speech holding papers. A large white quotation-mark glyph introduces the white serif pull-quote: 'A nation’s greatness lies in its possibility of achievement in the present, and nothing helps it more than the consciousness of achievement in the past.' Lower left credits in white/red: 'PRESIDENT TEDDY ROOSEVELT' and 'INDUCTED SEPTEMBER 29, 2025'. Bottom-right shows the DOL seal and a 'Freedom 250' Betsy-Ross-style 13-star flag mark (semiquincentennial branding). The visual style is patriotic and nostalgic (aged textures, historical portraiture, gold/cream serif type). Messaging technique: heritage/legacy appeal tying the DOL and the administration's 'America 250 / Freedom 250' celebration to revered historical figures. Target audience: the general public and patriotic constituencies.

notable_text: First 100 Days Accomplishments; '...the days of exploiting our immigration system are over. Aliens who want to live and work in America need to do it legally or get out.' — USCIS Spokesman Matthew Tragesser
description: A designed 'First 100 Days' accomplishments quote-card from second-Trump-administration USCIS, on a white background. Top left: bold navy heading 'First 100 Days' with red italic subhead 'Accomplishments'; top right: the full-color U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal beside the agency wordmark 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services'. The body is a large italicized navy serif quotation: '“In the first 100 days, USCIS put a stop to disastrous Biden-era ‘humanitarian’ policies that invited fraud and allowed criminal aliens to legally live and work in our communities; facilitated arrests of criminals attempting to gain immigration benefits; and for the first time in decades is ensuring every alien in the U.S. is registered as required by law,” said USCIS Spokesman Matthew Tragesser. “Aliens, immigration attorneys and non-government organizations take note: the days of exploiting our immigration system are over. Aliens who want to live and work in America need to do it legally or get out.”' At the bottom are decorative red and navy underline rule marks. The visual style is clean and official (agency seal, two-color typographic scheme). Messaging technique: a triumphalist spokesperson quote that disparages prior 'Biden-era humanitarian policies', conflates immigrants with 'criminal aliens' and fraud, and issues a blunt warning ('do it legally or get out') aimed explicitly at aliens, immigration attorneys, and NGOs. Notably, this is the hardline ideological counterpart to the 2021 Tracy Renaud welcoming quote-card elsewhere in this dataset. Target audience: the public and immigration stakeholders.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Special Agent John M. Lacey; End of Watch; Saturday, May 21, 1977; U.S. CUSTOMS — DEPARTMENT OF THE TREASURY — SPECIAL AGENT
description: A designed memorial 'Wall of Honor' tribute card from ICE honoring a fallen agent. The background is a softly blurred color photograph of two deep-red roses lying on a dark slate surface with an out-of-focus American flag (stars and stripes) draped behind at upper right, conveying solemn remembrance. Inset at the upper left, on a black field, is a gold-and-blue legacy law-enforcement badge reading 'U.S. CUSTOMS — THE DEPARTMENT OF THE TREASURY' at top and 'SPECIAL AGENT' on the bottom banner, with a central Treasury seal dated 1789 (reflecting the historic U.S. Customs Service / Treasury origin of the agent's service). Across the bottom, white serif typography reads 'Wall of Honor' (large) beside an ICE blue-ribbon memorial pin/logo, with the dedication text: 'Special Agent John M. Lacey', a gold-ruled divider, 'End of Watch', and the date 'Saturday, May 21, 1977'. The visual style is dignified and funereal (roses, flag, gold accents, serif type). Messaging technique: institutional remembrance/legacy-building that frames ICE as an honorable, sacrifice-bearing law-enforcement lineage. Target audience: ICE personnel, law-enforcement community, and the public. Tweet honors Special Agent John M. Lacey, who died in 1977 from a New Jersey auto-accident injury.

notable_text: PROTECTING THE AMERICAN WORKFORCE; $1M Recovered for Warehouse Workers Denied Pay
description: A horizontal, landscape-orientation slogan graphic from the U.S. Department of Labor. The background photo, warm-toned and dramatically backlit, shows a very large American flag hanging vertically from the ceiling girders inside a cavernous industrial warehouse or factory with sunlight streaming through overhead skylights. The flag is the dominant centerpiece, lit so its red, white and blue glow against the darker shadowed rafters. Across the bottom, large all-caps white text with a soft glow in a condensed stencil/industrial typeface reads 'PROTECTING THE AMERICAN WORKFORCE' and beneath it in white italic serif: '$1M Recovered for Warehouse Workers Denied Pay.' The composition uses patriotic symbolism (the flag, the gritty American factory) to frame a Department of Labor wage-recovery enforcement action as part of the administration's pro-American-worker mission. The messaging technique combines nationalist imagery with a concrete statistic to project the government as a champion fighting for blue-collar workers' paychecks. Target audience is American workers and the general public, signaling the administration delivers tangible results for labor.

notable_text: 13 CHARGED IN ICE HSI & ATF-LED PROBE INTO GUN SMUGGLING RING EXPLOITING THE U.S.-CANADA BORDER

Across the lower third, large bold white sans-serif text delivers the core message in two parts: a headline 'If you're here illegally, leave now.' followed by 'Return the right way and respect our laws for a chance to live the American dream.' The color palette is patriotic red, white, and blue with darkened, ominous shadowing over the human figures. The messaging technique is a self-deportation appeal blending threat ('leave now') with a conditional promise of legitimacy/reward ('the American dream'), targeting undocumented immigrants and signaling the administration's enforcement-plus-encouragement posture to the broader public. Designed agency graphic.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again
description: A vertical, portrait-orientation slogan graphic from USCIS using a dramatic red-to-blue duotone gradient overlay on a photograph of a street arrest. The scene shows, from behind, a tactical officer at left wearing a ballcap, a face covering/gaiter, and a vest with patches reading 'POLICE' and 'VIOLENT GANG TASK FORCE'; he escorts a handcuffed man (wrists cuffed behind back) who wears a patterned/baroque-print jacket and camouflage pants, walking along a residential street lined with brick apartment buildings. The left side of the image glows red and the upper-right is tinted blue, an evocative, cinematic color treatment. A solid dark-blue rectangle in the lower-left contains the slogan in bold white serif text on three lines: 'Making America Safe Again.' This is the recurring DHS/USCIS enforcement-branding template. The messaging technique pairs a gang-task-force arrest with the 'Making America Safe Again' (MAGA-echoing) slogan to frame immigration enforcement as crime-fighting and public protection. Target audience is the general public and Trump's base.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again. USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens.
description: A vertical designed graphic with a dark navy-blue background featuring faint, stylized 'data/tech' imagery - glowing binary digits (1s and 0s), abstract grid lines, bar-chart and circuit-like motifs, and small blue light particles - to evoke advanced analytics and surveillance technology. In the upper-left corner sits the standard campaign banner: a solid blue box with white serif text reading 'Making America Safe Again'. The central headline is large bold white sans-serif text: 'USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens'. Along the bottom are two official seals with white text labels: the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services seal ('U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services') and the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement seal ('U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'), both bearing the DHS eagle emblem. The visual style is corporate-governmental and tech-forward, using the digital aesthetic to project competence and data-driven enforcement power. The propaganda technique frames inter-agency data sharing as a high-tech tool against 'criminal aliens', targeting the general public to convey efficiency and toughness on immigration enforcement. Designed agency graphic.

notable_text: POLICE / ICE (officer's vest). Making America Safe Again.
description: A vertical graphic built on a real enforcement photograph with a slogan overlay. The photo shows an outdoor scene: in the foreground a detained man (gray hair and beard, dark shirt) stands with his back to the camera, hands cuffed behind his back, while at right a law-enforcement officer seen from behind wears a green tactical/molle vest with a tan/khaki patch reading 'POLICE / ICE' and tan cargo pants with a holstered sidearm; a chain-link fence and green trees form the background. Overlaid on the lower-left is the standard solid-blue box with white serif text 'Making America Safe Again'. The composition uses an authentic arrest image to dramatize enforcement of an alleged human smuggler, lending documentary credibility to the slogan. The propaganda technique pairs a real handcuffing scene with the campaign tagline to project successful inter-agency (USCIS/ICE) enforcement, targeting the general public to demonstrate toughness on immigration crime. Designed agency graphic (photo with branded overlay).

notable_text: If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to HELP. 1-855-48-VOICE. ICE.GOV/VOICE. VOICE - Victims of Immigration Crime Engagement.
description: A vertical designed outreach card promoting ICE's VOICE program. The right two-thirds is a warm-toned stock photograph of two pairs of hands clasped together in a comforting/supportive gesture, with a seated person (blurred, in light blue and gray clothing) suggesting a counseling or consoling moment. A blue translucent panel covers the left third and an orange angled wedge accents the lower right. On the left panel, white text reads 'If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to' followed by a very large bold white 'HELP'. A white footer bar at the bottom contains contact info in blue and orange: '1-855-48-VOICE' and 'ICE.GOV/VOICE', alongside the VOICE logo (a stylized blue 'V' checkmark) with text 'VOICE - VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT'. The visual style is soft, empathetic, and service-oriented (blue and orange palette).
