The messaging frames ICE as a compassionate victim-support resource, reinforcing the administration's narrative that immigration is linked to crime while positioning the agency as a helper; target audience is crime victims and the general public. Designed agency graphic.

notable_text: USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens. Making America Safe Again. Analytics Dashboard.
description: A vertical designed graphic split into two zones. The top half is a light powder-blue panel carrying a large bold dark-blue sans-serif headline: 'USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens'. The bottom half is a stock photograph showing a person's hand interacting with a futuristic translucent holographic interface labeled 'Analytics Dashboard' and 'Business Analytics', covered in bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, and figures (e.g. 'Sales', 'Purchased', 'Total Earning', percentages like 65%, 25%, 50%) - evoking big-data and AI-driven analysis. Overlaid on the lower-left is the standard solid-blue box with white serif text 'Making America Safe Again'. The visual style is clean corporate-tech, projecting a high-tech, data-driven enforcement capability. The propaganda technique uses imagery of advanced analytics dashboards to frame USCIS-ICE data sharing as a sophisticated tool for hunting 'criminal aliens', targeting the general public to convey competence and toughness. Designed agency graphic.

notable_text: Have you moved? You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.
description: A vertical informational graphic built on a warm, brightly-lit stock photograph of a home interior during a move: in the foreground sits a large brown cardboard moving box printed with a dark moving-truck icon, with more stacked cardboard moving boxes and a wicker basket blurred in the background near a sunlit window, on a wood floor. Overlaid text at top reads, in large bold red serif, the question 'Have you moved?' followed by bold blue serif body text 'You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.' In the lower-right corner is the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services seal (DHS eagle emblem) with blue serif text 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services'. The style is clean, friendly public-service design (red/blue on a soft warm photo). The messaging is a compliance reminder tied to the Alien Registration Requirement, instructing noncitizens of their address-change obligation (the tweet notes failure may result in fines or removal). Target audience is registered noncitizens. Designed agency graphic (photo with text overlay).

notable_text: Making America Safe Again. USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens.
description: A vertical designed graphic (identical template to other USCIS-ICE analytics posters) with a dark navy-blue background featuring faint stylized 'data/tech' imagery - glowing binary digits (1s and 0s), abstract grid lines, bar-chart and circuit-like motifs, and small blue light particles - evoking advanced analytics and surveillance. In the upper-left corner is the campaign banner: a solid blue box with white serif text 'Making America Safe Again'. The central headline is large bold white sans-serif text: 'USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens'. Along the bottom are two official seals with white text labels - the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services seal and the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement seal, both with the DHS eagle emblem. The visual style is corporate-governmental and tech-forward. The propaganda technique frames inter-agency data sharing as a high-tech tool against 'criminal aliens', targeting the general public to convey efficiency and toughness on immigration enforcement. Designed agency graphic.

notable_text: OSHA. WINTER SAFETY. OSHA.GOV/WINTER-WEATHER.
description: A vertical OSHA winter safety graphic (same design as the agency's other winter-weather card) built on a wintry photograph. The photo shows two white snowplow/utility trucks with orange front plow blades driving down a snow-covered, tree-lined road during active snowfall, headlights on, surrounded by snow-laden bare trees and a white overcast landscape. In the upper-left is the blue 'OSHA' logo. The lower portion fades to a dark navy gradient and carries large bold white italic serif text 'WINTER SAFETY' flanked by dashes, with the URL 'OSHA.GOV/WINTER-WEATHER' beneath in white caps. The style is clean public-service design with a cold blue-and-white palette. This is a DOL/OSHA workplace winter-weather safety message for employers and workers, not an immigration meme. Designed agency graphic (photo with branded text overlay).

notable_text: EXCLUSIVE: Trump "economy is on fire," Labor Secretary Keith Sonderling explains / Matthew Foldi - May 11, 2026
description: A vertical screenshot of an online news article, presented as a shareable graphic. On a white background, a small red 'EXCLUSIVES' tag sits at the top, followed by a large bold black serif headline: 'EXCLUSIVE: Trump "economy is on fire," Labor Secretary Keith Sonderling explains.' Beneath the headline is a byline row with a circular navy avatar containing the initials 'MF,' the author name 'Matthew Foldi,' and a timestamp 'May 11, 2026 - 53 seconds ago.' Below that is the article's lead image: an official portrait of a dark-haired smiling man in a dark suit and light blue tie (Acting Labor Secretary Keith Sonderling) standing before an American flag and a gold-fringed flag bearing the 'DEPARTMENT OF LABOR' seal. The style is a straightforward web-article capture rather than a custom-built meme; it is shared by USDOL to amplify favorable press. Messaging technique: borrowed credibility / favorable-coverage amplification touting Trump-era economic and jobs success ('economy is on fire'). Target audience: general public and supporters, reinforcing a positive economic narrative.

notable_text: ANNOUNCEMENT / Temporary Protected Status / Haiti / Secretary Alejandro N. Mayorkas announces the extension and redesignation of Haiti for Temporary Protected Status (TPS) for 18 months, from August 4, 2024, through February 3, 2026.
description: A horizontal (landscape) designed announcement card in the DHS brand style on a white background. A blue header bar across the top contains the centered white spaced-out word 'ANNOUNCEMENT,' with the circular DHS seal at the left edge; a thin red rule runs beneath the header. The left portion carries a large bold navy serif headline 'Temporary Protected Status.' The right portion shows a light gray world map graphic with a red-outlined label box reading 'Haiti' and a small red star marking Haiti's location in the Caribbean, beside body text: 'Secretary Alejandro N. Mayorkas announces the extension and redesignation of Haiti for Temporary Protected Status (TPS)' (the redesignation phrase emphasized in red) 'for 18 months, from August 4, 2024, through February 3, 2026.' The style is clean, official, government-communication layout (seal, two-tone typography, map locator). Note this is a Biden-era DHS post (Secretary Mayorkas) announcing a TPS extension for Haiti, an informational/policy announcement rather than enforcement propaganda. Target audience: Haitian nationals in the U.S., advocates, and the public.

notable_text: POLICE / ICE (officer's vest). Making America Safe Again.
description: A vertical graphic (same template/photo as another USCIS post) built on a real enforcement photograph with a slogan overlay. The photo shows an outdoor arrest scene: in the foreground a gray-haired, bearded detained man in a dark shirt stands with his back to the camera, hands cuffed behind his back; at right a law-enforcement officer seen from behind wears a green tactical/molle vest with a tan patch reading 'POLICE / ICE' and tan cargo pants with a holstered sidearm; a chain-link fence and green trees form the background. Overlaid on the lower-left is the standard solid-blue box with white serif text 'Making America Safe Again'. The composition uses an authentic handcuffing image to dramatize an arrest (the tweet describes a Nigerian national with an outstanding warrant for child cruelty and aggravated assault), lending documentary weight to the campaign slogan. The propaganda technique pairs a real arrest scene with the tagline to project successful USCIS/ICE enforcement 'one arrest at a time', targeting the general public. Designed agency graphic (photo with branded overlay).

notable_text: First 100 Days Accomplishments / Restoring Stronger Safeguards and Commonsense Policies / 7,120 benefit fraud records; 4,664; Screening 3,568 subjects' social media activity; 2,271 site visits; Referring 462 benefit fraud records and 4,672 egregious public safety records to ICE
description: A vertical statistics infographic card on a white background using the USCIS / DHS brand palette of navy blue and red. Top-left header reads 'First 100 Days' in large bold navy type with 'Accomplishments' in red beneath it; the top-right carries the circular DHS seal next to the navy 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark. A centered red bold subhead reads 'Restoring Stronger Safeguards and Commonsense Policies,' followed by an italic navy line 'Some of our screening and vetting efforts since Jan. 20, 2025 include:'. Below is a vertical list of five line-icon entries (folder, magnifying-glass over a document, person in a magnifier, building/warehouse, file folder with shield), each pairing a red bold statistic with navy descriptive text: 'Completing 7,120 benefit fraud records;', 'Identifying fraud in 4,664 number of records;', 'Screening 3,568 subjects' social media activity;', 'Completing 2,271 site visits to workplaces; and', 'Referring 462 benefit fraud records and 4,672 egregious public safety records to ICE for criminal investigation or enforcement.' The style is clean corporate-government infographic with flat outline icons and emphatic numerals. Messaging technique: a self-congratulatory milestone scorecard quantifying enforcement/vetting output to project competence and toughness within the first 100 days of the administration, including social-media surveillance and referrals to ICE. Target audience: general public and political supporters, signaling aggressive immigration vetting.

notable_text: 115,000 JOBS ADDED / THE GOLDEN AGE / AMERICAN JOBS
description: A horizontal designed graphic built from a dramatic event photograph with a huge text overlay. The photo, shot in a dim industrial/factory hall, shows President Donald Trump at left in a dark navy suit with a red tie, pumping his fist, while a crowd of workers in orange high-visibility jackets and white hard hats fills the right side, many holding up phones to photograph him; a blue podium and a blue banner reading 'THE GOLDEN AGE' (and partial 'AMERICAN JOBS') appear among the crowd. Overlaid across the top in massive distressed white capitals is '115,000' with 'JOBS ADDED' beneath it in large gold/yellow capitals, the numbers dominating the frame. The style is bold, cinematic political-rally propaganda: high-contrast lighting, oversized triumphant typography, hero framing of Trump before adoring blue-collar workers. Messaging technique: a celebratory jobs-report statistic ('The TRUMP Effect,' per the retweeted text) crediting the administration for a strong April Jobs Report, equating the leader with economic prosperity and a coming 'Golden Age.' Target audience: general public and supporters, projecting economic strength and pro-worker populism.

content_type: memorial / Wall of Honor tribute card
notable_text: Wall of Honor. Deportation Officer Frank E. Chaffin. End of Watch. Wednesday, January 28, 1948.
description: A designed 'Wall of Honor' memorial tribute graphic in landscape (1200x675) format honoring a fallen legacy officer. The left portion shows white serif text 'Wall of Honor' over a dark, moody slate-blue textured background, accompanied by a stylized ICE memorial badge with a blue mourning ribbon (badge reads 'ICE / U.S.'). Below in serif type: 'Deportation Officer / Frank E. Chaffin', a thin gold horizontal rule, 'End of Watch' (with the 'of' in italic), another gold rule, and the date 'Wednesday, January 28, 1948'. The center holds a large square inset of a grainy black-and-white archival portrait photograph of an older man (balding, in a suit and tie), consistent with a 1940s-era photo. The right side features a warm photograph of red roses in front of a softly blurred American flag, evoking remembrance and patriotism. There are no suspects, no enforcement scenes, and no aggressive messaging. Technique: solemn, patriotic commemoration (flag, roses, mourning-ribbon badge, refined serif typography) used to build the agency's institutional heritage and valorize officer sacrifice; the tweet notes Chaffin died in a 1948 plane crash while deporting aliens. Target audience: the public and agency personnel; intent is reverence, legacy-building, and legitimacy.

notable_text: Tell the truth in your interview / If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life. / U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services
description: A vertical (portrait) designed graphic combining a stock photo with a dark navy text panel. The top two-thirds is a warm-toned stock photograph of an immigration-interview-like scene: a bearded man wearing glasses and a gray blazer over a white shirt sits at a light wooden table gesturing with both hands as he speaks, facing a second person seen from behind (dark-haired, in a dark jacket) who is holding a notepad and pen, suggesting an applicant being interviewed by an officer. Bookshelves are blurred in the background. The bottom third is a solid dark navy blue panel containing, centered, the circular U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal (eagle), the agency wordmark 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' in gray serif type, then a bold headline in golden-yellow sans-serif reading 'Tell the truth in your interview' followed by white text 'If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life.' The visual style is clean, corporate, government-PSA: muted photo, official seal, two-tone typography (yellow emphasis line over white). The messaging technique is a deterrence/fear appeal warning immigration applicants that lying during USCIS interviews carries a permanent (lifetime) bar from the United States; the tweet ties it to a specific case of an alien convicted for concealing a criminal past. Target audience: immigration benefit applicants and the public, signaling strict vetting and 'make America safe' enforcement posture.

notable_text: There have been almost 47,000 submissions as of April 29 / ARR Alien Registration Requirement
description: A square designed graphic on a pale gray-blue background, styled to resemble a simplified browser or app window: a rounded-rectangle outline frame in medium blue with three short dashes (like window controls / a menu) in the upper left and the DHS seal plus navy 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark in the upper right. Inside the frame, large navy text reads 'There have been almost' with '47,000' rendered in bold red and 'submissions as of April 29' completing the line. Dominating the lower half is a large stylized 'ARR' logo: the letters 'ARR' in bold blue with a swooping red-and-blue ribbon/swoosh curving around the first letter, above the words 'Alien Registration Requirement' (with 'Requirement' in bold). The style is flat, modern, infographic. Messaging technique: a participation/compliance statistic promoting the new Alien Registration Requirement program and implying broad uptake; paired with the tweet's warning that failure to comply may bring fines or removal. Target audience: noncitizens required to register and the public, framing the registration mandate as active and being followed.

notable_text: LABOR NEWS / US Department of Labor eases regulatory burden for vital industries, protecting workers & cutting costs / DOL.GOV
description: A vertical designed 'Labor News' graphic combining a photograph with a headline overlay in the DOL brand style. The photo is a dramatic low-angle shot of the bow of a large cargo/freight ship (dark hull above a red-painted lower hull) in a dry dock or shipyard, with industrial gantry structures at left and an overcast sky. A faint watermark of the circular Department of Labor seal ('...ARTMENT OF...') is ghosted over the lower portion of the image. Across the bottom-left, a blue tab with white italic text reads 'LABOR NEWS' (with three short vertical accent bars), beneath which a bold white serif headline states 'US Department of Labor eases regulatory burden for vital industries, protecting workers & cutting costs.' The lower right carries 'DOL.GOV' in white. The style is a polished branded news-banner/headline card. Messaging technique: positive policy promotion framing deregulation (here, easing burdens under the Longshore & Harbor Workers' Compensation Act for shipbuilding/energy/defense) as simultaneously protecting workers and cutting costs. Target audience: industry stakeholders and the general public.

content_type: propaganda graphic (enforcement photo with slogan overlay)
notable_text: Making America Safe Again / POLICE FEDERAL AGENT / POLICE HSI
description: A designed graphic built from an outdoor enforcement photograph with a slogan overlay. The photo shows two federal agents, seen from behind, escorting a detained man along a brick-walled walkway with green shrubbery. The two officers wear dark navy raid jackets with large light lettering across the back: the left officer's jacket reads 'POLICE / FEDERAL AGENT' and the right officer's reads 'POLICE' with 'HSI' (Homeland Security Investigations) partially visible; the right agent's face is digitally blurred. The man being escorted in the center wears a dark green pullover and tan pants and appears to have his hands restrained behind his back. Overlaid at the lower right is a solid blue rectangle containing white bold serif text 'Making America Safe Again.' The visual style is documentary arrest photography turned into branded messaging via the slogan box, consistent with the recurring USCIS/DHS 'Making America Safe Again' campaign. Messaging technique: showcases a real apprehension (here, an alien linked to suspected human smuggling) as proof of tough enforcement and the USCIS-ICE partnership; the back-of-jacket 'POLICE/FEDERAL AGENT/HSI' lettering and restrained subject convey authority and successful action. Target audience: general public and supporters, projecting strength and deterrence.

notable_text: Labor News / US Department of Labor launches landmark initiative to integrate artificial intelligence skills into Registered Apprenticeships nationwide / DOL.GOV
description: A designed 'news card' style graphic combining a photograph with a captioned headline banner. The upper two-thirds is a color photograph taken inside an industrial warehouse/training facility: a woman in a green blazer and white hard hat with safety glasses (apparently Secretary of Labor) is shaking hands with workers/apprentices, several of whom wear numbered hard hats (38, 51, 61, etc.), safety glasses, and gray work shirts. Lifting equipment, pallet racking, and scaffolding fill the background. A faint embossed U.S. Department of Labor seal is overlaid in the lower-right corner. Across the bottom is a navy/blue label tab reading 'Labor News' in white italic serif, with a few vertical accent bars, above a bold white serif headline on a dark band: 'US Department of Labor launches landmark initiative to integrate artificial intelligence skills into Registered Apprenticeships nationwide.' The lower-right reads 'DOL.GOV'. The messaging is positive promotional/PR framing of a Trump-administration workforce initiative (AI skills in apprenticeships), projecting hands-on engagement with American workers. Target audience: general public, workers, and political supporters.

notable_text: Use the CBP Home app to begin planning your departure and transition back to your home country or another location where you have legal status. / PLAN YOUR DEPARTURE / Welcome To CBP Home / LOGIN OR SIGN UP
description: A polished, agency-designed promotional graphic urging unlawfully present immigrants to use the CBP Home App to self-deport. The composition is split diagonally with the DHS/CBP color palette of navy blue, bright cerulean blue, white, and red. The upper-left shows a smartphone mockup displaying the 'Welcome To CBP Home' app screen, labeled 'Official App of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security' with a small U.S. flag, the DHS seal, descriptive text 'A single portal to multiple CBP services to streamline your experience,' and a red 'LOGIN OR SIGN UP' button with a 'with LOGIN.GOV' note. A speech-bubble shape points from the phone to white headline text on a navy field reading: 'Use the CBP Home app to begin planning your departure and transition back to your home country or another location where you have legal status.' The circular U.S. Customs and Border Protection / DHS seal sits at center. The lower half shows a second smartphone (in a white-gloved hand) displaying app navigation options, beside a red-tinted photograph of a line of people being escorted/walking away across tarmac flanked by an armed officer wearing 'POLICE'. Large bold white block text reads 'PLAN YOUR DEPARTURE.' The propaganda technique pairs a friendly, helpful 'app convenience' framing with an implicit threat of forced removal (the red-tinted enforcement photo), nudging undocumented people toward 'voluntary' departure. Target audience: immigrants without legal status, pressured to leave on their own.

notable_text: U.S. Department of Labor Self-Audit Initiative: Identifies & fixes potential violations / Reduces likelihood of investigation / Demonstrates good-faith commitment to the law / Ensures worker safety & security / VISIT DOL.GOV/SELFAUDIT
description: A designed infographic / bullet-list graphic in a navy-blue palette. The background is a deep blue gradient with a large, faint watermark of the U.S. Department of Labor seal. At top center is the crisp white DOL seal ('DEPARTMENT OF LABOR / UNITED STATES OF AMERICA'). Beneath it, white text reads 'U.S. Department of Labor' (bold serif) and 'Self-Audit Initiative:' (italic serif, underlined with a small decorative line). Four checkmark-box bullet points in white small-caps serif list the program's benefits: 'IDENTIFIES & FIXES POTENTIAL VIOLATIONS', 'REDUCES LIKELIHOOD OF INVESTIGATION', 'DEMONSTRATES GOOD-FAITH COMMITMENT TO THE LAW', and 'ENSURES WORKER SAFETY & SECURITY'. Centered at the bottom in white caps: 'VISIT DOL.GOV/SELFAUDIT'. The technique is a clean, authoritative informational card promoting a voluntary employer compliance program, framing self-auditing as beneficial. Target audience: employers/businesses.

notable_text: “Bad actors are taking advantage of this humanitarian program. President Trump and Secretary Noem are taking decisive action to make America safe again.” — Matthew Tragesser, Chief of Public Affairs at USCIS
description: A designed pull-quote graphic on a dark navy-blue background featuring a large faint decorative open-quotation-mark watermark behind the text. The main quote is set in light blue/white sans-serif type with select phrases bolded for emphasis: '“Bad actors are taking advantage of this humanitarian program. President Trump and Secretary Noem are taking decisive action to make America safe again.”' Below the quote, a light blue attribution box contains dark text reading 'Matthew Tragesser,' (bold) and 'Chief of Public Affairs at USCIS' (italic). The composition is a standard official messaging/quote-card template. The propaganda technique frames the termination of Temporary Protected Status for Afghanistan as a fraud-fighting, public-safety measure, echoing the 'make America safe again' MAGA slogan and invoking Trump and DHS Secretary Noem by name. Target audience: general public and political base.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again / USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens
description: A vertical agency-designed slogan/propaganda poster in a dark navy-blue color scheme. The background is an abstract 'digital/tech' motif of faint binary digits (0s and 1s), data streams, bar-chart and circuit-like graphics, evoking surveillance and data analytics. In the upper-left corner a solid blue box contains white serif text reading 'Making America Safe Again' (a riff on the MAGA slogan). Centered, large bold white sans-serif headline text reads 'USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens.' Along the bottom are two official seals with white labels: the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services seal ('U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services') and the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement seal ('U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'). The messaging technique uses official branding plus a tech/data-power aesthetic to project competence and inevitability of enforcement, framing immigrants as 'criminal aliens' to be hunted via data sharing. Target audience: general public / political base (signaling toughness) and, implicitly, a deterrent message to immigrants.

notable_text: The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register. / ARR – Alien Registration Requirement
description: A clean designed promotional/infographic graphic on a pale blue-gray background advertising a USCIS digital tool. At top, blue sans-serif text reads 'The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register.' Below is a simple line-art illustration of a web browser window (with three dashes/tab marks and a small USCIS/DHS seal plus 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' label in the corner) containing three interlocking red gear/cog icons, symbolizing an online tool/process. Overlaid prominently is the program's logo: a large blue 'ARR' wordmark wrapped by a red swoosh, with 'Alien Registration Requirement' spelled out beneath. The technique is straightforward government-service promotion driving immigrants ('aliens') to a compliance tool. Target audience: noncitizens who may be subject to the Alien Registration Requirement.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again / POLICE
description: A designed slogan poster overlaying a stock-style enforcement photograph with a dramatic red/blue cinematic color grade (simulating police lights). The photo, shot from behind, shows a man in a blue hooded sweatshirt and jeans, hands handcuffed behind his back, being escorted by two officers in black tactical vests boldly marked 'POLICE' (one with a visible holstered sidearm). In the lower-left, a solid blue box holds bold white serif text: 'Making America Safe Again' (a riff on the MAGA slogan). The dark, high-contrast styling and the perp-walk imagery are used to convey toughness on crime and immigration enforcement. The propaganda technique pairs an anonymous handcuffed 'criminal alien' with the patriotic safety slogan to project results. Target audience: general public and political base.

notable_text: Making America Safe Again
description: A designed slogan graphic built over a sepia/duotone-toned photograph. The photo depicts an arrest in progress: a man (back to camera, face not shown) wearing a dark sweater and light khaki pants stands handcuffed behind his back, being held by an officer in a dark tactical jacket whose back reads 'POLICE FEDERAL' (suggesting a foreign/federal police force). They stand beside a dark SUV in a paved outdoor area; a tree is visible upper left. The whole image is rendered in a brown/sepia monochrome to give a gritty, serious tone. In the lower-left a solid blue rectangle box carries large white bold serif text reading 'Making America Safe Again.' No agency seal is visible on the image itself. The propaganda technique pairs an anonymized criminal-arrest scene with the administration's signature slogan to frame immigration enforcement as public-safety action; the cropped/anonymous subject lets it stand in for any 'criminal alien.' Target audience: general public and immigrants, messaging that lawbreakers will be caught and arrested. Tweet context says USCIS Houston helped apprehend an alien connected to a Terroristic Threats incident in partnership with ICE.

notable_text: Get rid of your Christmas tree after Christmas or when it is dry. A dry tree is dangerous. It can catch on fire easily.
description: A flat-illustration public-safety infographic on a solid bright-blue background (horizontal 1200x600 format). On the left, inside a pale circular vignette, a cartoon man with dark hair and a blue shirt carries a Christmas tree out through an open doorway; beside him a small table holds a tear-off wall calendar showing 'DEC 30' with a handwritten red note 'take out tree!' On the right, large white bold text reads 'Get rid of your Christmas tree after Christmas or when it is dry.' followed by lighter white text 'A dry tree is dangerous. It can catch on fire easily.' Bottom-center shows the DHS seal with the 'FEMA' wordmark and the 'U.S. Fire Administration' eagle logo; bottom-right is the 'Fire is Everyone's Fight' logo. This is a seasonal fire-safety PSA (FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration), not immigration content. Friendly flat-vector holiday illustration style. Target audience: general public, post-holiday fire prevention.

notable_text: Have you moved? You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.
description: A clean, professionally designed public-information graphic (vertical/square format). The background is a softly lit, bright photograph of a home interior during a move: a large open cardboard moving box in the lower-left foreground (printed with a simple dark moving-truck icon), additional stacked moving boxes and a wicker basket in the mid-ground, a sunlit window behind, and a wood floor. Overlaid text at top: 'Have you moved?' in large bold red type, followed by 'You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.' in bold blue type. Bottom-right shows the U.S. Department of Homeland Security circular seal next to the 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark in blue. The style is reassuring/clean government PSA aesthetic (red + blue federal palette). Messaging is a compliance reminder targeting immigrants/registrants about the legal obligation to report address changes promptly. Tweet adds that changing your address with USPS will not update it with USCIS and warns of penalties.

notable_text: Help USCIS by Reporting Fraud
description: A designed horizontal banner-style slogan graphic. The background is a dark navy-blue field with a glowing cyan/teal digital fingerprint rendered on the right side, composed of luminous dots and ridge lines evoking biometric scanning and a high-tech surveillance/forensics theme. On the left in large white serif type is the headline 'Help USCIS by Reporting Fraud', with the word 'Fraud' set in bold for emphasis. No additional body text or seal is visible on the image. The style is sleek and corporate-techy (digital-security visual language). The messaging is a call to action recruiting the public to act as informants and report suspected immigration fraud; the fingerprint imagery signals identity verification and law-enforcement capability. Tweet context provides the online tip-form link and the slogan 'Together we can keep America safe.'

notable_text: USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens; Making America Safe Again
description: A designed vertical headline graphic. The top portion is a light blue panel with a bold dark-blue sans-serif headline: 'USCIS Partnering with ICE to Use Advanced Analytics in Targeting Criminal Aliens'. The lower portion is a stock photograph of a hand using a stylus/finger to touch a glowing translucent 'Analytics Dashboard' interface floating above a tablet, showing bar charts, line graphs, pie charts and labels like 'Analytics Dashboard,' 'Business Analytics,' 'Sales,' 'Purchased,' 'Production' and various percentages, evoking big-data analysis. In the lower-left corner a solid blue box holds white text 'Making America Safe Again.' The style is a corporate data/tech aesthetic combined with the administration slogan. The propaganda technique frames inter-agency data sharing and 'advanced analytics' as a powerful surveillance tool aimed at locating 'criminal aliens,' projecting technological competence and toughness. Target audience: general public (reassurance about enforcement) and immigrants (deterrence). Tweet reiterates the data-sharing partnership in support of Trump's EO on immigration enforcement.

notable_text: BEWARE OF SCAMS and always keep these tips in mind; USCIS will never ask for money or payment over the phone; Always preview a link and check that a URL ends in .gov
description: A designed vertical anti-scam warning infographic. The top header is a dark red banner with bold white text 'BEWARE OF SCAMS and always keep these tips in mind:' accompanied by a large red exclamation-point graphic on the right. Below are three stacked tips, each with a small illustrative icon on the left and explanatory text on the right: (1) a computer/monitor icon showing an email with a '.gov' address - 'Make sure emails are sent from addresses ending in .gov'; (2) an illustration of a person on a phone with a red X - 'USCIS will never ask for money or payment over the phone'; (3) a browser/URL icon - 'Always preview a link and check that a URL ends in .gov before clicking on it.' Bottom-left shows the DHS seal and 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark. Clean white background with red/dark accents in standard government PSA style. Messaging is consumer-protection guidance warning immigrants about fraud and impersonation scams. Tweet describes scammers calling applicants to demand payment by phone.

notable_text: Have you moved? You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.
description: A clean public-information graphic identical in design to the other USCIS address-change PSA. Background is a softly lit, bright photo of a home mid-move: a large open cardboard moving box in the lower-left foreground printed with a simple dark moving-truck icon, additional stacked boxes and a wicker basket in the mid-ground, a sunlit window behind, and a wood floor. Overlaid text at top: 'Have you moved?' in large bold red type, then 'You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.' in bold blue type. Bottom-right shows the DHS circular seal beside the 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark. Reassuring clean federal PSA aesthetic (red+blue palette). Compliance reminder targeting immigrants/registrants to report address changes. Tweet emphasizes that updating with USPS will not update USCIS and that USPS will not forward USCIS mail.

notable_text: WINTER WEATHER wisdom; Keep heaters at least 3 feet from anything that can catch fire; Only use generators outside, at least 20 feet from windows & doors; Never use your oven to heat your home
description: A flat-illustration winter-safety infographic on a light blue background. The top half shows a stylized cityscape/skyline of blue and gray buildings topped with snow, a snow-capped lamppost, and a snow-covered house with an evergreen tree, behind a pale panel holding the title 'WINTER WEATHER wisdom' (WINTER WEATHER in bold dark navy caps, 'wisdom' in lighter script-like lowercase). The bottom half is a 2x2 grid of safety tips separated by dashed lines: 'Keep heaters at least 3 feet from anything that can catch fire.'; 'Check on your neighbors to make sure they're okay.'; 'Only use generators outside, at least 20 feet from windows & doors.'; 'Never use your oven to heat your home - it can be a fire hazard.' Bottom-right shows the 'Ready' logo (with a green check) and the DHS seal with 'FEMA' wordmark. This is a FEMA/Ready.gov seasonal preparedness PSA, not immigration content. Friendly flat-vector style. Target audience: general public, winter safety.

notable_text: The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register. ARR - Alien Registration Requirement
description: A designed promotional graphic for a USCIS digital tool on a pale blue background. Top text in dark/medium blue reads 'The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register.' (the phrase 'Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool' is bolded). Below is a flat-line illustration of a web browser window with three traffic-light-style dots in its top bar and the DHS seal plus the 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark in the corner of the browser frame; inside the browser are three interlocking red gear/cog icons (signifying a tool/process). Beneath the browser is the program's logo: a large blue 'ARR' monogram with a red swoosh, captioned 'Alien Registration Requirement.' Clean flat-icon government style. Messaging directs noncitizens/visitors to use the online tool to check whether they must comply with the Alien Registration Requirement; part of the administration's push to enforce registration. Tweet targets visitors to the U.S.

notable_text: ONLINE TOOLS; LOCATORS; SELF SERVICE TOOLS; CITIZENSHIP CORNER; RESEARCH TOOLS; ONLINE RESOURCES
description: A designed promotional graphic (square, white background) advertising USCIS digital resources. Large bold blue centered text reads 'ONLINE TOOLS.' Arranged around the center are small thin-line icons in blue/gray, each labeled, connected by light dotted/wiggly connector lines and small decorative triangles: a location pin labeled 'LOCATORS' (top-left), a wrench/gear labeled 'SELF SERVICE TOOLS' (top), a flag labeled 'CITIZENSHIP CORNER' (right), a compass labeled 'RESEARCH TOOLS' (lower-right), and a folder/document icon labeled 'ONLINE RESOURCES' (bottom-left). Bottom-right corner shows the DHS seal and 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark. Clean, light, modern flat-icon infographic style. Messaging encourages applicants to create a free USCIS online account and use self-service tools; purely service/administrative promotion. Tweet promotes creating a free account for instant case updates (#USCISOnlineTools).

notable_text: Tell the truth in your interview. If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life.
description: A designed warning graphic combining a stock photo with a slogan panel. The top two-thirds is a photograph of an interview/meeting scene: a bearded man in a gray blazer over a white shirt, wearing glasses, sits at a wooden table gesturing with both hands as he speaks across to another person seen from behind (dark hair, dark jacket) who holds a pen over an open notebook, staging an 'interview.' The bottom third is a solid dark-navy panel containing the DHS circular seal, the 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark in gray, then a bold gold/yellow headline 'Tell the truth in your interview' and white subtext 'If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life.' Serious, official government style with a threatening compliance message aimed at immigration applicants/interviewees. The propaganda technique uses an authoritative interview scene plus a severe lifetime-ban warning to deter fraud. Tweet describes the USCIS Denver office facilitating the arrest of a Mexican national wanted for assault, discovered via background checks.

notable_text: Have you moved? You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.
description: A clean public-information graphic identical to the agency's other 'Have you moved?' address-change PSAs. Background is a softly lit, bright photo of a home mid-move: a large open cardboard moving box in the lower-left foreground printed with a simple dark moving-truck icon, additional stacked boxes and a wicker basket in the mid-ground, a sunlit window behind, and a wood floor. Overlaid text at top: 'Have you moved?' in large bold red type, then 'You must notify USCIS of your new address within 10 days.' in bold blue type. Bottom-right shows the DHS circular seal beside the 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark. Reassuring clean federal PSA aesthetic (red+blue palette). Compliance reminder targeting immigrants/registrants to report address changes. Tweet emphasizes that updating with USPS will not update USCIS and that USPS will not forward USCIS mail.

notable_text: The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register. ARR - Alien Registration Requirement
description: A designed promotional graphic for the USCIS Alien Registration Requirement tool, identical to the agency's other ARR card. Pale blue background. Top text in dark/medium blue reads 'The Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool will help you determine if you need to register.' (with 'Alien Registration Requirement Determination Tool' bolded). Below is a flat-line illustration of a browser window with three dots in its top bar and the DHS seal plus 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' wordmark in its corner; inside are three interlocking red gear icons. Beneath sits the program logo: a large blue 'ARR' monogram with a red swoosh, captioned 'Alien Registration Requirement.' Clean flat-icon government style. Messaging directs noncitizens/visitors to use the online tool to determine whether they must register. Tweet targets visitors to the U.S.

notable_text: Register for a free USCIS online account and explore the benefits today! File Online with USCIS. Get started at uscis.gov/file-online.
description: A clean, flat-design promotional graphic on a solid USCIS-blue background, landscape (1200x630) format optimized for social media link cards. The left side carries white sans-serif text in mixed weights: 'Register for a' (regular), 'free USCIS online account' (bold, the headline emphasis), 'and explore the benefits today!' (regular), then in white italic 'File Online with USCIS', and at lower-left 'Get started at uscis.gov/file-online.' (with the URL bolded). The right side features a simple white line-art illustration of an open laptop displaying a login screen: a circular user/avatar icon, a 'NAME' field, and a password field showing asterisks, with two rounded button shapes below. A thin white connector-line motif (a routed circuit-style path with small open circles/nodes) links the laptop to the text block, evoking digital connectivity. There is no photography, no people, and no agency seal beyond the USCIS wordmark in the copy. The messaging is purely a customer-service / e-government convenience appeal encouraging immigrants and applicants to create a free online account and file forms electronically. Target audience is immigration applicants and the general public; intent is administrative outreach rather than enforcement propaganda. The technique is straightforward informational branding (institutional, friendly, benefit-framed), not the aggressive enforcement messaging seen elsewhere from DHS components.

notable_text: LABOR NEWS. US Department of Labor secures agreement with JBS USA, nation's largest meat packing processor, to address child labor. dol.gov/newsroom
description: A square (1080x1080) social-media 'news card' from the U.S. Department of Labor. The background is a darkened, desaturated photograph of a meat-processing/packing plant: a worker in a white coat/lab smock and blue nitrile gloves handling raw poultry or meat in clear plastic clamshell trays moving along a stainless-steel production line; additional blurred workers appear in the background. A semi-transparent dark gradient overlay is applied so the white text reads clearly. In the upper area a small blue rectangular badge/label holds white italic text 'LABOR NEWS'. Below it, large bold white sans-serif headline text reads 'US Department of Labor secures agreement with JBS USA, nation's largest meat packing processor, to address child labor'. At the bottom-right is the bold white URL 'dol.gov/newsroom'. This is a designed graphic (headline-card template with branded label and source URL) rather than a candid photo, even though it uses a stock-style photo as its base. Messaging technique: presenting an enforcement/regulatory win as a news headline, framing DOL as protecting children from unlawful labor and holding a major corporation accountable. Target audience is the general public and press; intent is reputational/accountability messaging. Note: this is a Department of Labor child-labor enforcement card, not an immigration-enforcement meme.

notable_text: Number of law enforcement officers needed to arrest a criminal alien: UNCOOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS 8 to 10 / COOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS 2; ICE.gov/detainers
description: A designed comparison-style infographic with a dark, gritty black asphalt/stone texture background, faintly showing a pair of metal handcuffs lying near the bottom center. The headline at top in white sans-serif (with 'law enforcement officers' bolded) reads: 'Number of law enforcement officers needed to arrest a criminal alien:'. Below are two contrasting columns separated by a vertical divider. The left column has a red banner labeled 'UNCOOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS' with a large white figure '8 to 10' and caption: 'ICE needs more officers to help protect the public from unpredictable behavior by an at-large criminal attempting to evade justice.' The right column has a blue banner labeled 'COOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS' with a large white figure '2' and caption: 'ICE can arrest a criminal alien with as few as two officers in a safe, controlled location, like a jail or court lockup area.' Bottom left shows the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' text; bottom right reads 'ICE.gov/detainers'. The messaging technique is a stark numeric contrast (8-10 vs 2) designed to pressure local jurisdictions to honor ICE detainers, framing non-cooperation (sanctuary policies) as dangerous and resource-wasting. Target audience: local law enforcement agencies, policymakers, and the public to build support for detainer cooperation.

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 1/27/2025 | Houston, Texas; Jose Guadalupe Garza; CRIME: Aggravated assault with deadly weapon; ICE; HOUSTON
description: A designed ICE 'rogues-gallery' card in the standard 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' template. The full-bleed background is the agency's recurring high-contrast black-and-white close-up of a hand locked in steel handcuffs, palm open, with the cuff and chain prominent on the right side. A dark overlay covers the left column and top band. The headline 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' is in bold pale-cyan serif capitals, with white subhead '1/27/2025 | Houston, Texas'. The subject's name 'Jose Guadalupe Garza' is in large bold white sans-serif, followed by 'CRIME:' and a bullet 'Aggravated assault with deadly weapon.' The white DHS/ICE seal and 'ICE' wordmark are in the lower-left corner; a diagonal red banner in the lower-right reads 'HOUSTON'. Like the rest of the series, the card personalizes enforcement by naming an individual and listing a violent crime over menacing handcuff imagery, a propaganda technique framing immigrants as dangerous criminals to justify mass deportation. Target audience: general public and administration supporters.

notable_text: Cook Safely! Prevent Kitchen Fires; Cooking is the leading cause of home fires and home fire injuries.; Stay in the kitchen when you are cooking at high temperatures.; Set a timer if you are simmering or baking
description: A designed FEMA / U.S. Fire Administration kitchen fire-safety infographic in landscape format. A bright blue header bar runs across the top with a flat dark-blue stockpot-with-steam icon at left, the words 'Cook Safely!' in a light script-style font, and a large white headline 'Prevent Kitchen Fires.' The light-gray body is divided into columns by thin vertical rules and uses flat blue/gray/red iconography. Left column ('Did you know?' in red): a stovetop icon with a red panel and pot, captioned 'Cooking is the leading cause of home fires and home fire injuries.' (with 'Cooking' emphasized in blue). Middle column: a pot-and-person silhouette icon with 'Stay in the kitchen when you are cooking at high temperatures.' (emphasis in blue). Right column: a large clock icon with 'Set a timer if you are simmering or baking so you don't get distracted and forget you are cooking.' (emphasis in blue). Bottom-center shows agency logos: FEMA, U.S. Fire Administration (eagle seal), and 'Fire is Everyone's Fight.' The messaging is a straightforward holiday-season cooking-safety PSA built around 'Did you know?' framing and simple actionable tips. Target audience: general public/home cooks. Not immigration-related.

notable_text: YOUR MENTAL HEALTH MATTERS. KNOW WHERE TO SEEK HELP. PTSD AWARENESS MONTH; Veterans Crisis Line 1-800-273-8255; National Suicide Prevention Lifeline 1-800-273-TALK; SAMHSA's National Helpline 1-800-662-HELP; Disaster Distress Helpline 1-800-985-5990; FINDTREATMENT.SAMHSA.GOV
description: A designed public-service infographic (co-branded SAMHSA) in a teal/aqua color scheme. The upper-left bold navy all-caps text reads 'YOUR MENTAL HEALTH MATTERS. KNOW WHERE TO SEEK HELP.' followed by 'PTSD AWARENESS MONTH' with a small teal awareness-ribbon icon. The 'SAMHSA' wordmark is faint at top-right. On the right side is a flat-illustration of a person with long flowing dark hair (the hair forming an abstract dark wave across the upper right) sitting hugging their knees, conveying distress. The body is organized into resource columns with phone and phone+text icons: 'CALL — Veterans Crisis Line 1-800-273-8255 (PRESS 1); National Suicide Prevention Lifeline 1-800-273-TALK (8255); SAMHSA's National Helpline 1-800-662-HELP (4357).' and 'CALL OR TEXT — Disaster Distress Helpline 1-800-985-5990' plus 'VISIT — Behavioral Health Treatment Services Locator FINDTREATMENT.SAMHSA.GOV.' This is a mental-health / PTSD-awareness public service announcement listing crisis hotlines; target audience is the general public, veterans, and disaster survivors. Not immigration-related; produced under the prior administration.

genre_refs: National Geographic 'To Catch a Smuggler' (TV series)
notable_text: TO CATCH A SMUGGLER; NEW EPISODES WEDS 9/8c; NATIONAL GEOGRAPHIC; (c)2025 National Geographic Partners, LLC.
description: A designed television-promo graphic (square format) for the National Geographic series 'To Catch a Smuggler', co-promoted by ICE. The central photograph shows a law-enforcement officer seen from behind, wearing a dark ball cap, jacket and jeans, standing in what looks like an airport/transit terminal, holding the leash of a black detection K-9 (a Belgian Malinois in a 'SECURITY' harness) at his side. Overlaid around the figure on a desaturated teal-tinted background are bright yellow rounded-square pictogram icons (directional arrows, an airplane, a briefcase/luggage, a passport/document, a train) evoking transit, customs and search themes. A bright yellow torn/brushstroke text box across the center carries the bold dark title 'TO CATCH A SMUGGLER'. Below in yellow and white: 'NEW EPISODES / WEDS 9/8c' and the 'NATIONAL GEOGRAPHIC' logo (yellow rectangle wordmark). Fine print bottom-right: '(c)2025 National Geographic Partners, LLC.' The style is a polished broadcast key-art promo. Messaging technique: entertainment cross-promotion that valorizes ICE/HSI and CBP interdiction work as exciting, heroic TV content, normalizing enforcement through pop-culture. Target audience: TV viewers and the public, to boost the agency's image and the show's ratings.

notable_text: JUNE 20, 2024; World Refugee Day
description: A horizontal observance/awareness-day graphic with a soft pale-yellow background. Centered top text in grey sans-serif reads 'JUNE 20, 2024,' below it in large blue serif italic-styled text 'World Refugee Day.' The lower-center features a flat illustrated planet Earth (blue oceans, green continents showing North/South America) ringed by an arc of brown/tan footprint and shoe-print symbols walking around the globe, symbolizing migration and refugee journeys. The white DHS (Department of Homeland Security) seal sits in the bottom-right corner. This is a designed commemorative graphic marking World Refugee Day, using warm colors and walking-footprint imagery to convey a sympathetic, humanitarian message about welcoming refugees; target audience is the general public, signaling agency support for refugee protection (note: this is Biden-era DHS messaging from 2024).

notable_text: PUBLIC SECTOR ENTITIES | Comprises government entities at all levels responsible for overseeing and ensuring the safe adoption of AI | Federal Government / Local Governments / International Cooperation | The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework...
description: A flat-design infographic slide in a deep-blue gradient background, part of a DHS series on its AI 'Roles and Responsibilities Framework.' The white all-caps headline at top reads 'PUBLIC SECTOR ENTITIES,' with an italic white subhead beneath: 'Comprises government entities at all levels responsible for overseeing and ensuring the safe adoption of AI.' A thin horizontal connector line with a circular node spans the slide and branches down to three white card panels, each containing a vector illustration and a light-blue label: (1) a domed Capitol-style building labeled 'Federal Government'; (2) a smaller columned civic building with lampposts, trees, and a red rooftop flag labeled 'Local Governments'; and (3) a green-and-blue stylized globe wrapped in orbital network lines labeled 'International Cooperation.' A lighter-blue band across the bottom contains white body text: 'The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages governments to establish AI safety standards, foster global collaboration, and invest in research that supports AI's safe integration in critical infrastructure.' The circular DHS seal appears at bottom-right. Visual style: clean corporate/government infographic with flat vector icons, sans-serif typography, and a cool blue palette. Messaging technique: explanatory policy communication, projecting competence and a structured framework for AI governance. Target audience: policy stakeholders, infrastructure operators, and the informed public. This is a designed infographic rather than a meme.

notable_text: HSI; Thank you to our HSI workforce for dismantling criminal organizations that threaten the safety of our communities.; #5DaysofThanks
description: A designed Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) 'thank you' graphic, part of a '#5DaysofThanks' Thanksgiving-week appreciation series, in landscape format. The right side is a photograph of a female forensic scientist/lab technician in a white lab coat, blue nitrile gloves, and clear safety glasses, holding up and examining a small brown glass vial of dark liquid (suggesting narcotics/evidence testing) in a clinical laboratory with an emergency shower fixture and equipment behind her. Overlaying the left/top is a very large semi-transparent green 'HSI' wordmark. A solid green band across the lower-left contains bold white text: 'Thank you to our HSI workforce for dismantling criminal organizations that threaten the safety of our communities.' Below that, in large dark text on the photo, is the hashtag '#5DaysofThanks'. The circular DHS seal appears at lower-right. The style pairs a real workforce photo with green brand graphics. Messaging is workforce gratitude plus a law-enforcement mission statement (drug interdiction, dismantling criminal networks). Target audience: HSI personnel and the supportive public. Tangentially enforcement-themed but framed as appreciation, not an attack/propaganda meme.

notable_text: TdA ARREST; Gianfranco Augusto Barazarte-Morales; ASSAULT, WEAPON POSSESSION & DRUG DISTIRBUTION; Tren de Aragua
description: A designed Tren de Aragua 'arrest card' in ICE's recurring template (square). A blue header bar reads 'TdA ARREST' in large gold serif caps. The center background is a candid/in-custody photo of a young man in a dark green hooded jacket against an institutional wall, looking at the camera. Overlaid at left in bold white sans-serif is the name across four lines: 'Gianfranco Augusto Barazarte-Morales.' A red banner across the lower-center lists charges in white caps: 'ASSAULT, WEAPON POSSESSION & DRUG DISTIRBUTION' (note 'DISTIRBUTION' is a typo for distribution). A bottom blue footer bar shows the circular DHS seal with 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' (white) at left and 'Tren de Aragua' in gold serif at right. The card spotlights an alleged gang member's charges in a criminal-card aesthetic aimed at the general public to reinforce the gang-threat narrative.

notable_text: TOP 10 TIPS2PROTECT; TIP 5; Turn off location data services on social media and nonessential apps.; know2protect - Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation
description: A square designed graphic from the DHS 'Know2Protect' 'Top 10 Tips2Protect' online-child-safety series. Blue gradient background (light blue top, navy below) with cut-paper summer clip-art across the top: clouds, butterflies, a camera, a red-and-white life ring, a green popsicle, blue sunglasses, a watermelon slice, and a sun. A yellow rounded banner with black bold text reads 'TOP 10 TIPS2PROTECT.' Large white bold text at left reads 'TIP 5.' Center features an orange silhouette map of the contiguous United States with a magenta/pink map-pin location marker rising from it; the pin's circular center shows a black 'X' (an X-mark / the X logo style), symbolizing turning off location sharing. Large white centered bold text reads: 'Turn off location data services on social media and nonessential apps.' At bottom are the white DHS seal and the 'know2protect' speech-bubble logo with tagline 'Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation.' A parental-guidance public-safety awareness graphic about protecting children's location privacy online; target audience is parents and guardians.

notable_text: 2,700 criminal alien arrests in New York State; U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement
description: A statistics/messaging card with a large black-and-white photographic portrait of a Black woman speaking into two microphones at a lectern; she wears a dark square-neck dress and a multi-strand pearl necklace (contextually New York Attorney General Letitia James, the target of the accompanying tweet). On the left, a semi-transparent navy panel holds the headline in a huge gold serif numeral '2,700' over white serif text 'criminal alien arrests in New York State.' The bottom is a solid navy band bearing the circular DHS seal and the white text 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.' The monochrome treatment of the official, combined with the gold statistic, creates a stark political-attack aesthetic. Propaganda/messaging technique: pairs an enforcement statistic with the image of a Democratic state official the tweet accuses of wanting to 'let them roam free,' framing a political opponent as soft on crime and protective of 'criminal aliens.' Target audience: the general public and political base, to attack sanctuary-aligned officials.

notable_text: ICE; A 19-year-old Bangladeshi migrant reportedly died in ICE custody in El Paso, according to witnesses... This would be the first death recorded since the facility opened; Migrante de bangladesh de 19 anos habria muerto bajo custodia de ICE en El Paso, segun testigos; NOPE.
description: A designed rapid-response rebuttal card on a dark navy background. At the top is the white DHS seal and bold white serif 'ICE' wordmark. Below, in white serif text, it paraphrases a news report being debunked: 'A 19-year-old Bangladeshi migrant reportedly died in ICE custody in El Paso, according to witnesses. A migrant has reportedly died at the Fort Bliss detention center. This would be the first death recorded since the facility opened.' Centered is an embedded screenshot of a Univision 'noticias EL PASO' news webpage with the Spanish headline 'Migrante de bangladesh de 19 anos habria muerto bajo custodia de ICE en El Paso, segun testigos' and subhead 'Presunta muerte de migrante en centro de detencion de Fort Bliss. Seria el primer fallecimiento registrado desde su apertura,' tagged 'NOTICIAS / INMIGRACION.' The bottom features a torn-newspaper texture strip over which is set a very large bold navy meme-style word 'NOPE.' followed by a row of red chevron arrows pointing right. Propaganda technique: directly attacks/discredits press coverage as false ('NOPE'), pairing an official seal with sarcastic internet-meme typography to delegitimize journalism critical of detention conditions (the companion tweet calls it 'dangerous journalism' and 'lies'). Target audience: ICE's social-media followers and the broader public, to preempt and discredit reporting on a detainee death.

notable_text: Worst of the Week; ice.gov/WoW; Sexual Assault; Child Abuse; Child Rape; Fentanyl Possession; Rape; Child Sexual Exploitation; ICE
description: A 'Worst of the Week' rogues-gallery infographic on a black textured background. Twelve real booking/mugshot-style photographs of men are arranged in a grid (four across the top, single columns down the left and right of a central title, and four across the bottom). Each headshot has a gray caption bar beneath it naming an alleged crime: 'Sexual Assault,' 'Aggravated Assault,' 'Child Abuse,' 'Child Sexual Assault,' 'Child Rape,' 'Fentanyl Possession,' 'Cocaine Possession,' 'Rape,' 'Indecency w/ Child,' 'Aggravated Assault,' 'Firearm Possession,' 'Child Sexual Exploitation.' Centered is a bold white serif/sans logo lockup reading 'Worst' with a red parallelogram banner 'of the' and 'Week.' Bottom left is a red bar with white text 'ice.gov/WoW'; bottom right shows the circular DHS seal and the 'ICE' wordmark. The men appear to be of varied ethnicities. Propaganda technique: aggregates mugshots and severe charge labels (heavy emphasis on child sex offenses and drugs) into a recurring branded series that frames immigrants as predators, encouraging followers to 'share' and visit the agency's web page. Target audience: the general public, to build fear-based support for mass enforcement.

notable_text: DOMESTIC VIOLENCE; Worst of the Worst; San Diego; ICE
description: A 'Worst of the Worst' rogues-gallery card styled as a Polaroid instant photo taped to a dark brick wall. The white-framed Polaroid contains a real booking/detainee photo of a Hispanic man with short black hair, a mustache and small soul-patch beard, wearing a dark jacket, seated in what looks like an office; on the white lower border of the Polaroid is handwritten-style black text 'DOMESTIC VIOLENCE.' Below the photo is the brand lockup: bold white serif 'Worst' with a red parallelogram 'of the' and 'Worst,' a thin vertical divider, and the location 'San Diego' in white. At the very bottom center are the DHS seal and the white 'ICE' wordmark. Propaganda technique: presents a single named-by-crime detainee in a casual scrapbook/Polaroid style as part of a recurring branded 'Worst of the Worst' series, personalizing the offender and tying the arrest to a specific ICE field office to dramatize local public-safety wins. Target audience: the general public/social-media followers.

notable_text: #EndTrafficking; Did You Know? Human trafficking victims often know the person who lures them into a trafficking situation.; BLUE CAMPAIGN - One Voice. One Mission. End Human Trafficking.
description: A simple text-driven public-service awareness card on a plain white background framed by a thin black border with small notches at the upper-left and lower-right corners. In the top-left corner, the hashtag '#EndTrafficking' appears in blue. The central message is set in heavy bold sans-serif: 'Did You Know?' in black, followed by the statement 'Human trafficking victims often know the person who lures them into a trafficking situation.' in bright blue. The lower-right corner carries the DHS Blue Campaign logo (a stylized blue circular swirl) with the wordmark 'BLUE CAMPAIGN' and the tagline 'One Voice. One Mission. End Human Trafficking.' This is an official DHS Blue Campaign awareness graphic (not a meme), using minimalist typographic design and a fact/'Did You Know?' framing to educate the public about how human trafficking recruitment works. Intent is educational/awareness; target audience is the general public and potential trafficking victims.

notable_text: This career decision, I can honestly say, is one of the best things I have done IN MY LIFE. -Eddie Craven, Craftsperson, Virginia; NATIONAL APPRENTICESHIP DAY
description: A vertical testimonial quote card (a duplicate/companion design to the other Eddie Craven apprenticeship card) on a deep navy-blue background watermarked with a large faint Department of Labor seal. A white circular quotation-mark emblem sits at top center. The featured pull-quote in large white serif type reads: 'This career decision, I can honestly say, is one of the best things I have done IN MY LIFE.' with 'IN MY LIFE.' emphasized in bold caps. The attribution beneath reads '-Eddie Craven, Craftsperson' in italic with 'Virginia' below. The bottom carries the 'NATIONAL APPRENTICESHIP DAY' wordmark. This is a designed promotional testimonial graphic encouraging people to pursue stable apprenticeship careers, using an inspirational personal quote over official DOL branding. Intent: workforce-development promotion aimed at prospective apprentices and the general public.

notable_text: PARTNER PROGRAMS; Jail Enforcement Model; Task Force Model; Warrant Service Officer; ICE.gov/287g
description: A square dark-navy infographic card explaining ICE's three local/state law-enforcement partnership models under the 287(g) program. The background is a mottled dark blue textured field. At top, white serif text reads 'ICE local and state law enforcement' above a large white serif headline 'PARTNER PROGRAMS'. Down the left side are three rounded-rectangle thumbnail photos, each tinted a different color: the top image (red tint) shows what appears to be jail/prison cell bars with an officer; the middle image (blue tint) shows tactical officers in vests; the bottom image (grayscale) shows a hand writing with a pen on paper/documents. To the right of each thumbnail is a bold white subhead and explanatory body text: 'Jail Enforcement Model - ID and process removable aliens you arrest with active or pending criminal charges.'; 'Task Force Model - Allows your officers to enforce limited immigration authority in certain situations.'; 'Warrant Service Officer - Serve and carry out administrative warrants against illegal aliens.' Bottom-left reads 'ICE.gov/287g' in white; bottom-right shows the DHS seal and the wordmark 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'. The graphic is a clean, corporate-style explainer aimed at recruiting local police departments into immigration enforcement partnerships, using official agency branding and structured bullet content to convey legitimacy and ease of participation.

notable_text: LABOR NEWS / Department of Labor recovers $844K in back wages, damages for 158 workers illegally deprived of overtime wages by Louisiana home care providers / dol.gov/newsroom
description: A square designed 'LABOR NEWS' announcement card in DOL's branded template. The background photo is a warm close-up of hands -- a caregiver in blue clothing clasping/holding the hands of an older person, evoking home health / companion care. Overlaid is a small navy tab with white italic caps 'LABOR NEWS' and a multi-line bold white sans-serif headline on translucent navy ribbon blocks: 'Department of Labor recovers $844K in back wages, damages for 158 workers illegally deprived of overtime wages by Louisiana home care providers.' A navy bar at lower right reads 'dol.gov/newsroom.' This is a designed enforcement-announcement graphic (not a candid photo) publicizing a Wage and Hour Division recovery against home-care employers who misclassified workers as independent contractors. Messaging technique: branded news-promo card portraying the agency as recovering stolen wages for vulnerable care workers; audience is general public, workers, employers. Posted Jan 17 2025, just before the Jan 20 inauguration, using the recurring DOL design template.

notable_text: IT'S TIME TO GET RIGHT WITH THE LAW; ICE.gov/IMAGE
description: A designed slogan/propaganda poster in portrait format built over a real photograph of a busy commercial restaurant kitchen. The photo shows a woman in a gray cap, white T-shirt and plastic apron working at a stainless-steel range, holding a frying pan with food; a second worker in a cap and apron stands at right with stacked dishes, amid steel pots, burner knobs and steam. Overlaid on the upper-left in a bold, distressed/grunge stencil typeface is the slogan 'IT'S TIME TO' (white) 'GET RIGHT' (yellow) 'WITH THE' (white) 'LAW' (very large yellow). The gritty, weathered lettering gives it an aggressive, warning tone. Bottom-left in white reads the URL 'ICE.gov/IMAGE'; bottom-right shows the round DHS seal and the 'ICE' wordmark. The messaging targets employers/businesses (per the tweet, about worksite enforcement and the IMAGE compliance program), using an implied-threat technique aimed at those who hire unauthorized workers, while the imagery of restaurant labor signals the industries under scrutiny. Target audience: business owners, with a coercive 'comply or face enforcement' message.

notable_text: FEMA | Thank you to our FEMA workforce for supporting our nation's communities before, during and after disasters with dedication and resilience. | #5DaysofThanks
description: A designed landscape social-media gratitude graphic in DHS/FEMA house style, a sibling to the CBP '#5DaysofThanks' card. The left portion shows a color photograph of a female FEMA worker wearing a light-blue polo embroidered with the DHS seal and the word 'FEMA'; she has a lanyard ID and holds a FEMA disaster-assistance document/flyer (a phone number is partially legible). Behind her, blurred, is another person and what appears to be a service-counter or field-office setting. The upper-right carries the letters 'FEMA' in giant bold orange capitals. A solid orange banner block on the right-center holds white bold text: 'Thank you to our FEMA workforce for supporting our nation's communities before, during and after disasters with dedication and resilience.' The hashtag '#5DaysofThanks' appears in dark bold text at lower-right. The circular DHS seal is at bottom-left. A faint watermark/graphic element (palm or wave motif) is visible in the lower right. Visual style: documentary photo plus flat FEMA-orange graphic overlays and DHS blue seal. Messaging technique: workforce appreciation and institutional self-praise tied to hurricane-season disaster response. Apparent intent: build public goodwill toward FEMA and recognize staff; audience is the general public and FEMA's own workforce.

notable_text: GANG ARREST; PCC - Primeiro Comando da Capital; Pedro Oscar De Souza-Almeida; Weapons sales and possession; ICE
description: A designed 'GANG ARREST' card with a grunge/graffiti aesthetic (square). The words 'GANG' and 'ARREST' are splashed diagonally across the image in large, distressed, cream-colored stencil/spray-paint lettering. The background is a darkened, pixelated/blurred candid surveillance-style photo of several men sitting outdoors on what appears to be a park bench. The upper-right header reads 'PCC' in large white serif caps with 'Primeiro Comando da Capital' beneath it (referencing the Brazilian prison gang). A semi-transparent red panel at lower left carries the subject's name in white sans-serif, 'Pedro Oscar De Souza-Almeida,' with a bulleted charge: 'Weapons sales and possession.' The lower right shows the circular DHS seal and 'ICE' wordmark in white serif. The gritty graffiti styling and gang branding dramatize the arrest for a general-public audience, tying transnational gangs to the enforcement mission.

notable_text: Sharing timely & relevant information with law enforcement partners around the world. LAW ENFORCEMENT SUPPORT CENTER  ICE.gov/LESC
description: A designed vertical agency promotional poster for ICE's Law Enforcement Support Center (LESC). The background is a desaturated black-and-white close-up photograph of a young Black man in profile wearing a telephone headset with boom mic and eyeglasses, evidently a call-center operator/analyst, with a faint blue digital '1s and 0s' binary-code overlay across the dark left side suggesting data and technology. Top-left carries the blue-and-white DHS seal and the text 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.' Mid-left, in white serif type with the last line in blue, reads: 'Sharing timely & relevant information with law enforcement partners around the world.' The lower-left features a large bold sans-serif lockup 'LAW ENFORCEMENT SUPPORT CENTER' (with 'SUPPORT' in blue and red triple-slash marks) followed by the URL 'ICE.gov/LESC'. Visual style is corporate-institutional, monochrome with blue/red accent typography. Messaging technique: promotes the LESC as a competent, high-tech 24/7 information hub aiding police nationwide/worldwide, positioning ICE as an indispensable law-enforcement partner. Target audience: state/local law enforcement agencies and the general public.

notable_text: TdA ARREST; Yerbis Manuel Garcia-Quintero; KIDNAPPING, FALSE IMPRISONMENT, ROBBERY AND ASSAULT WITH A WEAPON, EXTORTION & WITNESS INITIMIDATION; Tren de Aragua
description: A designed Tren de Aragua 'arrest card' in ICE's recurring template (square). A blue header bar reads 'TdA ARREST' in large gold serif caps. The center background is a candid/in-custody photo of a man with wavy dark hair in an orange jail uniform against a cinderblock detention wall, looking at the camera. Overlaid at left in bold white sans-serif is the name across four lines: 'Yerbis Manuel Garcia-Quintero.' A red banner across the lower portion lists the charges in white caps: 'KIDNAPPING, FALSE IMPRISONMENT, ROBBERY AND ASSAULT WITH A WEAPON, EXTORTION & WITNESS INITIMIDATION' (note 'INITIMIDATION' typo). A bottom blue footer bar shows the circular DHS seal with 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' (white) at left and 'Tren de Aragua' in gold serif at right. The card uses an extensive violent-charge list and most-wanted styling to dramatize the gang threat for the general public.

notable_text: ACCEPTABLE PROOF OF VACCINATION; VERIFIABLE RECORDS (DIGITAL OR PAPER); NON-VERIFIABLE PAPER RECORDS; NON-VERIFIABLE DIGITAL RECORDS; U.S. Customs and Border Protection
description: A clean, official CBP infographic on a dark navy-blue background with a thin light-blue rounded rectangular border framing the content. The headline at top reads 'ACCEPTABLE PROOF OF VACCINATION' in bold white condensed all-caps sans-serif. Inside the bordered box are three rows, each pairing a small blue line-art icon with a heading and explanatory text in white. The first row shows a QR code icon next to 'VERIFIABLE RECORDS (DIGITAL OR PAPER)' with subtext 'Vaccination certificate with QR code, digital pass via Smartphone application with QR code.' The second row shows an ID/card icon labeled 'COVID-19' next to 'NON-VERIFIABLE PAPER RECORDS' with subtext about printouts of COVID-19 vaccination records or certificates issued at national or subnational level. The third row shows a smartphone-with-photo icon next to 'NON-VERIFIABLE DIGITAL RECORDS' describing digital photos of a vaccination card or downloaded records without a QR code. The bottom-left bears the circular DHS/CBP eagle seal and the wordmark 'U.S. Customs and Border Protection.' This is a straightforward government public-information graphic (not a meme), using corporate iconography and a calm institutional palette to communicate COVID-19 vaccination documentation rules to non-U.S. travelers crossing land ports of entry. The intent is informational/regulatory, targeting cross-border travelers.

notable_text: Lightning is hotter than the surface of the Sun and can reach temperatures around 50,000°F; When Thunder Roars Go Indoors; weather.gov/lightning
description: A designed weather-safety infographic (NWS/NOAA) on a dark stormy background showing a photographic night sky with multiple bright lightning bolts striking. At top-left in white text: 'Lightning is hotter than the surface of the Sun and can reach temperatures around.' Dominating the right is the very large white figure '50,000°F.' On the lower-left is an illustration of a thermometer with a red rising column over a cross-section of the layered, glowing orange/yellow interior of the Sun, with a small lightning-bolt icon at the bottom. A small orange/tan caption at bottom-right reads 'When Thunder Roars Go Indoors' with 'weather.gov/lightning' beneath it. This is a Lightning Safety Awareness Week public-service graphic emphasizing how hot lightning is to encourage people to seek shelter; target audience is the general public. Not immigration-related; produced under the prior administration.

notable_text: Ask TSA | 8 a.m. - 6 p.m. Sunday - Saturday Even Holidays
description: A wide designed graphic promoting TSA's 'AskTSA' customer-assistance service, with a cozy autumn/holiday aesthetic. The background is a styled lifestyle photograph on a whitewashed wood surface: a white ceramic mug with a hand-drawn dotted heart, filled with tea, nestled in a chunky knit brown sweater/blanket, surrounded by fall maple leaves (orange/red), cinnamon sticks tied with twine, and star anise. In the lower-left is the 'AskTSA' logo — a stylized briefcase icon with a speech-bubble 'chat' (three dots) — with the wordmark 'Ask TSA.' To the right, black text reads: '8 a.m. - 6 p.m. Sunday - Saturday / Even Holidays.' Visual style: warm, seasonal stock-photo styling with minimal flat branding overlay; soft and inviting rather than enforcement-themed. Messaging technique: friendly/approachable service promotion encouraging travelers to use the AskTSA help channel during holiday travel. Target audience: the traveling public. A designed promotional graphic (not a meme).

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 1/26/2025 | St. Paul, MN; Octavio Juarez Bonilla; CRIME: Possession of child pornography; ICE; ST. PAUL
description: A designed ICE 'rogues-gallery' card in the agency's standard 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' template, but here the background is the subject's own photograph rather than the generic handcuffs image. The full-bleed background is a grainy black-and-white photo of a man with a shaved/short-haired head and a goatee, shown from the chest up in front of what appears to be an indoor setting with a plant; the image quality is low-resolution and surveillance-like. A dark overlay covers the upper band and left column. The headline 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' is in bold pale-cyan serif capitals, with white subhead '1/26/2025 | St. Paul, MN'. The subject's name 'Octavio Juarez Bonilla' is in large bold white sans-serif, followed by 'CRIME:' and a bullet 'Possession of child pornography.' The white DHS/ICE seal and 'ICE' wordmark are in the lower-left; a diagonal red banner in the lower-right reads 'ST. PAUL'.
