The card foregrounds an especially serious alleged offense, a technique meant to maximize public revulsion and justify the deportation campaign by emphasizing the most heinous charges. Target audience: general public and administration supporters.

notable_text: LABOR NEWS / Phoenix drywall, painting contractor must pay $7.4M+ in wages, damages after deliberately denying overtime to 1,400+ employees / dol.gov/newsroom
description: A square designed 'LABOR NEWS' announcement card combining a background photo with overlaid headline typography in DOL's standard template. The photo shows an interior construction/drywall site -- a partially finished room with bare gray drywall ceiling and a recessed light fixture, a stepladder leaning at right, and a worker in a white shirt crouched/working at the base of a wall near a bucket and broom. Across the upper-middle is a small navy-blue tab with white italic caps reading 'LABOR NEWS,' and below it a multi-line bold white sans-serif headline set on translucent navy ribbon blocks: 'Phoenix drywall, painting contractor must pay $7.4M+ in wages, damages after deliberately denying overtime to 1,400+ employees.' A navy bar at lower right carries the wordmark 'dol.gov/newsroom.' This is a designed press/news-promo graphic (not a candid photo) using the agency's branded enforcement-announcement template. Messaging technique: publicizing a Wage and Hour Division enforcement win to project the Department as an aggressive protector of workers' pay; target audience is the general public, workers, and employers. Note: this Jan 17 2025 post predates the second Trump administration's inauguration (Jan 20), reflecting the prior administration's wage-enforcement messaging, but uses the same recurring DOL design template.

notable_text: NATIONAL APPRENTICESHIP DAY; Electrician Joshua Lira says he learns best by doing...; 'Going through the apprenticeship was the best choice I've ever made'
description: A vertical text-heavy profile/story card on a dark navy-blue background watermarked with a faint Department of Labor seal. The header reads 'NATIONAL' in large white serif caps with 'APPRENTICESHIP DAY' in smaller italic beneath. Several paragraphs of small white serif body text narrate the success story of electrician Joshua Lira, who completed a five-year International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers (IBEW) Registered Apprenticeship in four years, became an 'Ultimate Journeyman,' and now enjoys a stable career bending pipe and running circuits, including the quote 'Going through the apprenticeship was the best choice I've ever made.' In the lower-right is an inset photograph of Lira, a bearded man in a gray T-shirt and safety glasses, using a flashlight while working on electrical equipment. This is a designed promotional/biographical graphic celebrating registered apprenticeships and union training, using a relatable worker success narrative on official DOL branding to encourage workforce participation. Target audience: prospective apprentices, workers, and the public.

notable_text: DAILY WIRE + ; EXCLUSIVE; ICE's Most Heroic Moments Of 2025; "Officers show up knowing the risks they face — and they choose to serve anyway."
description: A heavily designed vertical promotional/propaganda graphic co-branded with the conservative outlet Daily Wire+. The top half is a torn-paper, off-white textured background carrying the black 'DAILY WIRE +' wordmark (with a small red plus sign) above a thin red rule reading '— EXCLUSIVE —', followed by the serif headline 'ICE's Most Heroic Moments Of 2025' and a bold pull-quote: '"Officers show up knowing the risks they face — and they choose to serve anyway."' The bottom half, separated by a ragged torn-paper edge, is a dramatic, color-graded nighttime photograph bathed in blue/purple police-light glow: a masked, heavily armored officer wearing a vest marked 'POLICE' walks a handcuffed detainee (hands cuffed behind his back, gray jacket, jeans, ball cap) up a road past SUVs with flashing red-and-blue emergency lights and other officers in the misty background. The composition is cinematic and moody, designed to look like a film-poster or magazine cover. Messaging technique: heroic framing/glorification of ICE agents as selfless, courageous public servants, laundered through a friendly partisan-media 'exclusive,' to build positive emotional sympathy for ICE enforcement. Target audience: pro-enforcement, conservative-media-aligned public.

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 2/6/2025 | Omaha, NE; Adolfo Serrano-Hernandez; CRIMES: Attempted rape; OMAHA; -THE- WORST FIRST; ICE
description: A square designed graphic in ICE's 'THE WORST FIRST' rogues-gallery template, this variant featuring an actual booking/mugshot-style portrait of the subject. The right and central portion shows a color photo of a man (medium complexion, short dark hair, mustache) in what appears to be a tan detention uniform against a dark background. A red diagonal banner in the top-right corner reads 'OMAHA' in white serif caps. The top-left headline is light cyan bold sans-serif 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL,' with a white sub-line '2/6/2025 | Omaha, NE.' Overlaid white bold sans-serif text gives the name 'Adolfo Serrano-Hernandez,' with a 'CRIMES:' label and bulleted charge 'Attempted rape.' The DHS seal and white 'ICE' wordmark appear at bottom left, and a red '-THE- WORST FIRST' box at bottom right. This is the standard agency propaganda layout used to publicize an immigrant's arrest; pairing a mugshot with an emphasized violent sexual charge frames detainees as dangerous offenders and justifies removals. The tweet links to ICE newsroom resources. Audience: the general public and enforcement supporters.

notable_text: DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month; "I think of all the sacrifices my parents made just for the hope of having a different life...how incredibly brave and resilient my parents were to leap into this life with that hope."; Myung K., TSA, Acting Chief of Staff, 2nd generation from South Korea
description: A designed DHS 'National Immigrant Heritage Month' spotlight card in the series template: deep navy-blue background with a faint large American-flag watermark and the white bold headline 'DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month' at top. Centered is a rectangular black-and-white candid family photo of four smiling people (an older woman, a younger woman, and a man in glasses behind, with another woman in front) embracing together outdoors at a gathering. Below a large white quotation mark, the white testimonial reads: "I think of all the sacrifices my parents made just for the hope of having a different life. Just a dream and idea. They did not know how things were going to work out for our family. I think of how incredibly brave and resilient my parents were to leap into this life with that hope." A blue rounded banner at bottom-left reads: 'Myung K. / TSA, Acting Chief of Staff / 2nd generation from South Korea.' The circular DHS seal is at bottom-right. This is pro-immigrant-heritage workforce-spotlight messaging from the prior administration emphasizing family sacrifice and the American dream; target audience is the public and DHS workforce.

notable_text: With self-deportation, you have choices; Learn more at: ICE.gov/self-deportation
description: A designed 'self-deportation' promotional graphic in portrait format. The image shows three young people seen from behind (two with backpacks, hair tied up, one with short dark hair) gazing toward a glowing, warm-toned world map / globe rendered in fiery orange and blue tones with sun flare, suggesting travel abroad and a hopeful horizon. A dark gradient overlay at the bottom carries white and light-blue text: 'With self-deportation, you have' in white sans-serif, followed by a large light-blue script/serif word 'choices'. A solid dark-blue footer bar with a subtle pinstripe pattern contains the round DHS seal and the 'ICE' wordmark on the left, a vertical divider, and 'Learn more at: ICE.gov/self-deportation' on the right. The aspirational, upbeat color palette and forward-gazing youths are designed to reframe self-deportation as an empowering personal 'choice' rather than coercion. Propaganda technique: positive/euphemistic reframing and soft persuasion encouraging unauthorized immigrants to leave voluntarily (the 'CBP Home'/self-deport campaign). Target audience: undocumented immigrants, especially younger ones, nudging voluntary departure.

notable_text: DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month; "[Immigrant Heritage Month] reminds me where I came from and the struggles and sacrifices that my parents had to endure..."; Alexander M., CBP, Customs and Border Protection Officer, 1st generation from Albania
description: A designed DHS 'National Immigrant Heritage Month' spotlight card in the series template: deep navy-blue background with a faint large American-flag watermark and the white bold headline 'DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month' at top. Centered is a rectangular photo of a bearded man in a dark CBP/officer uniform wearing a campaign-style trooper hat, arms crossed, standing in an indoor hall in front of a stone memorial wall engraved with names. Below a large white quotation mark, the white testimonial reads: "[Immigrant Heritage Month] reminds me where I came from and the struggles and sacrifices that my parents had to endure so that I have a better future. Being an immigrant can be very challenging since you have to adjust to different cultures and people, but it also makes you resilient, humble, and able to overcome any difficult situation." A blue rounded banner at bottom-left reads: 'Alexander M. / CBP, Customs and Border Protection Officer / 1st generation from Albania.' The circular DHS seal is at bottom-right. This is pro-immigrant-heritage workforce-spotlight messaging from the prior administration; target audience is the public and DHS workforce, emphasizing resilience and gratitude.

notable_text: Nearly half of parents of teens (46%) say they are EXTREMELY OR VERY WORRIED that their teen's use of social media could lead to them BEING EXPOSED TO EXPLICIT CONTENT.; Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation; know2protect.gov
description: A designed Know2Protect (DHS/HSI) public-awareness statistic card in landscape format. The background is a warm orange-to-magenta gradient overlaid with a faint spiderweb texture (the Know2Protect brand motif). On the left sits a large rounded-square white speech-bubble panel containing a 'peeking through fingers' emoji (a blushing smiling face covering its eyes with both hands), with the bubble's tail pointing right toward the text. The right side carries the statistic in white sans-serif type with key phrases highlighted in white-on-blue boxes: 'Nearly half of parents of teens (46%) say they are EXTREMELY OR VERY WORRIED that their teen's use of social media could lead to them BEING EXPOSED TO EXPLICIT CONTENT.' Bottom-left in white reads the tagline 'Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation' and 'To learn more, go to know2protect.gov'; bottom-right shows the circular Know2Protect logo with a chat-bubble icon. The messaging technique is a data-driven appeal to parental anxiety (citing Pew-style polling) to drive traffic to the campaign's resources. Target audience: parents of teenagers. This is a child-safety/online-exploitation prevention campaign, not immigration-related.

notable_text: ICE local and state law enforcement PARTNER PROGRAMS; Jail Enforcement Model; Task Force Model; Warrant Service Officer; ICE.gov/287g
description: A designed vertical infographic on a dark navy-blue distressed/grunge textured background listing ICE's 287(g) partnership models. The top header reads, in small caps then large white serif: 'ICE local and state law enforcement PARTNER PROGRAMS'. Down the left edge are three rounded thumbnail photos, each color-tinted to match a section: a red-tinted image of hands gripping prison/cell bars (Jail Enforcement), a blue-tinted image of officers in 'POLICE/SHERIFF' marked gear (Task Force), and a grayscale image of a hand signing a document (Warrant Service Officer). To the right of each is a bold serif heading with description: 'Jail Enforcement Model — ID and process removable aliens you arrest with active or pending criminal charges.' / 'Task Force Model — Allows your officers to enforce limited immigration authority in certain situations.' / 'Warrant Service Officer — Serve and carry out administrative warrants against illegal aliens.' Bottom-left has the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'; bottom-right the URL 'ICE.gov/287g'. Style is an institutional explainer/recruitment infographic. Messaging technique: clearly marketing the three 287(g) cooperation tiers to police departments, normalizing local-federal immigration enforcement collaboration. Target audience: state and local law enforcement agencies (recruitment of agency partners).

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 2/12/2025 | Detroit, MI; Juan Antonio Tamayo-Beltran; CRIME: Heroin trafficking conspiracy; DETROIT; -THE- WORST FIRST; ICE
description: A designed square graphic from ICE's recurring 'THE WORST FIRST' arrest-card series. The full background is a high-contrast desaturated/black-and-white close-up photograph of a pair of human hands held out, palms up, locked in metal handcuffs, evoking a captured suspect. A dark gradient overlays the left side so white and pale-cyan text reads clearly. Top-left headline in light cyan small-caps serif reads 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL', with a white subline '2/12/2025 | Detroit, MI'. A red diagonal banner across the top-right corner reads 'DETROIT' in white serif caps. Mid-left, in large bold white sans-serif, the arrestee's name: 'Juan Antonio Tamayo-Beltran'. Below that a bold white label 'CRIME:' followed by a bulleted list: 'Heroin trafficking conspiracy'. Bottom-left shows the round blue-and-white DHS seal next to the white serif 'ICE' wordmark. Bottom-right is a solid red box with white serif text reading '-THE- WORST FIRST'. The propaganda technique pairs an unnamed generic handcuffed-hands stock image (no actual photo of the person) with the named individual and his alleged crime, framing immigration enforcement as the removal of dangerous criminals; the city banner localizes it for regional/local-news amplification. Target audience: general public and local media, to build support for mass deportation by emphasizing 'criminal alien' threats.

notable_text: LIVING OUR TRUTH; DHS celebrates LGBTQIA+ Pride; "This year's Pride theme, 'Living Our Truth,' resonates deeply with us..."; Paul A., Office of Intelligence & Analysis, Deputy Director of Workforce Well-Being, with DHS for 24+ Years
description: A designed DHS social-media spotlight card in a soft pastel color scheme (lavender/pink/light-blue paper-texture background). The top-left corner has a fan of diagonal rainbow/Pride-flag stripes (including the Progress Pride colors: black, brown, pink, white, blue) overlaid with the white outlined headline 'LIVING OUR TRUTH.' Top-right reads 'DHS celebrates' with 'LGBTQIA+ Pride' rendered in multicolor rainbow letters. Centered is a circular cropped photo, framed by a rainbow-gradient ring, of an older man in a light-gray/tan suit standing outdoors on a brick path in a garden, gesturing while speaking, with a small audience of onlookers behind him. Below is a large blue quotation-mark glyph and a serif/sans block quote: "This year's Pride theme, 'Living Our Truth,' resonates deeply with us. The Secretary encouraged us to embrace our authentic selves, both within the Department and beyond. Building on last year's theme, 'Voices for Change,' we recognize that being true to ourselves is essential to creating a safe and welcoming environment." Bottom-left has a small vertical rainbow bar and an attribution: 'Paul A. / Office of Intelligence & Analysis, Deputy Director of Workforce Well-Being / with DHS for 24+ Years.' Bottom-right shows the circular DHS (U.S. Department of Homeland Security) seal accented with rainbow strokes. This is workforce-affinity / diversity-celebration messaging (Pride Month) produced under the prior administration, intended to portray DHS as an inclusive, welcoming employer; target audience is the DHS workforce and the general public.

notable_text: Pet Preparedness Tips; Deborah & Indy; DHS S&T; Program Analyst
description: A horizontal designed graphic in the DHS Science & Technology employee-feature template, themed for '#PetPreparedness.' The background is cream/beige with a faint repeating pattern of dog-bone, fish, and paw-print line illustrations. Top-left has a brown rounded banner with white serif text 'Pet Preparedness Tips.' Below it, dark body text in a sans-serif font reads: 'Please meet Indy. Here's how I prepare her for the summer: 1. I keep a collapsible water bowl, a water bottle, a towel, and treats in my vehicle. 2. To keep her safe, I never leave her in the car alone and ensure she gets rest in the shade and plenty of water on warmer days. 3. On really hot days, I take her to wineries and restaurants in the area that permit dogs. She also participates in dock diving and really loves to swim.' On the right is a rounded-corner color photograph of a woman in a purple jacket standing behind a winery tasting bar lined with many wine bottles; a black-and-white border collie/Australian shepherd-type dog (Indy) sits facing her from the foreground. Beneath the photo a bold black serif name reads 'Deborah & Indy,' with a teal/blue accent line and the text 'DHS S&T' and 'Program Analyst.' The official circular DHS (Department of Homeland Security) seal appears bottom-right. This is a soft-messaging, humanizing public-affairs graphic showing a DHS employee and her pet to promote summer pet-safety preparedness; intended audience is the general public/pet owners, building agency goodwill rather than enforcement messaging.

notable_text: LIVING OUR TRUTH; DHS celebrates LGBTQIA+ Pride; "I respect other people's differing beliefs and religions, but equality and the freedom to love who we love are fundamental rights of humanity..."; Evelyn A., USSS, Officer - Technician of the Canine Unit, with DHS for 17 Years
description: A designed DHS Pride spotlight card in the 'LIVING OUR TRUTH' series template: soft pastel paper-texture background with a fan of diagonal rainbow/Progress-Pride stripes in the top-left corner overlaid with the white outlined headline 'LIVING OUR TRUTH,' and 'DHS celebrates' with multicolor 'LGBTQIA+ Pride' at top-right. Centered is a circular photo, framed by a rainbow-gradient ring, of a smiling woman in a dark Secret Service uniform kneeling on pavement beside a German Shepherd police/canine dog. Below a large blue quotation mark, the block quote reads: "I respect other people's differing beliefs and religions, but equality and the freedom to love who we love are fundamental rights of humanity that should be respected as well. Many people who came before us fought hard so that we could celebrate the month of pride." Bottom-left has a small vertical rainbow bar and attribution: 'Evelyn A. / USSS, Officer - Technician of the Canine Unit / with DHS for 17 Years.' The rainbow-accented circular DHS seal is at bottom-right. This is workforce-affinity / Pride diversity-celebration messaging from the prior administration portraying DHS as inclusive; target audience is the DHS workforce and general public.

notable_text: IT'S TIME TO GET RIGHT WITH THE LAW; ICE.gov/IMAGE
description: A designed slogan poster (portrait orientation) over a photograph of a person's weathered hand washing a dark-colored car with a yellow sponge, with sudsy water streaming down the wet vehicle body and a headlight visible at lower left; warm sunset light glints through trees in the background. Large bold caps text overlaid on the left reads 'IT'S TIME TO' (white) 'GET RIGHT' (bright yellow, distressed/textured font) 'WITH THE' (white) 'LAW' (very large yellow distressed font). The pun plays on the literal act of washing/'cleaning up' a car and the figurative idea of cleaning up one's legal status. Bottom left reads 'ICE.gov/IMAGE' and bottom right shows the DHS seal with the 'ICE' wordmark. This promotes ICE's IMAGE (Mutual Agreement between Government and Employers) program. The propaganda technique uses an everyday wholesome image (car washing) plus a colloquial idiom ('get right with the law') to deliver a veiled warning to employers to ensure workforce compliance or face enforcement. Target audience: business owners/employers.

notable_text: CASE SPOTLIGHT
description: A designed branded title-card graphic in portrait format announcing a 'CASE SPOTLIGHT'. The background is a moody, dark blue-gray textured concrete/fabric surface. At the top, a black tactical flashlight hangs from a lanyard/cord, its lens pointed downward and casting a bright bluish-white triangular beam of light down the center of the image. The headline text sits in the beam/lower-center: 'CASE' in white block caps and 'SPOTLIGHT' in large bold golden-yellow block caps (a literal visual pun on a flashlight 'spotlighting' a case). A darker horizontal band runs along the bottom, where the circular DHS seal and the white 'ICE' wordmark are centered. There are no people, suspects, or case details on the image itself; this is a reusable template/header used to introduce an enforcement case writeup (the accompanying tweet concerns a Medicaid kickback fraud guilty plea investigated by ICE HSI New York). Messaging technique: cinematic, investigative branding (the flashlight-beam motif signals 'shining a light' on crime) that packages routine case announcements in a slick, recurring visual identity. Target audience: general public/social followers; intent is to make ICE investigative work look dramatic and authoritative.

notable_text: DETAINED; Tomas Arana-Fuentes; PENDING CHARGES FOR SEXUAL ASSAULT ON A HELPLESS VICTIM, NON-CONSENSUAL SEXUAL ASSAULT, AND SEXUAL ASSAULT AGAINST A VICTIM OF INCAPABLE OF APPRAISING CONDITION; DENVER
description: A designed single-subject 'DETAINED' card in ICE Denver's standard template: medium-blue background with faint topographic-line texture. The centered square photograph is a candid/intake-style outdoor portrait of a middle-aged man with short dark hair and a mustache, wearing a gray button-up work shirt, standing in a fenced lot with trees behind him. A bold red banner across the bottom of the photo reads 'DETAINED' in large white all-caps. Below, large white serif type gives the name 'Tomas Arana-Fuentes', followed by smaller white text 'PENDING CHARGES FOR SEXUAL ASSAULT ON A HELPLESS VICTIM, NON-CONSENSUAL SEXUAL ASSAULT, AND SEXUAL ASSAULT AGAINST A VICTIM OF INCAPABLE OF APPRAISING CONDITION' (offense phrases bolded; note the awkward phrasing 'VICTIM OF INCAPABLE' appears on the image as printed). Beneath, spaced-out faint capitals read 'DENVER', with the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' wordmark at the bottom. The technique personalizes a single arrest with multiple severe sexual-assault charges to dramatize the danger of 'criminal aliens'; the tweet adds taunting language ('now HE'S the one feeling helpless'). Target audience: general public and enforcement supporters.

notable_text: You have the power to make your community SAFER  ICE.gov/287g
description: A designed vertical advocacy/propaganda poster built on a warm full-bleed photograph of an older white woman with silver hair, wearing a cream denim jacket and beaded bracelet, leaning over a table to handwrite/sign a note with a marker; a blurred American flag and other people are visible behind her, suggesting a community meeting or town hall. Overlaid large display text in golden-yellow and white reads 'You have the power' (with a smaller 'the' tucked in) followed by white text 'to make your community SAFER'. Bottom-left carries the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'; bottom-right the URL 'ICE.gov/287g'. Style is an emotive lifestyle-photo poster with bold mixed-case yellow/white typography. Messaging technique: civic empowerment / call-to-action urging ordinary citizens to lobby their lawmakers to support local police partnerships with ICE (the 287(g) program), using a sympathetic 'everyday grandmother' figure to make the appeal warm and relatable. Target audience: general public/voters, especially older constituents, to generate grassroots political pressure.

notable_text: IT'S TIME TO GET RIGHT WITH THE LAW; ICE.gov/IMAGE; ICE
description: A designed promotional poster for ICE's IMAGE workforce-compliance program. The background is a full-bleed color photograph of a commercial car wash interior: tall blue, teal and green hanging cloth brush rollers fill the left side, while in the right-center two workers (men in dark shirts and work pants, one wearing a hat) hand-wash a dark car with a pressure-spray hose; overhead conveyor track and a yellow awning are visible, and the wet tiled floor in the foreground reflects light. Layered over the left side in large all-caps condensed bold typography is the slogan, with 'IT'S TIME TO' in white and 'GET RIGHT WITH THE LAW' in distressed/grungy bright yellow, with 'LAW' rendered very large. Bottom left in white is the URL 'ICE.gov/IMAGE'; bottom right shows the circular blue-and-white DHS (U.S. Department of Homeland Security) seal beside the white wordmark 'ICE'. The propaganda technique pairs an image of an ostensibly immigrant-staffed worksite (a car wash, an industry associated with undocumented labor) with a coercive command, implicitly warning employers that hiring unauthorized workers is illegal while steering them to ICE's voluntary IMAGE compliance program. The target audience is U.S. business owners/employers, with an undertone of enforcement threat.

A red diagonal corner banner reads 'WASHINGTON' in white serif caps. The top-left headline is light cyan bold sans-serif 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL,' with a white sub-line '2/9/2025 | Washington, DC.' The arrestee's name is in white bold sans-serif: 'Mario Reyes-Flores.' Below is a 'CRIMES:' label with bulleted charges 'Hit and run' and 'DUI.' The DHS seal and white 'ICE' wordmark sit at bottom left; a red '-THE- WORST FIRST' box at bottom right. The fixed template pairs an anonymized handcuffs stock image with a named individual and listed charges as a propaganda device portraying immigration detainees as criminals to justify removal. The tweet links to ICE newsroom resources. Target audience: the general public and supporters of immigration enforcement.

notable_text: LIVING OUR TRUTH; DHS celebrates LGBTQIA+ Pride; "I am blessed to be able to work and live in an environment that is accepting of me on a professional level and family level..."; Angela M., ICE, Financial Program Specialist, with DHS for 5 Years
description: A designed DHS Pride spotlight card in the 'LIVING OUR TRUTH' series template: soft pastel paper-texture background with a fan of diagonal rainbow/Progress-Pride stripes in the top-left corner overlaid with the white outlined headline 'LIVING OUR TRUTH,' and 'DHS celebrates' with multicolor 'LGBTQIA+ Pride' at top-right. Centered is a circular selfie-style photo, framed by a rainbow-gradient ring, of a woman with dark hair pulled back, in a maroon top, in an office/indoor setting. Below a large blue quotation mark, the block quote reads: "I am blessed to be able to work and live in an environment that is accepting of me on a professional level and family level. Years ago, when I first came out, things were not how they are today. Now many of us are making strides to change laws in our workforce and provide training for offices on how to accept all LGBTQIA+ people and work together." Bottom-left has a small vertical rainbow bar and attribution: 'Angela M. / ICE, Financial Program Specialist / with DHS for 5 Years.' The rainbow-accented circular DHS seal is at bottom-right. This is workforce-affinity / Pride diversity-celebration messaging from the prior administration; target audience is the DHS workforce and general public.

notable_text: Worst of the Week; ice.gov/WoW; crime labels: Child Sex Abuse, Manslaughter, Robbery, Stalking, Drug Possession, Aggravated Assault, Homicide, Attempted Murder
description: A designed 'Worst of the Week' rogues-gallery collage card in portrait format on a black textured background. Twelve booking-style mugshot photographs of men are arranged in a grid (four columns, three rows, with the center positions occupied by the title block). Each mugshot has a small dark caption bar beneath it naming an alleged crime: top row 'Child Sex Abuse', 'Manslaughter', 'Robbery', 'Stalking'; middle 'Drug Possession', then the logo, then 'Child Sex Abuse'; next rows 'Aggravated Assault' and 'Manslaughter'; bottom row 'Homicide', 'Child Sex Abuse', 'Manslaughter', 'Attempted Murder'. The men appear to be of varied ages and ethnicities, photographed against jail/booking backgrounds (one shows a height-measurement scale). The centerpiece is large white serif/script branding 'Worst of the Week' with 'of the' set on a red banner. A bottom-left red bar contains the URL 'ice.gov/WoW' in white, and the bottom-right shows the round DHS seal and 'ICE' wordmark. The propaganda technique is a fear-and-threat montage: grouping crime labels with faces to suggest immigrants are dangerous criminals and to encourage the public to 'SHARE their information' (per the tweet). Target audience: the general public, to build support for aggressive immigration enforcement by emphasizing severe violent and sexual offenses.

notable_text: DHS Announces $160 Million First Tranche of Additional Nonprofit Security Grant Program Funds to Protect Faith-Based Institutions and Nonprofit Organizations Against Targeted Attacks; PRESS RELEASE
description: A simple designed 'PRESS RELEASE' title-card graphic on a solid medium-blue background. Large centered white serif text reads: 'DHS Announces $160 Million First Tranche of Additional Nonprofit Security Grant Program Funds to Protect Faith-Based Institutions and Nonprofit Organizations Against Targeted Attacks.' The white circular DHS (U.S. Department of Homeland Security) seal sits to the right of the text. A thin horizontal red rule separates the headline from the bold all-caps white words 'PRESS RELEASE' at the bottom with a subtle drop shadow. This is a policy/announcement title card from the prior (Biden) administration touting grant funding to protect faith-based institutions and nonprofits; target audience is the press and general public. Purely typographic government messaging, no photographs.

notable_text: Don't lose it all for love. | Romance and dating scammers strategically target recently widowed or divorced seniors due to their vulnerabilities and access to cash.
description: A designed public-awareness/anti-fraud graphic from ICE (HSI) warning about romance scams. The background is a close-up photo of a white computer keyboard. Conceptually composited: one keyboard key in the lower-right area is colored bright red and bears a white broken-heart icon. From the left, a folded U.S. dollar bill (Federal Reserve note, '$B2', 'CB 09 37250 A' serial visible) is shown morphing into / plugged in as a metallic USB flash drive connector inserted toward the keyboard — a visual metaphor for money being siphoned away digitally through online romance. Large bold black headline at top-left reads: 'Don't lose it all for love.' Lower-right white bold body text on the dark keyboard reads: 'Romance and dating scammers strategically target recently widowed or divorced seniors due to their vulnerabilities and access to cash.' The U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) seal/wordmark appears at bottom-left. Visual style: photo-composite PSA with strong red accent and bold sans-serif typography. Messaging technique: fear/cautionary appeal using a memorable visual pun (cash-as-USB, broken-heart key) for International Fraud Prevention Week. Target audience: general public, especially older/vulnerable adults and their families.

notable_text: Worst of the Week; Deadly Conduct; Aggravated Assault; Assault; Robbery; Cocaine Possession; Sex w/ Minor; Hit-and-Run; Rape; Fraud; Child Sexual Assault; ice.gov/WoW

notable_text: If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to HELP; 1-855-48-VOICE; ICE.GOV/VOICE; VOICE - VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT
description: A designed public-service/promotional graphic for ICE's VOICE (Victims of Immigration Crime Engagement) office, in vertical format. The right two-thirds is a warm-toned stock photograph of two people's clasped hands, one person comfortingly holding another's hand with both hands, suggesting support/consolation in a soft domestic setting. The left third is a semi-transparent blue overlay panel bearing white text: 'If you're the victim of a crime linked to immigration, we're here to' followed by the word 'HELP' in large bold white caps. An orange angled accent shape sits at the lower right. A white footer bar runs across the bottom: at left in blue and orange is the contact info '1-855-48-VOICE' and 'ICE.GOV/VOICE'; at right is the VOICE logo (a stylized blue/teal checkmark-like 'V') with the wordmark 'VOICE / VICTIMS OF IMMIGRATION CRIME ENGAGEMENT.' The technique frames ICE as compassionate and victim-centered, reinforcing the narrative that immigrants commit crimes against Americans by offering a dedicated victim hotline. Target audience: self-identified victims of crimes by noncitizens and the broader public.

notable_text: 'Native American Heritage Month is important to me because it's a month to celebrate my culture, to bring awareness to our people and our history...' — Jessica, Immigration Service Assistant, USCIS
description: A portrait-orientation employee-spotlight 'quote card' designed for Native American Heritage Month. The border is decorated with diagonal multicolored stripes (orange, teal, mustard, black, cream) evoking a Southwestern/Native textile or blanket pattern. At top, a circular photo medallion (with a layered orange/teal/black ring) shows a smiling woman with long dark hair, orange facial paint/markings on her cheeks, wearing a beaded white-and-green sash/regalia and holding what appears to be a feather fan. The center is a cream parchment-style panel with a large black quotation mark and dark serif/sans body text reading: 'Native American Heritage Month is important to me because it's a month to celebrate my culture, to bring awareness to our people and our history. There are stories of my culture that others may not fully understand, but I can bring awareness and truth about my people and the challenges we faced, not just for this month, but every single day.' Attribution in bold: 'Jessica — Immigration Service Assistant, USCIS.' The circular DHS seal appears at bottom-right. Visual style: testimonial/quote-card layout with culturally themed decorative framing and a documentary portrait. Messaging technique: humanizing the workforce and celebrating diversity/heritage; positions DHS/USCIS as inclusive. Target audience: general public and DHS workforce. A designed graphic, not a meme per se but a propaganda/PR card.

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 1/27/2025 | Dallas, Texas; Nestor Alexander Cortez Mejia; CRIMES: Robbery, Extortion; ICE; DALLAS
description: A designed ICE 'rogues-gallery' card in the standard 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' template. The full-bleed background is the agency's recurring high-contrast black-and-white close-up of a hand locked in steel handcuffs with the palm open. A dark overlay sits on the left column and top band. As with the rest of the series, the design pairs the generic handcuffs imagery with a named individual and listed crimes, a propaganda technique that personalizes immigration enforcement and frames migrants as dangerous criminals to build support for deportations. Target audience: general public and administration supporters.

notable_text: AI DEVELOPERS; Code; Machine Learning; Computing Power; The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages developers to build AI with safety and ethics at the forefront, emphasizing privacy, fairness, and reliability.
description: A designed DHS explainer infographic (one in a series on the DHS AI 'Roles and Responsibilities Framework') in landscape format on a blue gradient background. A bold white centered title reads 'AI DEVELOPERS', with an italic white subtitle: 'Design, train, and enable critical infrastructure owners and operators to access AI models, supplying the intelligence to power software tools or applications.' A thin horizontal connector line with a small node leads down to three white framed illustration panels, each with a light-blue label tab: (1) 'Code' - a desktop monitor screen filled with green binary digits (0s and 1s); (2) 'Machine Learning' - a flat illustration of a network/node graph with a central gear-and-gauge icon suspended on lines; (3) 'Computing Power' - a person seated at a multi-monitor workstation showing code brackets and cloud/data icons. A blue footer bar at the bottom carries white text: 'The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages developers to build AI with safety and ethics at the forefront, emphasizing privacy, fairness, and reliability.' The official DHS seal sits at bottom-right. The style is a clean corporate-government slide deck with flat vector illustrations. Messaging is policy education about responsible AI development. Target audience: AI industry/critical-infrastructure stakeholders and policy-interested public. Not immigration-related.

notable_text: Protect your every day.; If you see something, say something; REPORT SUSPICIOUS ACTIVITY TO LOCAL AUTHORITIES.
description: A designed DHS 'If You See Something, Say Something' public-awareness banner in wide landscape format. The upper-left is a solid light-blue panel with white text: a small line 'Protect your every day.' above the campaign's signature mixed-weight wordmark 'If you see something, say something' (with 'see' and 'say' bolded). Overlaid across the lower-left photo area in bold white all-caps is 'REPORT SUSPICIOUS ACTIVITY TO LOCAL AUTHORITIES.' Bottom-left shows the small 'SEE SAY' campaign logo and the DHS seal, with fine print: '"If You See Something, Say Something" used with permission of the NY Metropolitan Transportation Authority.' notable_text: Washington Examiner — Noem sends warning to illegal immigrants from El Salvador prison with Tren de Aragua members; FOX NEWS — ICE in Boston nabs previously deported Dominican fentanyl trafficker, child rape convict, murderers; Just the News — DHS Secretary Noem says 'gravy train' over at HUD for illegal immigrants; AP — US Homeland Security secretary agrees data-sharing program with Colombia during Bogota visit
description: A DHS-designed news-headline collage graphic arranged as a 2x2 grid with red, white and blue framing and a navy footer bar bearing the white DHS seal. Each quadrant pairs a real news outlet's logo with one of its headlines over a faded background photo: top-left, 'Washington Examiner' over 'Noem sends warning to illegal immigrants from El Salvador prison with Tren de Aragua members'; top-right, 'FOX NEWS' over 'ICE in Boston nabs previously deported Dominican fentanyl trafficker, child rape convict, murderers'; bottom-left, 'Just the News.' over 'DHS Secretary Noem says ‘gravy train’ over at HUD for illegal immigrants' (with a faded photo of Noem in a CBP cap and police vest); bottom-right, the 'AP' logo over 'US Homeland Security secretary agrees data-sharing program with Colombia during Bogota visit.' This is a curated 'press hits' montage the agency designed to showcase favorable media coverage of Secretary Noem and DHS enforcement actions, lending third-party credibility to the deportation agenda. The tweet caption reads 'America First.' Target audience: general public and supporters.

notable_text: Univision reported that this 19-year-old illegal alien died in custody Oct. 20. He's still in custody and very much alive...; Bottom line: This is just dangerous journalism and misinformation.
description: A designed text-statement card, the second image in a two-image rebuttal thread. On a dark navy background with a torn-newspaper texture strip along the bottom, the white DHS seal and bold white serif 'ICE' wordmark sit at top center. The body is a thin-bordered box containing a multi-paragraph white serif statement: 'Univision reported that this 19-year-old illegal alien died in custody Oct. 20. He's still in custody and very much alive, sitting in medical, and claiming to be a juvenile. Following a fainting episode described as a seizure, another alien witness must have called a family member who then called the media. The media states that he is presumed dead -- likely because he was getting medical care away from other detainees. Bottom line: This is just dangerous journalism and misinformation.' Messaging/propaganda technique: an official agency statement formatted as a shareable graphic to rebut and discredit a specific news report, repeatedly using the term 'illegal alien,' casting doubt on the detainee's claimed age, and branding the reporting as 'dangerous journalism and misinformation.' Target audience: the public and press, as part of the agency's anti-media rapid-response messaging.

The centered square photograph is a booking/intake-style portrait of a woman with dark hair pulled back (some strands loose), looking at the camera in front of a white door/wall. (the quantity/'fentanyl pills in her vehicle' bolded). Beneath that, spaced-out faint capitals read 'DENVER', and at the bottom is the DHS seal beside the white wordmark 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.' The technique is a trophy/'busted' card tying an arrest to a large drug seizure to dramatize the public-safety threat. Target audience: general public and immigration-enforcement supporters.

description: A square designed graphic from the DHS 'Know2Protect' online-child-safety campaign, part of a 'Top 10 Tips2Protect' series. The background is a flat blue gradient (lighter sky blue at top, deep navy below) decorated along the top with cut-paper-style summer clip-art: white clouds, a butterfly, a teal camera, a red-and-white life ring/lifesaver, a green popsicle, blue sunglasses, a watermelon slice, and a yellow sun. A yellow rounded banner with black bold text reads 'TOP 10 TIPS2PROTECT.' Large white bold text at left reads 'TIP 9.' Center is a magenta/pink and white line-art icon of a shadowy figure wearing a fedora and domino mask (representing an online predator) hunched over an orange laptop with an Apple logo. Large white centered bold text states: 'Warn your child that they should never leave a game to chat with someone they don't know on a different platform.' At bottom are the white DHS seal and the 'know2protect' logo (a speech-bubble mark) with tagline 'Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation.' This is a parental-guidance/public-safety awareness graphic warning about online grooming tactics; target audience is parents and trusted adults.

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 2/10/2025 | Chicago, IL; Jose Miguel Perez-Perez; CRIME: Child sexual assault; CHICAGO; -THE- WORST FIRST; ICE
description: A square designed graphic in ICE's recurring 'THE WORST FIRST' rogues-gallery template. The background is the standard desaturated black-and-white macro photograph of handcuffed hands against a dark body, serving as a generic image rather than a photo of the named subject. A red diagonal corner banner reads 'CHICAGO' in white serif caps. Top-left cyan bold sans-serif headline 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL'; white sub-line '2/10/2025 | Chicago, IL.' The arrestee's name is set large in white bold sans-serif over four lines: 'Jose Miguel Perez-Perez.' Below is the white 'CRIME:' label and a bulleted charge 'Child sexual assault.' The DHS seal and white 'ICE' wordmark sit at bottom left; a red '-THE- WORST FIRST' box at bottom right. The fixed template (red/white/black with cyan accent) pairs an anonymized handcuffs stock image with a named individual and an emphasized child-sex charge as a propaganda technique to frame immigration detainees as 'the worst' criminals and justify deportation. Audience: the general public and supporters of immigration enforcement.

notable_text: ICE; LEARN MORE; ICE.gov/newsroom; FBI; POLICE
description: A lightly designed arrest photo: a candid color photograph with an added ICE branding overlay in the lower-left corner. The photo shows a smiling young man with short dark hair and a beard, wearing a gray zip-up hoodie over a black graphic t-shirt, standing with hands apparently restrained behind his back between two officers seen from behind. The left officer wears a tactical vest with 'FBI' in yellow on the back; the right officer wears a vest marked 'POLICE' with an 'AR' (Air & Marine / arrest team) patch and a ball cap. A chain-link and white picket fence with trees is in the background. Overlaid graphics: the DHS seal and a white 'ICE' wordmark at lower left, a 'LEARN MORE' button in white on a blue underline, and the URL 'ICE.gov/newsroom' beneath. Per the tweet this is a criminal Brazilian national and confirmed gang member arrested in Bellingham, Mass., with FBI/DEA/ATF assistance. The faces are not blurred. The added agency branding and call-to-action make this a designed promotional graphic, though built on a single candid photo rather than an elaborate meme layout.

notable_text: ICE alone has arrested over 65,000 illegal aliens — including 2,288 gang members from Tren de Aragua, MS-13, 18th Street and other gangs. — TODD LYONS, ICE Acting Director
description: A designed official quote card with a solid medium-blue background. At the top center is a circular cropped headshot portrait of a middle-aged man in a dark suit with a red-and-white striped tie, standing in front of an American flag and a blue flag. Below, set between large light-blue decorative quotation marks, white serif text reads: 'ICE alone has arrested over 65,000 illegal aliens — including 2,288 gang members from Tren de Aragua, MS-13, 18th Street and other gangs.' The attribution beneath reads '— TODD LYONS' with 'ICE Acting Director' in italics. The bottom right shows the DHS seal and the large white 'ICE' wordmark. This is part of a 'FIRST 100 DAYS' series touting enforcement statistics. The propaganda technique uses an authoritative leadership portrait plus a direct quote and large hard numbers (65,000 arrests; 2,288 gang members) to project effectiveness and tie immigration enforcement to combating named violent gangs. Target audience: general public and media, to demonstrate the administration's enforcement results.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Special Agent Timothy M. O'Riordan; End of Watch; Tuesday, September 29, 1964; U.S. CUSTOMS - THE DEPARTMENT OF THE TREASURY - SPECIAL AGENT - 1789
description: A designed 'Wall of Honor' memorial tribute graphic in landscape format honoring a fallen officer. The background is a dark, moody, desaturated stone/slate texture; along the right edge is a soft-focus photograph of a furled American flag (red, white, blue stars visible) with a bouquet of deep-red roses in the lower-right corner, evoking a funeral/remembrance mood. At top-left, large elegant white serif typography reads 'Wall of Honor' (with 'of' in italic script). Beside the headline is the ICE memorial ribbon emblem (a blue-and-white ribbon with the round ICE/DHS seal at its center). Centered in the image is a rectangular black inset photo of a gold law-enforcement badge: a classic eagle-topped shield reading 'U.S. CUSTOMS / THE DEPARTMENT OF THE / TREASURY' across the top banner, a central Treasury seal with '1789', and 'SPECIAL AGENT' on the bottom banner. Lower-left text in white serif reads 'Special Agent / Timothy M. O'Riordan', a thin gold divider, then 'End of Watch' (with 'of' italic), and the date 'Tuesday, September 29, 1964'. The composition is solemn, formal and reverential. Messaging technique: institutional legacy-building and valorization of agents who died in the line of duty, tying modern ICE to the historical U.S. Customs/Treasury enforcement lineage. Target audience: ICE personnel, law-enforcement community, and the public, to evoke respect, sacrifice, and continuity of mission.

notable_text: CASE SPOTLIGHT  ICE
description: A designed vertical 'Case Spotlight' series cover/title card. The background is a dark blue gritty textured wall. From the top center hangs a black tactical flashlight (held by a lanyard/cord) angled downward, casting a bright white triangular beam of light down the middle of the image, evoking a detective/investigation or film-noir motif. Centered over the beam is the large bold title in two stacked words: 'CASE' (white/yellow) and 'SPOTLIGHT' (golden-yellow), in a heavy condensed sans-serif. A horizontal divider band runs near the bottom, beneath which sits the DHS seal and the white 'ICE' wordmark. Style is a moody, branded template graphic used to introduce a featured criminal case (here, per the tweet, a 'Santa Claus' impersonator/retired teacher arrested by HSI Newark for child sexual abuse material). The literal flashlight 'spotlight' is a visual pun on highlighting/exposing a case. Messaging technique: branded recurring-series packaging that dramatizes ICE/HSI investigations and frames the agency as shining a light on hidden criminals. Target audience: general social-media public following ICE enforcement content.

notable_text: Wall of Honor; Air Interdiction Officer John Edwin Hollingsworth; End of Watch; Saturday, October 20, 1984
description: A landscape memorial tribute graphic in ICE's 'Wall of Honor' series. The background is a softly blurred dark scene with a U.S. flag at upper right and a cluster of deep-red roses along the right and bottom edges, conveying solemn remembrance. At upper left, bold white serif text reads 'Wall of Honor' beside a memorial ribbon emblem incorporating an 'ICE' badge with the DHS seal. Centered/right is a large photograph of a gold U.S. Customs badge (Department of the Treasury) inscribed 'AIR INTERDICTION OFFICER,' set against a blue mat. Beneath the title, white serif text reads: 'Air Interdiction Officer / John Edwin Hollingsworth' over a thin gold rule, then 'End of Watch' and the date 'Saturday, October 20, 1984.' Messaging technique: a dignified line-of-duty-death memorial honoring a fallen Customs air officer, used to humanize the agency and emphasize officer sacrifice and 'public safety and national security.' Target audience: the public and agency personnel, as institutional/heritage messaging.

notable_text: CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE OWNERS AND OPERATORS; Energy Infrastructure; Water Infrastructure; Transportation Infrastructure; The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages these operators to deploy AI in ways that protect system integrity and public safety, balancing innovation with security.
description: A designed DHS explainer infographic from the AI 'Roles and Responsibilities Framework' series, in landscape format on a blue gradient background. A bold white centered title reads 'CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE OWNERS AND OPERATORS', with an italic white subtitle: 'Manage and maintain essential systems like energy, water, and transportation that increasingly rely on AI.' A thin horizontal connector line with a node leads to three white framed isometric/flat-illustration panels, each with a light-blue label tab: (1) 'Energy Infrastructure' - a wind turbine, electrical transmission towers/power lines, solar panels, and a substation against a faint cityscape; (2) 'Water Infrastructure' - an isometric water-treatment plant with tanks, solar-paneled roofs, greenery and a tanker truck; (3) 'Transportation Infrastructure' - an isometric city intersection with cars, a bus, a truck, traffic signals and wireless/connectivity icons. A blue footer bar carries white text: 'The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages these operators to deploy AI in ways that protect system integrity and public safety, balancing innovation with security.' The DHS seal is at bottom-right. Clean corporate-government deck style with vector illustrations. Messaging is policy education on securing AI use in critical infrastructure. Target audience: infrastructure operators and policy public. Not immigration-related.

notable_text: TdA ARREST; Pedro Colmenares-Gonzalez; HOMICIDE & SEXUAL ASSAULT WITH A FIREARM; Tren de Aragua
description: A designed Tren de Aragua 'arrest card' in ICE's recurring template (square). A blue header bar reads 'TdA ARREST' in large gold serif caps. The background is a close-up candid/in-custody photo of a middle-aged man with heavy eyebrows, facial tattoos near his left eye, and a gray hooded jacket, looking sternly at the camera. Overlaid at left in bold white sans-serif is the name 'Pedro Colmenares-Gonzalez.' A red banner across the lower-center carries the charges in white caps: 'HOMICIDE & SEXUAL ASSAULT WITH A FIREARM.' A bottom blue footer bar shows the circular DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' (white) at left, with 'Tren de Aragua' in gold serif at right. The most-wanted-style card uses the subject's facial tattoos and violent charges to dramatize the gang threat for a general public audience and bolster support for ICE enforcement.

notable_text: DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month; 'The significance of Immigrant Heritage Month to me is it is a reminder that the United States of America offers the greatest opportunity for anyone no matter where they come from.'; Shane B., FLETC, Civil Engineer (Structural), 1st generation from Canada
description: A vertical (portrait) designed graphic celebrating National Immigrant Heritage Month, in DHS public-affairs branding. The background is a softened/blurred photo of a rippling American flag in blue tones with visible white stars and a sliver of red stripes at the bottom. Top center white bold text reads 'DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month.' Below is an inset rectangular color photo of a smiling middle-aged man with short hair seated in an office, wearing a red/white/blue tropical/Hawaiian-print shirt and a beaded lanyard. Beneath, a large white pull-quote with quotation marks reads: 'The significance of Immigrant Heritage Month to me is it is a reminder that the United States of America offers the greatest opportunity for anyone no matter where they come from.' A blue rounded banner at the bottom-left contains white text 'Shane B.', 'FLETC, Civil Engineer (Structural)', '1st generation from Canada.' The DHS seal sits bottom-right. This is a humanizing employee-feature graphic celebrating immigrant heritage, using a patriotic flag backdrop and a personal testimonial to convey a pro-immigrant, opportunity-of-America message; target audience is the general public and the DHS workforce (Biden-era 2024 messaging).

notable_text: DEPORTED; Rigoberto Carranza-Mendez; CONVICTED OF MURDER AND DUI AND HAS BEEN REMOVED FROM THE UNITED STATES; DENVER
description: A designed single-subject 'DEPORTED' card in ICE Denver's standard template: medium-blue background with faint topographic-line texture. The centered square photograph is a front-facing booking/intake portrait of a man with short dark hair and a thin mustache wearing a dark shirt, photographed inside an institutional room (window and fixtures visible behind him). A bold red banner across the bottom of the photo reads 'DEPORTED' in large white all-caps. Below, large white serif type gives the name 'Rigoberto Carranza-Mendez', followed by smaller white text 'CONVICTED OF MURDER AND DUI AND HAS BEEN REMOVED FROM THE UNITED STATES.' ('MURDER AND DUI' bolded). Beneath that, spaced-out faint capitals read 'DENVER', and the DHS seal with the 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' wordmark appears at the bottom. The technique is a completed-action 'trophy' card celebrating a removal, paired with a tweet mocking 'sanctuary' Denver. Target audience: general public and supporters of aggressive deportation policy.

notable_text: CBP MARCH REPORTING; Making America Safe Again; The United States Southern Border is safer TODAY than any other day in American history; Monthly Total Encounters at the Southern Border; 137,473; 7,180; 95% down; March 2024; March 2025
description: A designed CBP/DHS statistics infographic on a dark green textured (chalkboard/military-canvas style) background. The top is bordered with white stars flanking the header 'CBP MARCH REPORTING'. Beneath, a bold white headline reads 'Making America Safe Again' with a subhead: 'The United States Southern Border is safer TODAY than any other day in American history.' The centerpiece is a bar chart titled 'Monthly Total Encounters at the Southern Border' with a y-axis from 0 to 140,000. A tall red bar for 'March 2024' is labeled '137,473' and has a small circular cut-out photo of President Biden's face at its base; a tiny green bar for 'March 2025' is labeled '7,180'. A large green downward-curving arrow sweeps from the top of the Biden bar to the small 2025 bar, with a circular cut-out photo of President Trump's face and a '95%' down-arrow label, dramatizing the decline. A footnote reads '*CBP data from March 1 - 30, 2024 versus 2025.' The white DHS seal sits in the lower-right. The infographic uses the politicians' faces, color coding (red=bad/Biden, green=good/Trump), and a giant arrow to credit Trump with a steep drop in border crossings. Target audience: general public and supporters.

notable_text: ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL; 2/11/2025 | New Orleans, LA; Dany Belkis Vasquez-Rodriguez; CRIMES: Fentanyl trafficking conspiracy; THE WORST FIRST; NEW ORLEANS
description: A square 'arrest card' graphic from ICE's 'THE WORST FIRST' branded series. The background is a high-contrast black-and-white close-up photograph of a person's hand and wrist locked in a metal handcuff, with the open palm facing up. A red diagonal banner across the top-right corner reads 'NEW ORLEANS' in white serif text (a regional/field-office tag). The upper-left carries a light-blue (cyan) serif headline 'ARRESTED AND PENDING REMOVAL' with a white sub-line '2/11/2025 | New Orleans, LA'. The subject's name is set large in bold white sans-serif over the photo: 'Dany Belkis Vasquez-Rodriguez'. Below it, 'CRIMES:' in bold white with a bullet: 'Fentanyl trafficking conspiracy.' Lower-left shows the DHS seal and white 'ICE' wordmark. Lower-right is a red box containing the program logotype '-THE- WORST FIRST' in white serif type. The design uses dramatic handcuff imagery, a consistent red/black/white template, and the 'Worst First' slogan to frame the individual as a dangerous priority criminal, targeting a public social-media audience to publicize and justify removals.

notable_text: DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month; "Although I am very Americanized in many ways, I am still fluent in my native tongue Ga..."; Alvin M., FEMA, Contract Specialist, 1st generation from Ghana
description: A designed DHS 'National Immigrant Heritage Month' spotlight card matching the series template: deep navy-blue background with a faint large American-flag watermark, and the white bold headline 'DHS Celebrates National Immigrant Heritage Month' at top. Centered is a rectangular portrait of a smiling young Black man in a dark suit, white shirt and dark tie, standing in front of an American flag and a second (state/agency) flag. Below a large white quotation mark, the white testimonial reads: "Although I am very Americanized in many ways, I am still fluent in my native tongue Ga. Speaking my native country's dialect and eating our native foods brings me a deep sense of connection to the many generations of my family that came before me." A blue rounded banner at bottom-left gives the attribution: 'Alvin M. / FEMA, Contract Specialist / 1st generation from Ghana.' The circular DHS seal sits at bottom-right. This is pro-immigrant-heritage workforce-spotlight messaging from the prior administration, celebrating cultural diversity among federal employees; target audience is the public and DHS workforce.

notable_text: MOST WANTED; ANONYMOUS TIP LINE 866-DHS-2-ICE
description: A designed 'MOST WANTED' fugitive card in a square format. A bold red banner across the top carries the white serif headline 'MOST WANTED'. The center is filled with a booking/mugshot-style color photograph of a man with short dark hair, thin eyebrows, a mustache and a goatee, wearing an orange jail jumpsuit, photographed against a plain pale blue-gray wall and looking slightly off-camera. A matching red banner across the bottom reads 'ANONYMOUS TIP LINE' above the large white number '866-DHS-2-ICE', with the DHS seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement' text to the right. Per the tweet this is ICE Most Wanted fugitive Ismael Ortega Hernandez (homicide warrant in Arizona, a Mexican national). The styling deliberately evokes a classic law-enforcement wanted poster (red/white, mugshot, tip line) to solicit public tips and reinforce a narrative of dangerous criminal aliens at large. Target audience: the general public, to crowdsource location tips.

notable_text: Student and Exchange Visitor Program; Government agencies needing information about aliens coming to the U.S. primarily as students can contact us at: ICE.gov/SEVP
description: A designed square promotional graphic for ICE's Student and Exchange Visitor Program (SEVP). The background is a moody, cinematic (likely AI-generated or heavily stylized) photo of a young person with a backpack seen from behind, silhouetted against a hazy sunset sky, gazing toward a billowing American flag on a pole at left. Overlaid in large white serif type at upper right is the headline 'Student and Exchange Visitor Program' (with 'and' smaller). Beneath it, smaller white sans-serif body text reads: 'Government agencies needing information about aliens coming to the U.S. primarily as students can contact us at:' followed by a blue angular call-to-action banner reading 'ICE.gov/SEVP' (with SEVP in orange). The lower right carries the circular blue DHS seal and the wordmark 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.' The patriotic flag imagery and 'aliens' terminology frame student-visa tracking as a national-security/surveillance function; the target audience is other government agencies and the general public.

notable_text: Despite ... false information ... about the brave men and women who work for ICE, they continue to ... put their own lives at risk to make our communities safer. -BRET BRADFORD, ICE Houston Field Office Director
description: A square solid-blue (ICE corporate blue) quotation graphic with no photograph. A large light-blue/gray opening curly quotation mark sits at the upper left. The main text is set in a large white serif typeface reading: 'Despite ... false information ... about the brave men and women who work for ICE, they continue to ... put their own lives at risk to make our communities safer.' Below the quote, an attribution in smaller white type reads '-BRET BRADFORD' with 'ICE Houston Field Office Director' beneath it. The lower-right corner carries the DHS seal and the bold white 'ICE' wordmark. This is a defensive/morale messaging graphic: the propaganda technique frames ICE officers as heroic and self-sacrificing while dismissing criticism as 'false information,' aimed at supportive public audiences and at boosting agency image amid scrutiny.

notable_text: On this day, In 2012, President Obama launched DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals).
description: A wide (1200x675) designed 'On this day' anniversary/infographic card commemorating the 12th anniversary of DACA. The left two-thirds is a clean off-white/pale-blue background carrying bold black text: a large serif headline 'On this day,' followed by sans serif body text 'In 2012, President Obama launched DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals).' Along the bottom runs a horizontal timeline graphic — a multicolor (yellow-to-green-to-blue gradient) line punctuated by open circular node markers, evoking a chronological milestone. The right third is an angled photographic inset showing President Barack Obama (in a dark suit) standing in the Oval Office beside a young woman in a black blazer and pink top, with another person in the background; the historical photo references the program's launch. The blue-and-white U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal sits in the lower-right corner over the photo. Style: editorial 'this day in history' card mixing flat infographic timeline design with a documentary photo. Intent: celebrate DACA recipients and frame the program positively on its anniversary (Biden-era DHS messaging).

genre_refs: Black Friday / Fashion Friday seasonal shopping theme
notable_text: Avoid scams. Buy from reputable manufacturers and stores; Research online sellers you aren't familiar with; ICE Homeland Security Investigations investigates commercial fraud. Report scams: 866-DHS-2-ICE
description: A designed vertical public-service infographic with a Black Friday / holiday-shopping theme ('Fashion Friday' per the tweet). The background is a dim photograph of a person reclining and holding a tablet/phone displaying a 'FASHION OUTLET' online store page with category tabs (TOPS, DRESSES, JEANS, JACKETS, COATS, LINGERIE, SWIM, SPORTS, ACCESSORIES), a model in a red top, and a red 'BLACK FRIDAY SALE' with a 'SHOP NOW' button. Overlaid on the dark left/lower area, a bold red headline reads 'Avoid scams.' followed by a white bulleted list (each bullet a red dot): 'Buy from reputable manufacturers and stores.' / 'Research online sellers you aren't familiar with.' / 'Be suspicious of websites that do not provide a toll-free contact number. Verify that the phone number works.' / 'Keep a record of your purchases and compare them to your statements. Report discrepancies immediately.' / 'Don't buy expensive items from third-party sellers.' At the bottom are the DHS seal, the white 'ICE' wordmark, and text 'ICE Homeland Security Investigations investigates commercial fraud. Report scams: 866-DHS-2-ICE'. Style is a consumer-tips safety infographic tied to a seasonal shopping moment. Messaging technique: helpful PSA framing that positions ICE/HSI as a consumer protector against fraud and counterfeits. Target audience: general public/online shoppers.

notable_text: If you self-deport now, you MAY be able to return as an immigrant or visitor someday — the LEGAL way; Learn more at: ICE.gov/self-deportation
description: A designed promotional graphic built over a soft-focus stock photograph of two people embracing in what appears to be an airport terminal (blurred travelers and ceiling lights in the background). One person, wearing a denim jacket and a face mask and carrying a shoulder bag, hugs another person dressed in a white sleeved top; the scene reads as an emotional reunion or farewell at a departure gate. Over the left/lower portion of the image, white sans-serif text reads: 'If you self-deport now, you MAY be able to return as an immigrant or visitor someday —' followed by a very large serif headline 'the LEGAL way' where the word 'LEGAL' is rendered in bright green for emphasis. A dark blue band across the bottom carries the DHS seal and the large 'ICE' wordmark on the left, and on the right: 'Learn more at: ICE.gov/self-deportation'. The propaganda technique pairs a warm, hopeful human image (reunion, return) with the coercive 'self-deport now' message, using the color-highlighted word 'LEGAL' to frame voluntary departure as the path to legitimate future return. Target audience: undocumented immigrants, encouraging voluntary departure via the CBP Home / self-deportation program.

notable_text: TOP 10 TIPS2PROTECT; TIP 1; Start an open, two-way conversation with your child.; know2protect — Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation
description: A square (1080x1080) designed social-media tip card from DHS's 'Know2Protect' online child-safety campaign. The background is a flat blue scene split into a lighter sky band across the top and a darker blue lower field. The top band is decorated with cheerful summer paper-cutout emoji-style icons: white clouds, a yellow sun, a camera, a red-and-white life preserver/ring buoy, a green popsicle, blue sunglasses, a slice of watermelon, and small orange butterflies. A yellow rounded banner near the top center holds bold black text 'TOP 10 TIPS2PROTECT'. On the left, large white outlined text reads 'TIP 1'. The center features two overlapping illustrated speech bubbles (one orange-outlined, one magenta-outlined) each containing an ellipsis of three dots, symbolizing conversation. Bold white centered text below reads 'Start an open, two-way conversation with your child.' At the bottom center are the white U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal and the 'know2protect' logo (a speech-bubble-with-dots mark) with the tagline 'Together We Can Stop Online Child Exploitation'. Style: clean, bright, parent-facing public-service-announcement infographic. Intent: educate parents/trusted adults on preventing online child exploitation; this is the first of a numbered tip series, paired with an interactive quiz in the tweet.

notable_text: Secretary Mayorkas Outlines National Priorities for Critical Infrastructure Security and Resilience for Next Two Years; PRESS RELEASE
description: A horizontal solid-blue announcement card in DHS official branding. The background is a flat medium-blue. Centered-left in white serif type reads 'Secretary Mayorkas Outlines National Priorities for Critical Infrastructure Security and Resilience for Next Two Years.' To the right is the white line-art version of the official DHS (U.S. Department of Homeland Security) circular seal with eagle. A thin red horizontal rule runs across the lower-middle of the image, beneath which large white/grey bold sans-serif text reads 'PRESS RELEASE.' This is a standardized DHS press-release title-card template used to accompany policy announcements on social media; it is a designed graphic but a plain bureaucratic announcement card rather than a meme or persuasion/propaganda image. Target audience is press and the general public.

notable_text: Personal Safety & Security; YOUR SAFETY OUR PRIORITY; #YourSafetyOurPriority; DHS.gov/FPS; @FPSDHS
description: A designed Federal Protective Service (FPS/DHS) title card / publication-promo graphic in square format on a deep navy-blue background. The upper portion presents the DHS seal inside a tilted white parallelogram badge at left, with white text to the right reading 'Personal Safety & Security' in a lighter weight above a very large bold white all-caps headline 'YOUR SAFETY OUR PRIORITY.' A white horizontal band separates the top from a bottom footer. The footer features a blue-and-green diagonal-stripe motif with the bold 'FPS' wordmark (and small 'FEDERAL PROTECTIVE SERVICE' lettering plus a small DHS badge) at left; to the right, white text gives campaign references with small globe/phone icons: 'Your Safety, Our Priority.' / 'DHS.gov/Publication/YourSafetyOurPriority' / '#YourSafetyOurPriority' and 'Federal Protective Service' / 'DHS.gov/FPS' / 'Social media @FPSDHS'. The style is corporate-government branded layout, clean and minimal, with no photography. The messaging promotes a new FPS series of personal safety/security awareness materials and drives readers to the publication. Target audience: federal-building occupants and the general public. Not immigration-themed.

notable_text: ICE local and state law enforcement PARTNER PROGRAMS; Jail Enforcement Model / Task Force Model / Warrant Service Officer; ICE.gov/287g
description: A designed informational poster on a dark navy textured (denim/canvas-like) background. The header reads 'ICE local and state law enforcement' in smaller white text above a large white serif title 'PARTNER PROGRAMS'. Down the left side are three stacked photo tiles tinted in red and blue duotone: the top tile (red) shows a jail/cell-block scene; the middle tile (blue) shows officers in tactical gear with 'POLICE' visible; the bottom tile (grayscale) shows a hand writing/signing a document. To the right of each tile is a bold white heading with description: 'Jail Enforcement Model — ID and process removable aliens you arrest with active or pending criminal charges.'; 'Task Force Model — Allows your officers to enforce limited immigration authority in certain situations.'; 'Warrant Service Officer — Serve and carry out administrative warrants against illegal aliens.' Bottom left reads 'ICE.gov/287g' and bottom right shows the DHS seal with 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement'. This is part of the 'FIRST 100 DAYS: Partnerships' series. The technique presents the 287(g) program in a clean, professional recruitment layout to encourage local/state agencies to partner with ICE. Target audience: police departments and sheriffs' offices.

notable_text: This is the highest rate of (worksite enforcement) arrest in HSI's history. — ROBERT HAMMER, ICE HSI Acting Director
description: A designed official quote card with a solid medium-blue background, matching ICE's leadership-quote series. At top center is a circular cropped headshot portrait of a smiling middle-aged man with a shaved head and gray goatee, wearing a dark suit and a light blue tie, standing before an American flag and a blue flag. Below, framed by large light-blue quotation marks, white serif text reads: 'This is the highest rate of (worksite enforcement) arrest in HSI's history.' The attribution beneath reads '— ROBERT HAMMER' with 'ICE HSI Acting Director' in italics. The bottom right shows the DHS seal and the large white 'ICE' wordmark. The technique uses an authoritative leadership portrait and a superlative claim ('highest rate ... in HSI's history') to tout record worksite/employer enforcement results. Target audience: general public, media, and employers, signaling aggressive worksite enforcement.

notable_text: CIVIL SOCIETY; Universities and Research Facilities; Governments; Advocacy Groups; The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages civil society to evaluate AI's societal impact, contribute to standard-setting, and advocate for safe, ethical AI in essential services.
description: A designed DHS explainer infographic from the same AI 'Roles and Responsibilities Framework' series, in landscape format on a blue gradient background. A bold white centered title reads 'CIVIL SOCIETY', with an italic white subtitle: 'Consists of universities, research institutions, and advocacy groups dedicated to public safety, ethical standards, and societal impact.' A thin horizontal connector line with a node descends to three white framed flat-illustration panels, each with a light-blue label tab: (1) 'Universities and Research Facilities' - a classical domed academic building with a graduation cap and books; (2) 'Governments' - a woman at a podium with a microphone addressing a diverse crowd of seated people; (3) 'Advocacy Groups' - a woman holding a megaphone beside a large smartphone, surrounded by user-profile bubbles, star ratings, and like/comment icons (social media advocacy). A blue footer bar holds white text: 'The new DHS Roles and Responsibilities Framework encourages civil society to evaluate AI's societal impact, contribute to standard-setting, and advocate for safe, ethical AI in essential services.' Arranged in a grid around the central title are twelve booking/mugshot-style photographs of men of varied ages and ethnicities, each in a small rounded-rectangle frame with a dark caption bar beneath naming an alleged or convicted crime. Target audience: the general public / supporters, intended for viral sharing.

notable_text: Number of law enforcement officers needed to arrest a criminal alien: UNCOOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS 8 to 10 / COOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS 2; ICE.gov/detainers
description: A designed comparison infographic in portrait format on a dark, gritty black asphalt/concrete textured background. A faint ghosted image of an open metal handcuff sits in the lower portion of the graphic. The headline at top in white sans-serif (bold emphasis) reads 'Number of law enforcement officers needed to arrest a criminal alien:'. Below are two side-by-side columns introduced by colored banner labels: a red banner reading 'UNCOOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS' on the left and a blue banner reading 'COOPERATIVE JURISDICTIONS' on the right, separated by a thin vertical divider. Under the red side, a very large white numeral '8 to 10' with supporting text: 'ICE needs more officers to help protect the public from unpredictable behavior by an at-large criminal attempting to evade justice.' Under the blue side, a large white numeral '2' with text: 'ICE can arrest a criminal alien with as few as two officers in a safe, controlled location, like a jail or court lockup area.' Bottom-left bears the DHS/ICE seal and 'U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement', and bottom-right the URL 'ICE.gov/detainers'. The propaganda technique is a stark numeric contrast (red/danger vs. blue/safe) arguing that sanctuary or non-cooperative jurisdictions force ICE to expend more manpower and create public risk, pressuring local governments to honor detainers. Target audience: the public and local officials, framing cooperation as the safe, efficient, taxpayer-friendly choice.

notable_text: CNN's promotion of an 'ICE spotting' app is reckless and irresponsible...My officers and agents are already facing a 500% increase in assaults...CNN is willfully endangering the lives of officers...Is this simply reckless 'journalism' or overt activism?
description: A designed quote-card built around a black-and-white screenshot of a CNN remote video interview, framed by a yellow border. The top half shows a split-screen: on the left a woman seated at a laptop, identified by a red-and-white chyron 'CNN TECH REPORTER / Clare Duffy' with the red 'CNN' logo in the upper corner; on the right a bespectacled man with a beard speaking from a home kitchen (the interview subject). The bottom half is a black panel with an ICE statement in white text introduced by a large yellow quotation mark: 'CNN's promotion of an ‘ICE spotting’ app is reckless and irresponsible. Advertising an app that basically paints a target on federal law enforcement officers’ backs is sickening. My officers and agents are already facing a 500% increase in assaults, and going on live television to announce an app that lets anyone zero in on their locations is like inviting violence against them with a national megaphone. CNN is willfully endangering the lives of officers who put their lives on the line every day and enabling dangerous criminal aliens to evade U.S. law.' A closing yellow-text rhetorical question reads 'Is this simply reckless ‘journalism’ or overt activism?”' The technique is a media-criticism/attack graphic that reframes a news report as a threat to officers, using a statistic ('500% increase in assaults') and loaded language to delegitimize CNN. Target audience: supporters and the press, intended to rally outrage against media coverage of ICE-tracking tools.

description: A designed DHS graphic styled as a 'headline wall' / press-clipping collage on a dark navy-blue textured background that resembles faded newspaper print. A red banner across the top carries bold white all-caps text reading 'MAKING AMERICA SAFE AGAIN'. Below it, five torn-paper / ripped-newsprint cards are arranged in a staggered grid, each reproducing a mainstream news outlet logo above a quoted headline about Secretary Kristi Noem's visit to El Salvador's CECOT mega-prison. The cards read: (1) AXIOS - 'Noem warns immigrants during tour of El Salvador prison where deported Venezuelans held'; (2) AP - 'Homeland Security Secretary Noem visits the El Salvador prison where deported Venezuelans are held'; (3) Washington Examiner - 'Noem sends warning to illegal immigrants from El Salvador prison with Tren de Aragua members'; (4) FOX NEWS - 'Noem comes face-to-face with alleged Tren de Aragua members during tour of notorious El Salvador prison'; (5) The Washington Post - 'Noem threatens to send more immigrants to El Salvador prison'. The official circular DHS seal is centered at the bottom. The propaganda technique is the 'media validation' or earned-media montage: by stacking logos of ideologically varied outlets (left-leaning WaPo/Axios alongside Fox), the graphic frames the deportation/CECOT messaging as universally newsworthy and credible, and projects toughness and deterrence. Target audience: the general public and the administration's base, signaling that the crackdown is making national headlines.
